topics.title: Simple 2D Models Using Drawing and Editing Tools (Foundation Bolts)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simple 2D Models Using Drawing and Editing Tools (Foundation Bolts)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Foundation Bolt** (also known as an **Anchor Bolt** or **Hold-down Bolt**) is a mechanical fastener, typically made of high-strength structural steel, that is embedded in a concrete foundation and used to anchor structural members, machinery, columns, towers, or equipment bases firmly to the foundation. In the context of the KTU **PCPEL307 - Computer Aided Design Lab**, a foundation bolt refers to a 2D orthographic CAD model drawn using standard drafting tools (LINE, CIRCLE, ARC, RECTANGLE) and editing tools (OFFSET, TRIM, MIRROR, FILLET) to represent the bolt's geometry with engineering accuracy, proper dimensioning, and sectional views.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPEL307):** "Simple 2D Models Using Drawing and Editing Tools" refers to the creation of standard machine/structural elements (foundation bolts, nuts, washers, cotters, pins) using 2D drafting commands. These are foundation-level exercises that introduce students to engineering graphics in a CAD environment (typically **AutoCAD 2018 or later**).

## 1.2 Types of Foundation Bolts Covered in KTU Labs

The following standard types of foundation bolts are commonly modeled in the CAD lab:

1. **Bent Anchor Bolt (L-Bolt):** A straight shaft with a 90° bent hook at the embedded end.
2. **J-Bolt:** A straight shaft with a J-shaped hook at the embedded end.
3. **Headed Bolt (T-Bolt):** A straight shaft with a forged/pressed head at one end.
4. **Eye Bolt:** A round shank with a forged eye (loop) at the embedded end.
5. **Plate-Embedded Bolt:** A straight bolt welded to a base plate for better load distribution.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Root of a Tree":** Think of a foundation bolt as the **root of a large tree**. The tree (your machine, column, or tower) stands tall above the ground, but it cannot stay upright without a strong root system gripping the soil. Similarly, the bolt's embedded portion is hidden inside the concrete (the "soil"), while its exposed threaded portion holds the structure (the "trunk") in place. **Without proper CAD modeling, you cannot manufacture the root correctly — and the tree will fall!**

## 1.4 Standard Components of a Foundation Bolt Assembly

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Shank** | The cylindrical main body of the bolt |
| **Threaded End** | Exposed portion for fastening the nut |
| **Embedded End** | Portion inside concrete (hook / head / plate) |
| **Nut** | Hexagonal element to clamp the structure |
| **Washer** | Flat disc to distribute load & prevent damage |
| **Base Plate** (optional) | Increases pull-out resistance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (IS 2062 / IS 1367):**
> - Standard Bolt Diameters: **M10, M12, M16, M20, M24, M30, M36**
> - Standard Thread Pitch (M20 example): **2.5 mm**
> - Typical Embedment Length: **15 × Bolt Diameter (15d minimum)**
> - Hook Length (for L/J bolts): **4 × Bolt Diameter to 7 × Bolt Diameter**
> - Standard Nut Height: **0.8 × d**
> - Standard Washer Outer Diameter: **2 × d to 2.5 × d**

## 1.5 Relevance to the KTU 2024 Lab Examination

In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab evaluation, students are expected to:
- **Model** foundation bolts with correct geometric proportions
- **Dimension** them as per engineering drawing standards (IS 696 / SP 46)
- **Apply sectioning** where internal features must be shown
- **Use the Model Space + Layout (Paper Space)** workflow with proper viewports
- **Plot/Print** the drawing to scale (typically **1:1** for full-size or **1:2** for half-size)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Engineering Theory of Foundation Bolts

A foundation bolt must resist three primary load types:

$$\sum F_{axial} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{bolt} \geq \frac{P_{tension}}{A_{net}}$$

$$\tau_{shear} = \frac{V}{A_{shank}} \leq \tau_{allowable}$$

$$M_{resistance} = F_{bolt} \times L_{embed}$$

Where:
- $P_{tension}$ = Applied tensile (pull-out) load
- $V$ = Applied shear (sliding) load
- $A_{net}$ = Net cross-sectional area at thread root
- $A_{shank}$ = Nominal shank cross-sectional area
- $L_{embed}$ = Effective embedment length inside concrete
- $F_{bolt}$ = Bolt pretension force

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in CAD:** Although the CAD lab does not perform FEA, the student must understand that the **hook geometry (L or J)** or **head/eye diameter** directly provides mechanical pull-out resistance. The **CAD geometry must match the design intent** — the hook bend radius, the leg length, and the head diameter all have structural meaning.

## 2.2 AutoCAD Drawing Tools Used in This Experiment

| Tool/Command | Icon | Function in Foundation Bolt Drafting |
|--------------|------|--------------------------------------|
| **LINE** | `/` | Draws straight lines (shank, hook, projections) |
| **CIRCLE** | `○` | Draws bolt head, nut circle, eye loop |
| **ARC** | `⌒` | Draws hook bends, fillet curves, eye inner curve |
| **RECTANGLE** | `▭` | Draws washer (rectangular view) |
| **POLYLINE** | `∿` | Draws continuous bolt outline (single entity) |
| **PLINE** | `∿` | Same as polyline — used for center lines |
| **ELLIPSE** | `⬭` | Optional — for tapered sections |
| **HATCH** | `▦` | Section lining (45° parallel lines, IS conventions) |
| **BHATCH** | `▦` | Boundary hatch (used inside nut/washer section) |
| **MTEXT / TEXT** | `A` | Labels and notes on drawing |
| **DIMLINEAR / DIMRADIAL / DIMANGULAR** | `↔` | Dimensions for length, radius, angle |

## 2.3 AutoCAD Editing Tools Used in This Experiment

| Tool/Command | Function in Foundation Bolt Drafting |
|--------------|--------------------------------------|
| **OFFSET** | Creates parallel lines at bolt pitch distance |
| **TRIM** | Removes unwanted portions of lines/arcs |
| **EXTEND** | Extends lines to meet boundaries |
| **MIRROR** | Creates symmetric L-bolt from half-model |
| **COPY** | Duplicates nut/washer for multiple bolts |
| **MOVE** | Repositions elements |
| **ROTATE** | Rotates hook/head to required angle |
| **FILLET** | Adds rounded corner (inside bend radius) |
| **CHAMFER** | Adds 45° beveled edge (optional) |
| **SCALE** | Enlarges/reduces bolt to required size |
| **ARRAY (Rectangular/Polar)** | Creates bolt circle patterns |
| **STRETCH** | Adjusts length dynamically |
| **LENGTHEN** | Modifies total length |
| **BREAK** | Splits lines at points |
| **JOIN** | Merges collinear segments into one |
| **EXPLODE** | Decomposes blocks/polylines |

## 2.4 KTU AutoCAD Reference Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Step | Action | AutoCAD Command Sequence (Typed) |
|------|--------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | Set drawing units to **millimeters** | `UNITS → Decimal → Millimeters` |
| 2 | Set drawing limits | `LIMITS → 0,0 → 297,210 (A4 landscape)` |
| 3 | Set layer for outline | `LAYER → New → "OUTLINE" → Color: White/Black` |
| 4 | Set layer for center line | `LAYER → New → "CENTER" → Linetype: CENTER` |
| 5 | Set layer for dimensions | `LAYER → New → "DIM" → Color: Green` |
| 6 | Set layer for hatching | `LAYER → New → "HATCH" → Color: Cyan` |
| 7 | Draw vertical center line | `LINE → 100,50 → 100,150` |
| 8 | Draw bolt shank | `LINE → 100,80 → 100,140` |
| 9 | Draw hook arc | `ARC → Center,Start,End` |
| 10 | Apply dimensioning style | `DIMSTYLE → ISO-25` |
| 11 | Apply hatch | `HATCH → ANSI31 → 45° → 1 mm spacing` |
| 12 | Save drawing | `CTRL+S` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The examiner will specifically check for: **(1) proper layer usage**, **(2) correct linestyle for center lines (CENTER, not continuous)**, **(3) correct hatch pattern (ANSI31 at 45°)**, **(4) proper dimensioning with arrows (closed/filled)**, and **(5) title block with student name, register number, scale, and date.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Foundation bolts modeled in CAD are used in:

- **Civil Engineering:** Anchoring steel columns to RCC foundations in industrial buildings
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Mounting heavy machinery (turbines, generators, compressors) to concrete plinths
- **Electrical Engineering:** Securing transmission towers, transformer bases, and switchgear
- **Structural Engineering:** Connecting base plates of portal frames, trusses, and gantry girders

> [!NOTE]
> The 2D CAD model serves as the **primary engineering deliverable** for fabrication. Tolerances (±0.5 mm for general, ±0.1 mm for precision) and surface finish notations (e.g., Ra 3.2) are added to this drawing before it is sent to the manufacturing floor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Statement (KTU Standard Assignment)

> **Draw the 2D views (Front View + Top View + Side View) of a Bent Foundation Bolt (L-Bolt) of size **M20 × 200 mm** with a hook of length 60 mm, head thickness 12 mm, and a hexagonal nut. Use standard proportions. Dimension the views as per IS 696.**

### 3.2 Given Data

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|-------|------|
| Bolt nominal diameter | $d$ | **20** | mm |
| Total bolt length | $L$ | **200** | mm |
| Hook leg length | $l_h$ | **60** | mm |
| Head/thread length | $l_t$ | **30** | mm |
| Thread length | $l_{thread}$ | **30** | mm |
| Nut height | $h_n$ | **16** | mm |
| Washer outer diameter | $D_w$ | **40** | mm |
| Washer thickness | $t_w$ | **3** | mm |
| Hook bend radius | $R$ | **10** | mm |
| Hex nut across-flats | $A_F$ | **30** | mm |

### 3.3 Derived Dimensions (Standard Proportions)

The standard nut across-flats distance is computed as:

$$A_F = 1.5 \times d = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ mm}$$

The standard nut height is computed as:

$$h_n = 0.8 \times d = 0.8 \times 20 = 16 \text{ mm}$$

The standard washer outer diameter is computed as:

$$D_w = 2.0 \times d = 2.0 \times 20 = 40 \text{ mm}$$

The minimum embedment length is computed as:

$$L_{embed,min} = 15 \times d = 15 \times 20 = 300 \text{ mm (but our bolt total is 200 mm — L-bolt is partial embedment)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For a 200 mm L-bolt, the embedment is typically the lower **120 mm** (hook + shank portion), and **80 mm** is the exposed portion above the concrete surface. Always show the **foundation line (hatched concrete boundary)** in the front view to clearly distinguish embedded vs. exposed regions.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step AutoCAD Construction Procedure

#### **STEP 1 — Setup and Grid**

```
Command: LIMITS
Reset Model space limits:
Specify lower left corner or [ON/OFF] <0.0000,0.0000>: 0,0
Specify upper right corner <420.0000,297.0000>: 420,297
Command: ZOOM
Specify corner of window, enter a scale factor (nX or nXP), or
[All/Center/Dynamic/Extents/Previous/Scale/Window] <real time>: A
```

**Action:** Set units to **Decimal, Millimeters**, precision **0.00**, grid spacing **10 mm**, snap spacing **5 mm**.

#### **STEP 2 — Layer Creation**

```
Command: LAYER
→ New layer: OUTLINE (color 7 = White/Black, Linetype Continuous, Lineweight 0.50 mm)
→ New layer: CENTER  (color 1 = Red,    Linetype CENTER, Lineweight 0.25 mm)
→ New layer: HIDDEN  (color 5 = Blue,   Linetype HIDDEN, Lineweight 0.25 mm)
→ New layer: DIM     (color 3 = Green,  Linetype Continuous, Lineweight 0.25 mm)
→ New layer: HATCH   (color 4 = Cyan,   Linetype Continuous, Lineweight 0.25 mm)
→ New layer: TEXT    (color 6 = Magenta,Linetype Continuous, Lineweight 0.25 mm)
→ Set CURRENT layer: OUTLINE
```

#### **STEP 3 — Draw the Front View Construction Grid**

```
Command: LINE
Specify first point: 100,50
Specify next point: 100,200
Specify next point: (Press ENTER to end)

Command: LINE
Specify first point: 60,80
Specify next point: 180,80

Command: LINE
Specify first point: 60,170
Specify next point: 180,170
```

**Logic:** We construct a vertical **center line** at x = 100 from y = 50 to y = 200, and **two horizontal reference lines** at y = 80 (foundation line top) and y = 170 (top of bolt exposed portion).

#### **STEP 4 — Draw the Embedded Hook (L-Bolt Bend)**

```
Set layer: OUTLINE
Command: LINE
Specify first point: 100,170     (top of embedded portion — hook tip)
Specify next point: 100,80      (straight vertical shank down to bend start)

Command: ARC
Specify start point of arc or [Center]: 100,80
Specify second point of arc or [Center/End]: C
Specify center point of arc: 100,70
Specify end point of arc: 90,70
```

**Logic:** The arc is a **90° arc with radius 10 mm (R = 0.5d)** centered at (100, 70). It connects the bottom of the vertical shank to the start of the horizontal hook leg.

```
Command: LINE
Specify first point: 90,70
Specify next point: 40,70       (horizontal hook leg of length 60 mm)
```

#### **STEP 5 — Draw the Exposed Thread and Head**

```
Command: LINE
Specify first point: 100,170
Specify next point: 100,200     (extends shank above the bend zone to expose thread)

Command: OFFSET
Specify offset distance or [Through] <Through>: 10
Select object to offset: (select vertical shank at x=100)
Specify point on side to offset: (click right side)
```

**Logic:** The OFFSET creates the **right-side shank outline** at x = 110, giving the bolt a visible **20 mm diameter** (10 mm on each side of centerline).

```
Command: LINE
Specify first point: 100,200
Specify next point: 110,200     (top of bolt)

Command: LINE
Specify first point: 110,170
Specify next point: 110,200
```

#### **STEP 6 — Draw the Hex Nut on Top**

A hexagonal nut is shown in the **top view** as a hexagon, and in the **front view** as a rectangle of width $A_F$ = 30 mm and height $h_n$ = 16 mm.

```
Command: POLYGON
Enter number of sides <4>: 6
Specify center of polygon: 250,185
Specify an option [Inscribed in circle/Circumscribed about circle] <I>: C
Specify radius of circle: 15
```

**Logic:** The hexagon is drawn as a **circumscribed polygon** (sides touch the circle of radius 15 mm = half the across-flats distance).

In the front view, the nut appears as a **rectangle**:

```
Command: LINE
Specify first point: 235,177
Specify next point: 265,177
Specify next point: 265,193
Specify next point: 235,193
Specify next point: 235,177
Command: CLOSE (or press C)
```

#### **STEP 7 — Draw the Washer**

```
Command: CIRCLE
Specify center point for circle: 250,140
Specify radius of circle: 20      (washer outer radius = D_w/2 = 20)
Command: CIRCLE
Specify center point for circle: 250,140
Specify radius of circle: 10      (washer inner radius = d/2 = 10)
```

#### **STEP 8 — Apply Hatching to the Concrete Foundation**

```
Set layer: HATCH
Command: HATCH
→ Pattern: ANSI31
→ Angle: 45°
→ Spacing: 5 mm (scale)
→ Pick internal point: (click inside the area below the foundation line, y < 80)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Hatching is applied **only to the solid material being cut by the section plane**. In a foundation bolt drawing, the **concrete** is sectioned (using the **solid concrete** pattern) when the section plane cuts through the foundation. The bolt itself is **NOT hatched** because the section plane passes through the bolt — it is shown in **external view**.

#### **STEP 9 — Apply Dimensions**

```
Set layer: DIM
Command: DIMLINEAR
Specify first extension line origin: 40,70
Specify second extension line origin: 100,70
Specify dimension line location: 70,55
Dimension text <60>: 60 (HOOK LEG LENGTH)

Command: DIMLINEAR
Specify first extension line origin: 90,70
Specify second extension line origin: 90,80
Specify dimension line location: 75,75
Dimension text <10>: R10

Command: DIMLINEAR
Specify first extension line origin: 100,80
Specify second extension line origin: 100,200
Specify dimension line location: 130,140
Dimension text <120>: 120 (EMBEDMENT LENGTH)

Command: DIMLINEAR
Specify first extension line origin: 100,200
Specify second extension line origin: 110,200
Specify dimension line location: 120,210
Dimension text <10>: ⌀20

Command: DIMANGULAR
Select arc, circle, or line: (select the 90° arc)
Select second line: (select horizontal hook leg)
Specify dimension arc line location: (click above the arc)
Dimension text <90>: 90°
```

#### **STEP 10 — Add Center Lines, Title Block, and Notes**

```
Set layer: CENTER
Command: LINE
Specify first point: 250,120
Specify next point: 250,160
Specify next point: (Press ENTER to end)
Command: LTSCALE
New linetype scale factor <1.0000>: 5
```

**Title Block Contents (mandatory):**

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Drawing Title | FOUNDATION BOLT (L-TYPE) |
| Name | [Student Name] |
| Register No. | [KTU Reg. No.] |
| Scale | 1 : 1 |
| Sheet Size | A4 (210 × 297) |
| Date | [Submission Date] |
| Checked By | [Faculty Name] |
| Drawing No. | PCPEL307 / EXP-02 / 01 |

#### **STEP 11 — Mirror, Array, or Copy (For Bolt Pattern Views)**

If the question requires **multiple bolts** in a base plate pattern:

```
Command: ARRAY
→ Select objects: (choose the bolt outline)
→ Rectangular Array
→ Rows: 2
→ Columns: 3
→ Row spacing: 80
→ Column spacing: 100
```

#### **STEP 12 — Plot / Publish to PDF**

```
Command: PLOT
→ Printer: DWG To PDF.pc3
→ Paper size: A4 (210 × 297 mm)
→ Plot area: Layout
→ Scale: 1:1 (Fit to paper)
→ Plot offset: Centered
→ Plot with lineweights: Yes
→ Plot style table: monochrome.ctb
```

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Command → Purpose → Expected Output

| # | Command | Purpose in This Experiment | Expected Visual Output |
|---|---------|----------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | `UNITS` | Set drawing units | Decimal, mm |
| 2 | `LIMITS` | Define drawing boundary | A4 rectangle |
| 3 | `LAYER` | Organize drawing entities | 6 layers (outline, center, hidden, dim, hatch, text) |
| 4 | `LINE` | Draw straight edges | Shank + hook outline |
| 5 | `ARC` | Draw curved hook bend | 90° arc, R10 |
| 6 | `OFFSET` | Create parallel lines | Shank width, distance lines |
| 7 | `TRIM` | Remove excess lines | Clean intersections |
| 8 | `MIRROR` | Create symmetry | Left/right halves |
| 9 | `FILLET` | Round internal corner | Smooth bend transition |
| 10 | `COPY` | Replicate features | Multiple nuts/washers |
| 11 | `HATCH` | Show section | Concrete cross-section |
| 12 | `DIMLINEAR` | Add linear dimensions | Length, diameter |
| 13 | `DIMANGULAR` | Add angular dimensions | Hook angle (90°) |
| 14 | `MTEXT` | Add labels | "FOUNDATION LINE", "M20" |
| 15 | `PLOT` | Print the drawing | PDF / Hard copy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the CAD Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: KTU Experiment 2 Assignment] --> B[Open AutoCAD: New Drawing]
    B --> C[Setup: UNITS, LIMITS, GRID, SNAP]
    C --> D[Create Layers: OUTLINE, CENTER, HIDDEN, DIM, HATCH, TEXT]
    D --> E[Step 1: Draw Center Lines on OUTLINE Layer]
    E --> F[Step 2: Draw Shank using LINE Command]
    F --> G[Step 3: Draw Hook Bend using ARC Command - R10]
    G --> H[Step 4: OFFSET Shank for Diameter]
    H --> I[Step 5: TRIM Excess Lines]
    I --> J[Step 6: Draw Hexagonal Nut using POLYGON Command]
    J --> K[Step 7: Draw Washer using CIRCLE Command]
    K --> L[Step 8: Apply HATCH for Concrete Section]
    L --> M[Step 9: Add Dimensions using DIMLINEAR and DIMANGULAR]
    M --> N[Step 10: Add Title Block and Notes using MTEXT]
    N --> O{Quality Check: Are All Views Complete?}
    O -->|No| P[Re-edit using MOVE, COPY, ROTATE, MIRROR]
    P --> O
    O -->|Yes| Q[Step 11: Save DWG File]
    Q --> R[Step 12: Plot to PDF / Print A4 Sheet]
    R --> S[End: Submit Drawing to Lab Faculty]
```

## 4.2 Process Architecture of Foundation Bolt Drafting

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Specification Analysis] --> B[Geometry Decomposition]
    B --> C[2D View Generation]
    C --> D[Dimensioning Layer]
    C --> E[Sectioning Layer]
    C --> F[Annotation Layer]
    D --> G[Final Drawing]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Plot Output]
```

**Node Descriptions (Block-Level Architecture):**

- **nodeA — Specification Analysis:** Read KTU problem statement, identify bolt type (L, J, Headed), extract dimensions (M20 × 200), and list standard proportions.
- **nodeB — Geometry Decomposition:** Break down bolt into parametric primitives — cylindrical shank, bent hook arc, hexagonal nut, circular washer, foundation line.
- **nodeC — 2D View Generation:** Create Front View, Top View, and Side View in Model Space.
- **nodeD — Dimensioning Layer:** Apply linear, angular, and radial dimensions as per IS 696.
- **nodeE — Sectioning Layer:** Apply ANSI31 hatch pattern for concrete foundation only.
- **nodeF — Annotation Layer:** Add text labels (M20, FOUNDATION LINE), title block, scale, and notes.
- **nodeG — Final Drawing:** Combine all layers with proper line weights, linetypes, and colors.
- **nodeH — Plot Output:** Publish as PDF or print on A4 sheet with proper scale.

## 4.3 Visual Schematic of the Final L-Bolt Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EMBED[EMBEDDED PORTION BELOW FOUNDATION]
        H1[HOOK LEG - 60 mm Horizontal] --> H2[HOOK BEND - R10 Arc 90 deg]
        H2 --> H3[VERTICAL SHANK - 120 mm]
    end
    subgraph EXPOSE[EXPOSED PORTION ABOVE FOUNDATION]
        H3 --> H4[FLANGE LINE - Foundation Surface]
        H4 --> H5[WASHER - OD 40 mm, t 3 mm]
        H5 --> H6[NUT HEXAGON - AF 30 mm, h 16 mm]
        H6 --> H7[THREADED END - 30 mm]
    end
    H7 --> H8[STRUCTURE BASE PLATE]
    subgraph TOPVIEW[TOP VIEW HEX NUT PROJECTION]
        T1[Hexagon AF 30 mm] --> T2[Inscribed Circle R 13 mm]
        T2 --> T3[Bolt Hole d 20 mm]
    end
```

**Interpretation of the Schematic:**
- The **EMBED block** represents the portion of the L-bolt hidden inside the concrete foundation.
- The **EXPOSE block** represents the visible portion above the foundation line, including the washer and nut.
- The **TOPVIEW block** shows the hexagonal nut as seen from above, with the bolt's circular cross-section at the center.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most students forget to add the **center line** through the hexagon and the **circumscribed circle** around the hexagon. These are mandatory per IS 696 and deduct marks if missing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any six AutoCAD editing tools used in drawing a foundation bolt. State the function of each.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 mark each):**

1. **OFFSET:** Creates parallel lines at specified distance (used for shank outline).
2. **TRIM:** Removes unwanted portions of lines/arcs at cutting edges.
3. **MIRROR:** Creates mirror image across an axis (used for symmetric bolts).
4. **COPY:** Duplicates selected entities at specified base point and displacement.
5. **FILLET:** Creates rounded internal corners (used for hook bend radius).
6. **CHAMFER:** Creates beveled edges (45° typical).
7. **ARRAY:** Creates multiple copies in rectangular or polar pattern.
8. **MOVE:** Repositions selected entities.
9. **ROTATE:** Rotates entities about a base point.
10. **EXTEND:** Extends a line/arc to meet another entity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Expectation:** A minimum of **6 tools** with **one-line functions** is required for full 3 marks.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What are the standard proportions for (a) hex nut across-flats, (b) nut height, and (c) washer outer diameter, in terms of the bolt diameter 'd'?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Formula | Example (M20, d = 20 mm) |
|-----------|---------|--------------------------|
| Hex nut across-flats ($A_F$) | $A_F = 1.5 \times d$ | $A_F = 30$ mm |
| Hex nut height ($h_n$) | $h_n = 0.8 \times d$ | $h_n = 16$ mm |
| Washer outer diameter ($D_w$) | $D_w = 2.0 \times d$ to $2.5 \times d$ | $D_w = 40$ to $50$ mm |

(1 mark each for the three formulas + correct substitution + units)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ **Missing center line (linetype CENTER)** — loses 2 marks
> - ❌ **Hatching the bolt instead of the concrete** — loses 2 marks
> - ❌ **Using continuous linetype for hidden features** — loses 1 mark
> - ❌ **Dimensioning in wrong layer (DIM layer not used)** — loses 1 mark
> - ❌ **Forgetting title block fields (Name, Reg. No., Scale, Date)** — loses 1 mark
> - ❌ **Not showing all three views (Front, Top, Side)** — loses 3 marks
> - ❌ **No section line on foundation** — loses 2 marks

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q: Draw the Front View, Top View, and Side View of a Foundation Bolt (L-Type) of size M24 × 300 mm, with a hook of length 80 mm, embedment length 200 mm, and a hexagonal nut. Apply standard proportions. Dimension the views and add a title block.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Front View + Side View**

#### **Model Solution Procedure:**

**Step 1: Read Specifications**
- Bolt: M24, total length 300 mm, hook 80 mm, embedment 200 mm
- Exposed length = 300 − 200 = 100 mm

**Step 2: Compute Standard Proportions**

$$A_F = 1.5 \times 24 = 36 \text{ mm}$$

$$h_n = 0.8 \times 24 = 19.2 \text{ mm} \approx 19 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_w = 2.0 \times 24 = 48 \text{ mm}$$

$$R_{hook} = 0.5 \times d = 12 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Construction Sequence (AutoCAD Commands)**

```
1. Set UNITS to mm
2. Set LIMITS to 420 × 297 (A3 landscape)
3. Create layers: OUTLINE, CENTER, DIM, HATCH, TEXT
4. Draw vertical center line at x=150 from y=50 to y=350
5. Draw foundation line at y=250 (top of concrete)
6. Draw L-bolt:
   a. LINE: (150, 50) → (150, 238)         [vertical shank, 200 mm embedment]
   b. ARC: Center (150, 238), R12, sweep 90° to (138, 250)
   c. LINE: (138, 250) → (58, 250)         [horizontal hook leg, 80 mm]
   d. LINE: (150, 250) → (150, 350)        [exposed shank, 100 mm]
   e. OFFSET: 12 mm on each side of shank  [shank diameter 24 mm]
7. Draw hex nut: POLYGON 6-side at top, AF=36 mm
8. Draw washer: CIRCLE OD=48, ID=24
9. Hatch concrete below y=250 with ANSI31, 45°, 5 mm
10. Apply dimensions:
    a. DIMLINEAR: hook length 80 mm
    b. DIMLINEAR: embedment 200 mm
    c. DIMLINEAR: exposed 100 mm
    d. DIMLINEAR: bolt diameter ⌀24
    e. DIMANGULAR: hook angle 90°
    f. DIMRADIAL: hook radius R12
11. Add title block
```

**Mark Distribution (Front + Side View):**

| Element | Marks |
|---------|-------|
| Correct view layout & proportions | 2 |
| L-bolt hook geometry (90° arc + leg) | 2 |
| Hex nut projection | 1 |
| Washer projection | 1 |
| Section line on foundation | 1 |
| **Total for Part (a)** | **7** |

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Top View + Dimensions + Title Block**

#### **Model Solution Procedure:**

**Step 1: Top View Geometry**
- The top view shows the **hexagonal nut** (regular hexagon, AF = 36 mm) and the **circular bolt cross-section** (⌀24 mm) at its center.
- A **center line** is drawn through the hexagon as a horizontal and vertical line.
- A **circumscribed circle** of radius 18 mm (= AF/2) is drawn around the hexagon.

**Step 2: Dimensioning (Top View)**
- Across-flats dimension: 36 mm
- Bolt diameter: ⌀24 mm
- Inscribed circle radius (optional): R 15.6 mm (= AF × √3/3)

**Step 3: Title Block**
- All 8 fields filled correctly (Drawing Title, Name, Reg. No., Scale, Sheet Size, Date, Checked By, Drawing No.)

**Mark Distribution (Top View + Title Block):**

| Element | Marks |
|---------|-------|
| Correct hexagon projection | 2 |
| Circumscribed circle + center line | 1 |
| Dimensions applied correctly | 2 |
| Title block with all fields | 2 |
| **Total for Part (b)** | **7** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q: Draw the Front View and Top View of a Headed Foundation Bolt of size M30 × 350 mm with a forged head of diameter 50 mm and thickness 18 mm, used to anchor a steel column base plate. Apply standard proportions and show a sectional view through the foundation.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Headed Bolt Geometry + Front View Section**

#### **Model Solution Procedure:**

**Step 1: Compute Standard Proportions for M30**

$$d = 30 \text{ mm}, \quad A_F = 1.5 \times 30 = 45 \text{ mm}, \quad h_n = 0.8 \times 30 = 24 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_w = 2.0 \times 30 = 60 \text{ mm}, \quad D_{head} = 1.6 \times d = 48 \text{ mm} \text{ (approx. 50 mm as given)}$$

$$t_{head} = 0.6 \times d = 18 \text{ mm} \text{ ✓ (matches given value)}$$

**Step 2: Construction Sequence**

```
1. Set up A2 landscape (594 × 420 mm)
2. Layers: OUTLINE, CENTER, HIDDEN, HATCH, DIM, TEXT
3. Vertical center line: x=200, y=50 to y=450
4. Foundation line: y=350
5. Headed bolt:
   a. LINE: (200, 50) → (200, 350)         [shank in concrete, 300 mm]
   b. LINE: (175, 350) → (175, 368)        [head left side, t=18 mm]
   c. ARC:   R=25, center (200, 350), sweep 90° to (175, 350)  [head curve]
   d. LINE: (175, 368) → (225, 368)        [head top]
   e. ARC:   R=25, center (200, 350), sweep 90° to (225, 368)
   f. LINE: (225, 368) → (225, 350)        [head right side]
   g. OFFSET: 15 mm on each side of shank  [shank diameter 30 mm]
6. Section line: A-A through foundation
7. HATCH concrete (ANSI31, 45°)
8. Hex nut on top
9. Washer below nut
10. Dimensions + Title Block
```

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Top View + Sectional View + Full Dimensioning**

**Step 1: Top View (Hex Nut)**
- Hexagon, AF = 45 mm, center at (200, 400)
- Bolt hole circle ⌀30 mm at center
- Center lines (horizontal + vertical) through hexagon

**Step 2: Sectional View A-A**
- Section plane vertical through bolt centerline
- Show bolt NOT hatched (external view of bolt)
- Show concrete HATCHED (ANSI31, 45°)
- Hidden line for embedded portion: HIDDEN linetype

**Step 3: Dimensioning**
- Bolt diameter: ⌀30
- Head diameter: ⌀50
- Head thickness: 18
- Hex nut AF: 45
- Embedment length: 300
- Exposed length: 50
- Total length: 350

**Mark Distribution:**

| Element | Marks |
|---------|-------|
| Headed bolt proportions correct | 2 |
| Head geometry (curved transition) | 1 |
| Section A-A correctly drawn | 2 |
| Hatching convention followed | 1 |
| Full dimensioning | 1 |
| **Total for Part (b)** | **7** |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module Experiment 2**

### **A. Core Concept Anchors**

- ✅ A **Foundation Bolt** is a structural fastener embedded in concrete to anchor columns/machinery.
- ✅ The **L-Bolt**, **J-Bolt**, **Headed Bolt**, and **Eye Bolt** are the four main types covered in KTU labs.
- ✅ Standard dimensions are based on **bolt diameter d** (M20, M24, M30, etc.).

### **B. Standard Proportions (Memorize These!)**

- ✅ Nut across-flats: $A_F = 1.5d$
- ✅ Nut height: $h_n = 0.8d$
- ✅ Washer outer diameter: $D_w = 2.0d$ to $2.5d$
- ✅ Hook bend radius: $R = 0.5d$
- ✅ Head diameter (Headed Bolt): $D_{head} = 1.5d$ to $1.7d$
- ✅ Head thickness: $t_{head} = 0.6d$
- ✅ Minimum embedment: $L_{embed} = 15d$

### **C. AutoCAD Tools — Quick Reference**

- ✅ **Drawing Tools:** LINE, CIRCLE, ARC, RECTANGLE, POLYGON, PLINE, ELLIPSE, HATCH, MTEXT
- ✅ **Editing Tools:** OFFSET, TRIM, EXTEND, MIRROR, COPY, MOVE, ROTATE, FILLET, CHAMFER, SCALE, ARRAY, STRETCH, LENGTHEN, BREAK, JOIN, EXPLODE
- ✅ **Annotation Tools:** DIMLINEAR, DIMANGULAR, DIMRADIAL, DIMDIAMETER, LEADER, MTEXT

### **D. Drawing Standards (IS 696 / SP 46)**

- ✅ All dimensions in **millimeters**
- ✅ Hidden lines: **short dashed** (HIDDEN linetype)
- ✅ Center lines: **long-short-long** (CENTER linetype)
- ✅ Hatching: **ANSI31, 45°, 5 mm spacing** for concrete
- ✅ Title block is **mandatory** with 8 fields

### **E. KTU Examiner Traps to Avoid**

- ❌ Never use the same layer for everything
- ❌ Never skip the center line through the hexagon
- ❌ Never hatch the bolt (hatch only the sectioned concrete)
- ❌ Never forget the foundation line in the front view
- ❌ Never use continuous linetype for hidden features
- ❌ Never omit the title block fields

### **F. Submission Checklist Before Lab Exam**

- ✅ DWG file saved with proper filename (e.g., `RegNo_FoundationBolt_M20.dwg`)
- ✅ PDF plot generated on **A4 sheet, 1:1 scale**
- ✅ All three views visible: Front, Top, Side
- ✅ All dimensions in mm, ISO-25 style
- ✅ Title block complete with student details
- ✅ Drawing borders and zones present (if A3/A2)

### **G. Cross-Cutting Engineering Knowledge**

- ✅ Foundation bolts resist **axial tension** (pull-out) and **shear** (sliding).
- ✅ Hooked bolts (L, J) provide pull-out resistance through **mechanical interlock** with concrete.
- ✅ Headed bolts provide pull-out resistance through the **head bearing against concrete**.
- ✅ Embedment length is the most critical parameter for holding strength.

### **H. One-Line Final Mantra**

> *"A correctly modeled foundation bolt has three views, proper dimensions, hatched concrete, unhatched bolt, and a complete title block — nothing more, nothing less."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced 2D Models Using Drawing and Editing Tools (Cotter Joint with Bill of Materials)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced 2D Models Using Drawing and Editing Tools (Cotter Joint with Bill of Materials)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cotter Joint (KTU Machine Drawing Terminology):**
> A *cotter joint* is a temporary, detachable mechanical fastening used to connect two coaxial rods that transmit **axial tensile or compressive loads**. It is realized by inserting a flat, wedge-shaped key (called a *cotter*) through a transverse slot cut across the enlarged end of one rod (the *socket*) and the reduced end of the other rod (the *spigot*). The joint is named after its keying element — the cotter — which is responsible for transmitting the load via bearing on the slot walls.

A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** in the CAD context is a structured tabular document that itemizes every standard, bought-out, and fabricated component of an assembly drawing. It is generated as a CAD entity (table object) linked to part balloons or callouts and contains part numbers, descriptions, quantities, and material specifications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a cotter joint as the engineering equivalent of a **wooden clothespin mechanism**:
- Imagine two wooden dowels (rods) you want to connect end-to-end.
- You flatten one end of each dowel on the same plane (creating the slot).
- You then slide a small wedge-shaped wooden stick (the *cotter*) through both flattened ends.
- The wedge pulls both dowels together tightly, holding them against axial pull, yet you can easily knock the wedge out whenever you want to disconnect them — this is exactly what engineers do with pistons, pump rods, and valve stems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Use Cases (KTU Approved Examples):**
> - Connecting the **piston rod to the crosshead** of a reciprocating steam engine.
> - Coupling the **ram and pull rod** of a hydraulic press.
> - Joining **valve stems and tail rods** in feed pumps.
> - Used in **tie-rod and strut assemblies** of structural trusses where occasional dismantling is required.

### 1.3 Standard Empirical Proportions (KTU Standard Data Book)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **standard proportions** are derived from PSG / KTU Machine Design Data Books and must be memorized for the viva and ESE:
>
> - **Diameter of the spigot rod:** $d$
> - **Diameter of the socket collar end:** $d_1 = 1.5\,d$
> - **Outside diameter of socket collar:** $d_2 = 2.0\,d$
> - **Thickness of the cotter:** $t = 0.31\,d$
> - **Width of the cotter:** $b = 4\,d$
> - **Length of the socket collar:** $L = 1.5\,d_1$
> - **Taper on cotter (slope):** $1:6$ (typical), acceptable range $1:6$ to $1:20$
> - **Distance from socket end to first cotter slot:** $a = 0.25\,d$
> - **Distance between the two cotter holes (slotted length):** $e = 2\,b$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Cotter Joint (Disassembled View)

A cotter joint assembly is composed of the following discrete components, each of which must be modeled as an independent part drawing before assembly:

| Component | Function | CAD Layer / Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Socket Rod** | Rod with enlarged socket end containing a slot | Fabricated part |
| **Spigot Rod** | Rod with reduced slotted end fitting inside socket | Fabricated part |
| **Cotter** | Wedge-shaped key transmitting axial force | Standard part |
| **Gib (optional)** | Tapered metal strip on opposite side of cotter | Standard part |
| **Nuts / Split Pins** | Prevents accidental slipping of the cotter | Fastener |

### 2.2 Working Principle — Why and How

- The axial pull $P$ on the rod creates a **bearing stress** on the contact surfaces between the cotter and the slot walls.
- The cotter experiences **shear stress** along two transverse cross-sections (one through each rod).
- The taper generates a wedging action that tightens the joint under load (self-locking taper).
- The taper is held in place either by a **gib head**, a **split pin**, or simply the friction between faces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a taper?** A pure rectangular cotter (no taper) would slacken under alternating/vibratory loads because bearing clearance develops. The taper re-seats itself when pulled, providing a *self-tightening* action.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Design & Drafting)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Proportion / Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | Diameter of spigot rod | Given / to be designed | mm |
| $d_1$ | Enlarged socket diameter | $d_1 = 1.5\,d$ | mm |
| $d_2$ | Outside diameter of socket collar | $d_2 = 2.0\,d$ | mm |
| $t$ | Thickness of cotter | $t = 0.31\,d$ | mm |
| $b$ | Width of cotter (across rod axis) | $b = 4\,d$ | mm |
| $L$ | Length of socket collar | $L = 1.5\,d_1$ | mm |
| $a$ | End distance to first slot | $a = 0.25\,d$ | mm |
| $e$ | Length of slotted region | $e = 2\,b$ | mm |
| $s$ | Slot depth inside rod | $s = 1.5\,t$ | mm |
| Taper | Cotter slope | $1:6$ (standard) | ratio |
| $P$ | Axial load on rod | Design input | N |
| $\sigma_b$ | Bearing stress on cotter | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P}{b \cdot (t - s)}$ | MPa |
| $\tau$ | Shear stress in cotter | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{2 \, b \, t}$ | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Replacement Note:** Inside all markdown tables in this document, vertical bars used as absolute-value delimiters are written as `\vert` so that the markdown table parser does not interpret them as column separators.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ease of Assembly / Disassembly:** Used where frequent maintenance is required (engine valves).
- **Load Direction:** Purely axial; cannot resist rotation or transverse shear — a *limitation*.
- **Material Selection:** Cotter made of **mild steel (MS)** or **medium carbon steel**; rods usually MS.
- **Failure Modes (Viva Question):** Shear failure of cotter, crushing of slot walls, splitting of rod end, bending of cotter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Drafting Procedure & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The procedure below is the **KTU-recommended drafting sequence** using AutoCAD (or equivalent 2D CAD platform). Every command invoked is mapped to the NEP 2020 CAD Lab CO: *“Construct 2D parametric models of mechanical joints using drawing and editing tools.”*

### 3.1 Pre-Drafting Setup

1. Open AutoCAD → **New Drawing** → choose `acadiso.dwt` (millimeter template).
2. Set **UNITS** to *Millimeters*, precision *0.00*.
3. Set **LIMITS** to `0,0` and `420,297` (A3 sheet).
4. Set **LTSCALE** to *1.0*.
5. Create the following **layers**:
   - `CENTER` (color red, linetype center) — for centerlines
   - `OBJ` (color white) — for visible outlines
   - `HIDDEN` (color yellow, linetype hidden) — for hidden details
   - `DIM` (color cyan) — for dimensions
   - `TEXT` (color green) — for notes
   - `BOM` (color magenta) — for the Bill of Materials table
6. Insert **Title Block** in the lower-right corner (KTU Lab format).

### 3.2 Assumption for the Numerical Model

Let the **spigot rod diameter** be chosen as the standard KTU exam value:

$$
d = 40 \text{ mm}
$$

All derived proportions are computed below using the standard formulas from Section 2.3.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Calculation of Cotter Joint Dimensions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Spigot rod diameter } d &= 40 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Socket collar diameter } d_1 &= 1.5 \times d = 1.5 \times 40 = 60 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Outside diameter of socket } d_2 &= 2.0 \times d = 2.0 \times 40 = 80 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Cotter thickness } t &= 0.31 \times d = 0.31 \times 40 = 12.4 \text{ mm} \;\approx\; 12 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Cotter width } b &= 4 \times d = 4 \times 40 = 160 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Socket collar length } L &= 1.5 \times d_1 = 1.5 \times 60 = 90 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{End distance } a &= 0.25 \times d = 0.25 \times 40 = 10 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Slot region length } e &= 2 \times b = 2 \times 160 = 320 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Slot depth } s &= 1.5 \times t = 1.5 \times 12.4 = 18.6 \text{ mm} \;\approx\; 19 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Taper on cotter (1:6) over length } e: \quad \Delta h &= \dfrac{e}{6} = \dfrac{320}{6} \approx 53.33 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the math:** Every numeric substitution is explicitly shown so the student can replicate the calculation for any value of $d$ in the KTU lab record.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step AutoCAD Command Sequence (Analytical Matrix)

The drafting workflow is decomposed into a stage-by-stage command matrix. Each row identifies the *operation*, the *AutoCAD command(s)* used, and the *geometric intent*.

| Step | Stage | Command Used | Geometric Purpose |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Draw spigot rod | `LINE` + `OFFSET` | Two parallel lines $40\,\text{mm}$ apart representing rod width in front view |
| 2 | Draw socket collar | `OFFSET` ($d_2 = 80$) | Enlarged cylindrical end on left |
| 3 | Draw socket transition | `FILLET` (R = 5 mm) | Smooth fillet between $\varnothing\,40$ rod and $\varnothing\,60$ socket |
| 4 | Add centerlines | `LINE` (center linetype) | Horizontal centerline through the entire front view |
| 5 | Create top slot | `OFFSET` (offset $s = 19\,\text{mm}$ above centerline) | Defines slot boundary |
| 6 | Draw cotter hole | `RECTANGLE` ($b = 160\,\text{mm}$ width, $t = 12\,\text{mm}$ height) | Cotter through-slot |
| 7 | Create slot opening on rod | `TRIM` + `EXTEND` | Cuts the slot into both rods |
| 8 | Draw the cotter | `LINE` (with 1:6 taper) | Two converging lines representing cotter profile |
| 9 | Apply taper | `ROTATE` + `ALIGN` (optional) | True taper in side view |
| 10 | Draw side view | `COPY` + `MIRROR` | Generates sectional end view of socket |
| 11 | Add hatch | `HATCH` (ANSI31, scale 2) | Sectional indication on cut portions |
| 12 | Dimension the assembly | `DIMLINEAR` + `DIMDIAMETER` | Adds overall and key feature dimensions |
| 13 | Insert BOM table | `TABLE` | Creates the Bill of Materials in the BOM layer |
| 14 | Add part balloons | `LEADER` + `MTEXT` | Numbered callouts linking to BOM items |

### 3.5 Construction of the Bill of Materials (BOM)

The **BOM table** is constructed using the `TABLE` command. Standard column widths and sample entries are tabulated below:

| Item No. | Part Name | Material | Quantity | Remarks |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Socket Rod | Mild Steel (IS 2062) | 1 | Fabricated |
| 2 | Spigot Rod | Mild Steel (IS 2062) | 1 | Fabricated |
| 3 | Cotter | Medium Carbon Steel | 1 | Standard, taper 1:6 |
| 4 | Split Pin | Mild Steel | 1 | DIN 94 |

**AutoCAD TABLE command workflow:**

- Command: `TABLE`
- Insertion point: top-left corner of title block
- Rows: *5* (1 header + 4 data), Columns: *5*
- Column widths (mm): 20, 60, 80, 25, 60
- Row height: 8 mm
- Text style: `Standard`, height 2.5 mm, center-aligned
- Border line weight: 0.5 mm
- Link part balloons (`LEADER` command) to each row using the matching item number.

### 3.6 Editing Tools Used — Summary

The lab experiment specifically tests the following **editing tools** on the cotter joint model:

- `OFFSET` — for generating parallel socket contours
- `TRIM` + `EXTEND` — for slot definition
- `FILLET` — for rod-to-collar transition
- `CHAMFER` — for the cotter's tapered edge (alternative to ROTATE)
- `MIRROR` — for generating the half-section view
- `COPY` with rectangular array — for repeated dimensions
- `ARRAY` (rectangular) — for generating periodic bolt patterns (in derivative joints)
- `ROTATE` — for orienting the cotter to its true taper angle

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Drafting):** Students commonly forget to **change the linetype of hidden lines** to `DASHED2`. The slot depth lines appearing in the top view *must* be drawn as `HIDDEN` linetype. Marks are routinely deducted for this single oversight.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid diagrams are used here to depict the **CAD workflow architecture** and **BOM data model** because cotter joint physical schematics are best left to the actual lab drawing sheets. All node IDs are purely alphanumeric to satisfy Mermaid v10+ syntax rules.

### 4.1 Cotter Joint CAD Modelling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start New Drawing acadiso dwt] --> A1[Set UNITS mm and LIMITS A3]
    A1 --> A2[Define Layers CENTER OBJ HIDDEN DIM TEXT BOM]
    A2 --> A3[Insert Title Block KTU Format]
    A3 --> B1[Draw Spigot Rod Front View using LINE]
    B1 --> B2[OFFSET to draw Socket Collar Diameter 2d]
    B2 --> B3[FILLET for Rod to Collar Transition]
    B3 --> C1[OFFSET to Define Slot Depth 1.5t]
    C1 --> C2[RECTANGLE for Cotter Through Slot]
    C2 --> C3[TRIM and EXTEND to Clean Slot Geometry]
    C3 --> D1[Draw Cotter with Taper 1 colon 6]
    D1 --> D2[MIRROR to Generate Half Section View]
    D2 --> D3[HATCH ANSI31 for Cut Sections]
    D3 --> E1[DIMLINEAR for Overall Dimensions]
    E1 --> E2[LEADER and MTEXT for Part Balloons]
    E2 --> F1[Insert TABLE for Bill of Materials]
    F1 --> F2[Link Balloons to BOM Rows]
    F2 --> G1[Plot to PDF and Submit]
    G1 --> H1[End of Lab Record]
```

### 4.2 Bill of Materials Data Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[BOM Table Object] --> C1[Item Number Column]
    T1 --> C2[Part Name Column]
    T1 --> C3[Material Column]
    T1 --> C4[Quantity Column]
    T1 --> C5[Remarks Column]
    C2 --> R1[Socket Rod]
    C2 --> R2[Spigot Rod]
    C2 --> R3[Cotter]
    C2 --> R4[Split Pin]
    C3 --> M1[Mild Steel IS 2062]
    C3 --> M2[Medium Carbon Steel]
    C3 --> M3[Mild Steel DIN 94]
    R1 --> L1[Leader Balloon 1]
    R2 --> L2[Leader Balloon 2]
    R3 --> L3[Leader Balloon 3]
    R4 --> L4[Leader Balloon 4]
    L1 --> T1
    L2 --> T1
    L3 --> T1
    L4 --> T1
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Cotter Joint Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sub1[Input Force Path]
        P1[Axial Tensile Load P] --> SR[Spigot Rod d equals 40 mm]
    end
    subgraph Sub2[Load Transfer Block]
        SR --> SLOT[Slot in Spigot s equals 19 mm]
        SLOT --> COT[Cotter b equals 160 mm t equals 12 mm]
        COT --> SOCK[Socket Slot s equals 19 mm]
        SOCK --> SOCKET[Socket Collar d2 equals 80 mm]
    end
    subgraph Sub3[Reaction Block]
        SOCKET --> RF[Reaction Force equals P]
    end
    COT -.->|Taper 1 colon 6| TLOCK[Self Tightening Wedging Action]
    TLOCK --> SR
    TLOCK --> SOCKET
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *(2 Marks for answer + 1 Mark for sketch/diagram)*

**Q1.** Define a *cotter joint*. Mention any two industrial applications.

**Model Answer:**
A cotter joint is a temporary, detachable fastening used to connect two coaxial rods that transmit axial loads. The connection is made by inserting a flat, wedge-shaped key (called a cotter) into a transverse slot cut through the enlarged socket end of one rod and the reduced spigot end of the other rod.

**Two Industrial Applications:**
1. Connection between the piston rod and the crosshead of a reciprocating steam engine.
2. Coupling the tail rod and plunger in a feed pump or hydraulic press.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Retest]** *(2 Marks for answer + 1 Mark for sketch/diagram)*

**Q2.** Why is a *taper* provided on the cotter? State the standard taper ratio used.

**Model Answer:**
The taper on a cotter is provided to make the joint **self-tightening** under axial tensile loads. As the load pulls the rods apart, the cotter is drawn deeper into its slot, eliminating any slack that may develop. The taper also aids in easy removal by driving it out from the opposite side.

**Standard taper:** $1:6$ (one unit rise per six units of length). Acceptable design range is $1:6$ to $1:20$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Draw the *front view* and *side view* (half in section) of a cotter joint used to connect two $\varnothing\,40\,\text{mm}$ mild-steel rods. Show all standard proportions and the cotter in position. Use AutoCAD commands and list the editing tools applied.

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. Compute proportions:
   - $d_1 = 60\,\text{mm}$, $d_2 = 80\,\text{mm}$, $t = 12.4\,\text{mm}$, $b = 160\,\text{mm}$, $L = 90\,\text{mm}$, $a = 10\,\text{mm}$, $e = 320\,\text{mm}$, $s = 18.6\,\text{mm}$, taper $1:6$.
2. Front view: draw two horizontal centerlines (rod axis), use `LINE` and `OFFSET` for parallel outlines, `FILLET` for transition, `RECTANGLE` for cotter slot, `OFFSET` for slot depth.
3. Side view: `MIRROR` to generate half-section; `HATCH` ANSI31 for cut areas.
4. Editing tools list: `OFFSET`, `TRIM`, `EXTEND`, `FILLET`, `MIRROR`, `COPY`.
5. Dimensions added using `DIMLINEAR`, `DIMDIAMETER`, `DIMRADIUS`.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* Prepare the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** for the cotter joint in AutoCAD using the `TABLE` command. List four components with material, quantity, and a leader-balloon linkage strategy.

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. Use `TABLE` command, insert at top-left of title block.
2. Define 5 columns (Item, Part, Material, Qty, Remarks) and 5 rows.
3. Populate:

| Item | Part | Material | Qty | Remarks |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Socket Rod | Mild Steel IS 2062 | 1 | Fabricated |
| 2 | Spigot Rod | Mild Steel IS 2062 | 1 | Fabricated |
| 3 | Cotter | Medium Carbon Steel | 1 | Taper 1:6 |
| 4 | Split Pin | Mild Steel | 1 | DIN 94 |

4. Use `LEADER` to balloon each part on the drawing with matching numerals.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **design proportions** of a cotter joint for a rod of diameter $d = 50\,\text{mm}$.

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 50 \text{ mm} \\[2pt]
d_1 &= 1.5 \times 50 = 75 \text{ mm} \\[2pt]
d_2 &= 2.0 \times 50 = 100 \text{ mm} \\[2pt]
t &= 0.31 \times 50 = 15.5 \text{ mm} \\[2pt]
b &= 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ mm} \\[2pt]
L &= 1.5 \times 75 = 112.5 \text{ mm} \\[2pt]
a &= 0.25 \times 50 = 12.5 \text{ mm} \\[2pt]
e &= 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ mm} \\[2pt]
s &= 1.5 \times 15.5 = 23.25 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Sketch: full front view with cotter inserted; label all proportions; indicate taper $1:6$.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* Describe the **drafting procedure in AutoCAD** for generating the cotter profile with a $1:6$ taper. Mention the exact commands and the geometric construction steps.

**Model Solution:**

1. Draw a rectangle of size $160\,\text{mm} \times 12\,\text{mm}$ (the cotter, untapered) using `RECTANGLE`.
2. Draw a horizontal reference line through the cotter's center.
3. Offset the rectangle's left edge by $160\,\text{mm}$ to mark the tapered end.
4. Compute the rise: $\Delta h = 160/6 = 26.67\,\text{mm}$.
5. Use `LINE` to connect the top-left corner to a point $26.67\,\text{mm}$ higher on the right edge — this defines the taper.
6. Apply `TRIM` to remove the unwanted rectangular portion.
7. Use `ROTATE` with angle $\arctan(1/6)$ if the taper is to be drawn horizontally (alternative construction).
8. Verify with `DIMALIGNED` measurement between the two tapered faces.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Cost to Student):**
> 1. **Forgetting the BOM layer separation** — Examiner checks layer isolation. Marks lost if BOM is drawn on the `OBJ` layer.
> 2. **Missing linetype conversion** — Hidden slot lines drawn as continuous lines = −2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong proportion** — Using $t = 0.31\,d$ but rounding incorrectly (e.g., $12\,\text{mm}$ for $d = 40$ is acceptable, but $13\,\text{mm}$ is not). Examiner expects *one* decimal.
> 4. **No taper shown on cotter** — Cotter drawn as a plain rectangle = −3 marks (major error).
> 5. **Part balloons not matching BOM item numbers** — Examiner cross-checks balloon numerals with table; mismatches lead to −2 marks.
> 6. **Title block incomplete** — Missing student name, roll number, sheet number, or scale = −1 mark each.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cotter joint** = temporary, axial-load, detachable fastening.
- **Five standard proportions to memorize:** $d_1 = 1.5\,d$, $d_2 = 2.0\,d$, $t = 0.31\,d$, $b = 4\,d$, $L = 1.5\,d_1$.
- **Taper** $1:6$ is *self-tightening* under tensile load — never omit it.
- **Slot depth** $s = 1.5\,t$ (used by both rod and socket).
- **Editing tools tested:** OFFSET, TRIM, EXTEND, FILLET, MIRROR, COPY, ROTATE.
- **BOM must be on its own layer** with linked leader balloons.
- **Hidden lines for slot depths must use the HIDDEN linetype.**
- **Failure modes** (viva-favourite): cotter shear, slot crushing, rod splitting, cotter bending.
- **Industrial uses**: piston–crosshead connection, pump tail rod, hydraulic press ram.
- **AutoCAD commands**: `LINE`, `OFFSET`, `RECTANGLE`, `FILLET`, `TRIM`, `EXTEND`, `MIRROR`, `HATCH`, `DIMLINEAR`, `LEADER`, `TABLE`, `MTEXT`.
- **Sheet standard**: A3 (420 × 297 mm) with appropriate title block.
- **Always verify** computed proportions using the standard data book table in the lab record.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced 2D Models Using Drawing and Editing Tools (Knuckle Joint with Bill of Materials)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced 2D Models Using Drawing and Editing Tools (Knuckle Joint with Bill of Materials)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Knuckle Joint** is a mechanical pin-type joint used to connect two intersecting coplanar rods that are subjected to tensile loads. It permits limited angular movement (oscillation) between the connected rods in the plane of the joint. The joint is assembled using a **knuckle pin** which is inserted through a **single eye** (on one rod) and a **double eye / fork end** (on the other rod), secured by a **taper pin**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPEL307):** A knuckle joint is a standard machine component drawn in 2D using advanced drafting tools — Line, Polyline, Circle, Offset, Trim, Fillet, Mirror, Hatch, Dimensioning, Layers, and Table/Bill of Materials (BOM). It tests the learner's ability to produce a **production-ready engineering drawing** with full sectional views, standard dimensioning, and a structured parts list.

### Designation as a "Pin Joint" in Machine Design

The knuckle joint is categorized under **Permanent / Semi-Permanent Fastened Joints** because once the taper pin is driven in, the joint can only be dismantled by destroying the pin. It is widely used in:
- Connecting rod of IC engines (small ends, historically)
- Steering linkages of automobiles
- Roller mill stands
- Valve mechanisms
- Pull rods of locomotives

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine your **finger knuckles**. Your hand has bones (rod 1), and another hand's bone (rod 2) is connected at the knuckle using a **pin** (your finger joint). The bones can rotate a little bit relative to each other — that is exactly how a mechanical knuckle joint behaves. The **taper pin** at the end is like a **cotter pin** in a lock — once you hammer it in, the joint is locked and can only be opened by destroying the pin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for Students:** Think *"Pin + Eyes + Fork = Knuckle Joint"*. The single eye goes INTO the fork like a sandwich filling, and the pin pierces through all three layers like a skewer through food.

## 1.3 Components of a Knuckle Joint (Terminology Primer)

| Sl. No. | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Single Eye Rod** | One rod end has a forged circular eye (one thickness). |
| 2 | **Double Eye / Fork Rod** | The other rod end is forked into two prongs. |
| 3 | **Knuckle Pin** | Cylindrical pin passing through both eyes to hold them together. |
| 4 | **Pin Head** | Hexagonal/circular head preventing pin from sliding through. |
| 5 | **Taper Pin (Split Pin equivalent)** | Tapered pin driven through the knuckle pin to lock it. |
| 6 | **Collar** | A small raised boss on the single eye that fits between the two prongs of the fork. |

## 1.4 What is a Bill of Materials (BOM)?

A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is a structured tabular list attached to an engineering drawing that itemizes:
- **Part Number / Item No.**
- **Part Description / Name**
- **Material of Construction** (e.g., Mild Steel IS 2062, EN8)
- **Quantity Required per Assembly**
- **Key Dimensions / Size**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU CAD Lab viva, a common question is: *"Why is BOM placed on the drawing?"* — The answer is that BOM acts as a **manufacturing instruction sheet**, telling the shop-floor what to make, in what material, and how many. Without BOM, the drawing alone is incomplete for production.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standard Empirical Proportions (Machine Design Data Book Values)

Every dimension of a knuckle joint is expressed as a **multiple of the rod diameter `d`**. This is the universal design language. If your examiner gives you only the rod diameter `d`, you can derive every other dimension.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Empirical Formulas** (as per PSG Design Data Book / V.B. Bhandari / Khurmi — universally accepted for KTU exams).

### 2.1.1 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Rod Diameter = `d`)

| Symbol | Parameter | Empirical Relation | Typical Range |
|:---:|---|:---:|:---:|
| $d$ | Diameter of the rod | Given | — |
| $d_1$ | Diameter of knuckle pin | $d_1 = 1.1 \, d$ | $1.0d$ to $1.1d$ |
| $d_2$ | Outer diameter of eye (single) | $d_2 = 2.0 \, d$ | $2.0d$ |
| $d_3$ | Inner diameter of eye | $d_3 = 1.5 \, d$ | $1.5d$ |
| $d_4$ | Diameter of pin head | $d_4 = 1.6 \, d_1$ | $1.6d_1$ |
| $d_5$ | Diameter of collar | $d_5 = 1.5 \, d_1$ to $2.0 \, d_1$ | — |
| $d_6$ | Diameter of taper pin | $d_6 = 0.25 \, d_1$ to $0.35 \, d_1$ | $d_1/3$ typical |
| $t$ | Thickness of single eye | $t = 1.25 \, d$ | $1.1d$ to $1.25d$ |
| $t_1$ | Thickness of fork (each prong) | $t_1 = 0.75 \, d$ | $0.75d$ |
| $a$ | Distance from rod centre to eye end | $a = 1.5 \, d$ to $2.0 \, d$ | — |
| $b$ | Width of single eye (flat portion) | $b = 2.0 \, d$ to $2.5 \, d$ | — |
| $c$ | Clearance between eye and fork | $c = 0.1 \, d$ to $0.2 \, d$ | Negligible in drawing |

### 2.1.2 Worked Numerical Reference (Most Asked in KTU)

Assume the most frequently asked rod diameter in KTU boards:

$$d = 30 \text{ mm}$$

Plugging into the formula sheet:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= 1.1 \times 30 = 33 \text{ mm} \\
d_2 &= 2.0 \times 30 = 60 \text{ mm} \\
d_3 &= 1.5 \times 30 = 45 \text{ mm} \\
d_4 &= 1.6 \times 33 = 52.8 \text{ mm} \approx 53 \text{ mm} \\
t   &= 1.25 \times 30 = 37.5 \text{ mm} \\
t_1 &= 0.75 \times 30 = 22.5 \text{ mm} \\
a   &= 1.75 \times 30 = 52.5 \text{ mm} \\
b   &= 2.25 \times 30 = 67.5 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Examiners usually round off to convenient numbers. If `d = 25 mm`, then `d₁ = 27.5 ≈ 28 mm`. Always cross-check with your specific question paper.

## 2.2 Design Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Formula

- **Knuckle pin (`d₁ = 1.1d`)**: Larger than the rod to prevent shear failure. Pin is designed in **double shear** because the fork sandwiches the eye — so its shear area is doubled.
- **Outer eye (`d₂ = 2d`)**: Twice the rod diameter gives a safe stress distribution around the pin hole.
- **Inner eye (`d₃ = 1.5d`)**: Must accommodate the knuckle pin `d₁` with a small clearance (≈ 1 mm).
- **Single eye thickness (`t = 1.25d`)**: Prevents the eye from tearing out (tearing/shear failure of the eye).
- **Fork prong thickness (`t₁ = 0.75d`)**: Each prong is thinner than the single eye because the load is shared between two prongs. Hence the sum `2 × t₁ = 1.5d ≈ t`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Check (Why `2t₁ ≈ t`?):** The single eye resists the full tensile pull `P`. The fork has TWO prongs, so each resists `P/2`. For equal shear stress: `t × τ = 2 × t₁ × τ`. Hence `t = 2 × t₁`, meaning `1.25d = 2 × 0.625d`. Designers use `t₁ = 0.75d` with a safety factor.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

Knuckle joints are the **workhorses of low-cost, load-bearing linkages**. They are preferred where:
- Precise alignment is **not critical**
- **Small angular motion** is required (≈ 30° max)
- Loads are **tensile / compressive** (NOT torsional)
- Cost of manufacture must be low (forged components)

**Failure Modes** that BOM/Drawing must help the inspector catch:
1. **Shear failure of the pin** — Prevented by `d₁ = 1.1d`.
2. **Tearing of the eye** — Prevented by `d₂ = 2d`.
3. **Crushing of the pin against hole** — Prevented by adequate plate thickness.
4. **Bending of the pin** — Negligible for short joints; `L_pin ≈ 2t₁ + t`.

## 2.4 Drawing Convention Summary (KTU Standard)

- **Hidden lines (dashed)**: Show the knuckle pin inside the assembly.
- **Centre lines (chain)**: Through all circular features (eyes, pin).
- **Sectional view (Hatching)**: Typically a **half-section** on the assembly drawing.
- **Hatch angle**: **45°**, spacing ≈ 2–3 mm, for ferrous metals.
- **Title block**: Bottom-right of the sheet (per IS 1505 / SP 46).
- **BOM location**: **Top-right corner** of the drawing sheet (per ISO 7200 / IS conventions).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Drafting Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pre-Drawing Setup (AutoCAD Workflow)

### Step 1 — Sheet & Layer Configuration

```
Layer Setup (MUST):
  1. Layer "OUTLINE"   — Continuous thick (0.5 mm), Colour WHITE
  2. Layer "HIDDEN"    — Dashed thin (0.25 mm), Colour MAGENTA
  3. Layer "CENTRE"    — Centre thin (0.25 mm), Colour RED
  4. Layer "HATCH"     — Continuous thin (0.25 mm), Colour CYAN
  5. Layer "DIM"       — Continuous thin (0.25 mm), Colour GREEN
  6. Layer "TEXT"      — Continuous thin (0.25 mm), Colour BLUE
  7. Layer "BORDER"    — Continuous thick (0.7 mm), Colour WHITE
```

> [!TIP]
> Examiners check the Layer list. A drawing without layered architecture loses 1–2 marks.

### Step 2 — Limits, Units & Dimension Style

- `LIMITS`: A2 sheet (594 × 420 mm) or A3 (420 × 297 mm) for knuckle joint.
- `UNITS`: Decimal, 0 decimal places for mm.
- `DIMSTYLE`: Create a style named `ISO-25` with:
  - Arrow size = 2.5 mm
  - Text height = 2.5 mm
  - Extension gap = 1 mm

---

## 3.2 Drawing the **Single Eye Rod** (Analytical Construction)

### Step 3 — Draw the Rod Centre-line and Profile

1. **Command:** `LINE` from `(0, 0)` to `(0, -100)`. This is the **vertical centre-line** of the rod.
2. **Command:** `OFFSET` the centre-line by `d/2 = 15 mm` on both sides (left and right) for `d = 30 mm`.
3. **Command:** `OFFSET` the centre-line by `(t/2) = 18.75 mm` on both sides to define eye thickness `t`.

> [!NOTE]
> Vertical line represents the rod axis. The "15 mm" half-width is the rod. The "18.75 mm" half-width is the wider eye region.

### Step 4 — Construct the Eye

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Eye outer radius} &= d_2 / 2 = 30 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Eye inner radius} &= d_3 / 2 = 22.5 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Eye hole radius (for pin)} &= d_1 / 2 = 16.5 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Construction sequence:**

1. Mark the **centre of the eye** at distance `a = 52.5 mm` from the bottom of the rod.
2. `CIRCLE` command: Draw outer eye `Ø60 mm` and inner eye `Ø45 mm` at the eye centre.
3. `CIRCLE` command: Draw the **knuckle pin hole** `Ø33 mm` concentric with the eye circles.
4. Use `TRIM` to remove the rod lines that enter the eye region.
5. Use `LINE` to draw the **straight tangent portions** connecting rod to the eye — these are **shoulder lines**.

### Step 5 — Fillet the Shoulder

**Command:** `FILLET` with radius `r = 1.5 × d` to **3 × d` (typically `45 mm` to `90 mm`).

- `FILLET` rounds the sharp transition between the rod and the outer eye, simulating the **forged curve**.
- For KTU drawing, a fillet radius of `2d = 60 mm` is acceptable.

---

## 3.3 Drawing the **Fork / Double Eye Rod**

The fork is a **mirror image** of the single eye — with two prongs.

### Step 6 — Draw the Fork Profile

1. Copy the single eye rod block using `COPY` with a base point at `(200, 0)` (offset to the right).
2. Draw the **central slot** between the two prongs:
   - Slot width = `d₃ + clearance = 45 + 2 = 47 mm` (so pin `Ø33` fits with breathing room).
   - Slot length = `t + 2 = 37.5 + 2 ≈ 40 mm`.
3. Use `OFFSET` to create the two prong thicknesses `t₁ = 22.5 mm` each.
4. `TRIM` to convert the single eye into a fork.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The total width across both prongs = `2t₁ + slot_width = 2 × 22.5 + 47 = 92 mm`. The rod width `b` should match this on the outer side.

### Step 7 — Construct the Slot End

The slot terminates in a **semicircle** of diameter = slot width = `47 mm`. Use `ARC` command with `Start, End, Radius` option, or `CIRCLE` + `TRIM`.

---

## 3.4 Drawing the **Knuckle Pin**

### Step 8 — Pin Geometry

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pin shaft diameter} &= d_1 = 33 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Pin head diameter} &= d_4 = 53 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Pin head thickness} &= 0.5 \times d_1 = 16.5 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Taper pin hole diameter} &= d_6 = d_1/3 = 11 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Commands:**

1. Draw a separate view of the pin (top-right of sheet) — this is the **detail view**.
2. `RECTANGLE` for the head profile (or use polygon for hex).
3. `CIRCLE` for the cylindrical body `Ø33`.
4. `CIRCLE` for the taper pin hole `Ø11` at the centre of the head.
5. `CHAMFER` the head to give it a hex shape (optional, shows skill).
6. Add a **key/keyway** (small slot) on the pin body to show taper pin entry.

---

## 3.5 Assembly View & Sectioning

### Step 9 — Position the Single Eye INSIDE the Fork

1. `MOVE` the single eye rod so that its eye centre coincides with the fork's slot centre.
2. The single eye must fit **between** the two fork prongs.
3. Use `ALIGN` or `MOVE` with displacement `(Δx, Δy)` to lock them in place.

### Step 10 — Insert the Knuckle Pin

1. `COPY` the pin from its detail view and `ROTATE` it to horizontal orientation (if needed).
2. Place it through the holes of both eyes.
3. The pin shaft passes through: `Prong1 → SingleEye → Prong2`.
4. Pin head sits on the outside of one prong.

### Step 11 — Half-Section View

For the KTU submission, **half-section in front view** is mandatory.

**Procedure:**
1. Select the front half of the assembly (the side you want to section).
2. `HATCH` command:
   - Pattern: `ANSI31` (or `ANSI32`)
   - Scale: **1.0** (adjust to 0.5 if pattern too dense)
   - Angle: **45°**
   - Pick internal points inside the rod, eye, and pin.

> [!TIP]
> **Hatching Tip:** Always confirm that you pick closed boundaries. Open boundaries will throw an error. Use `HATCH > Select Boundary > Pick Points` for AutoCAD ease.

---

## 3.6 Dimensioning the Drawing

### Step 12 — Apply Dimensions

| Dimension Type | Apply To | Command |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | Rod diameter `Ø30`, Pin diameter `Ø33` | `DIMLINEAR` |
| **Aligned** | Eye outer diameter `Ø60`, eye inner `Ø45` | `DIMALIGNED` |
| **Radius** | Fillet radius `R60` | `DIMRADIUS` |
| **Angular** | If fork has taper — optional | `DIMANGULAR` |
| **Leader** | "Ø30 — Rod", "M.S. EN8" | `QLEADER` |

**Dimension Style Checklist (KTU Examiner Looks For):**
- ✅ Arrows filled triangles, not dots/strokes
- ✅ Extension lines 1 mm beyond dimension line
- ✅ Text **above** dimension line, centred
- ✅ Decimal separator `.` (not comma)
- ✅ Diameter prefix `Ø` (Alt+0216) or text "DIA"
- ✅ No dimension on hidden lines (use aligned view)

---

## 3.7 The Bill of Materials (BOM) — Tabular Drafting

### Step 13 — Build the BOM Table

In AutoCAD, use `TABLE` command. Set up:

- **Rows**: 7 (1 header + 6 items)
- **Columns**: 5 (Item No., Description, Material, Qty, Remarks)
- **Row height**: 8 mm
- **Column widths**: 15, 60, 50, 15, 30 mm

### Step 14 — Populate the BOM

| ITEM NO. | DESCRIPTION | MATERIAL | QTY | REMARKS |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Single Eye Rod | Mild Steel IS 2062 | 1 | Forged |
| 2 | Double Eye (Fork) Rod | Mild Steel IS 2062 | 1 | Forged |
| 3 | Knuckle Pin | EN8 / Medium Carbon Steel | 1 | Hardened & Ground |
| 4 | Pin Head | EN8 | 1 | Integral with pin |
| 5 | Taper Pin | Mild Steel | 1 | Split type |
| 6 | Collar (if separate) | Mild Steel | 1 | Press-fitted on single eye |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> BOM convention: **Item 1 is the main/base component** (the larger rod). Components are listed in **assembly order** — the part that fits last (taper pin) appears last.

### Step 15 — Title Block

- Place title block at bottom-right corner.
- Fields: Drawing Title, Name, Scale, Sheet Size, Date, College Logo, Roll No.
- Scale for assembly: **1:1** for knuckle joint (small component).

---

## 3.8 Final QC Checklist Before Submission

- [ ] All layers correctly used (no white-on-white invisible lines).
- [ ] No duplicate / overlapping objects.
- [ ] All closed regions properly hatched.
- [ ] All hidden lines dashed (Linetype = `DASHED`).
- [ ] Centre lines drawn as `CENTER` linetype, extending 2 mm beyond circles.
- [ ] BOM aligned to top-right corner above title block.
- [ ] Title block complete with all fields.
- [ ] Border line thickness 0.7 mm (continuous).
- [ ] Drawing saved as `.dwg` AND exported as `.pdf`.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Component Interaction Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Sheet Setup A2 Limits 594x420] --> B[Step 2: Layer Configuration 7 Layers Created]
    B --> C[Step 3: Draw Single Eye Rod with CIRCLE OFFSET TRIM]
    C --> D[Step 4: Apply FILLET to Shoulder Radius 2d]
    D --> E[Step 5: Copy Single Eye and Convert to Fork using OFFSET TRIM]
    E --> F[Step 6: Position Single Eye inside Fork Slot using MOVE]
    F --> G[Step 7: Insert Knuckle Pin through both eyes using COPY ROTATE]
    G --> H[Step 8: Add Taper Pin detail to lock assembly]
    H --> I[Step 9: Apply Half Section using HATCH ANSI31 45 Degree]
    I --> J[Step 10: Dimension all features using DIMLINEAR DIMALIGNED DIMRADIUS]
    J --> K[Step 11: Build BOM Table at Top Right using TABLE 5 Columns 7 Rows]
    K --> L[Step 12: Add Title Block Bottom Right with Name Scale Date]
    L --> M[Step 13: Final Quality Check Export DWG and PDF]
    M --> N[Submission Ready Knuckle Joint Assembly]
```

## 4.2 Drafting Workflow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Stage]
        A1[Rod Diameter d equals 30 mm]
        A2[Formula Table from Data Book]
    end
    subgraph Calc[Calculation Stage]
        B1[Compute d1 d2 d3 d4 t t1]
        B2[Validate using t approx 2 times t1]
    end
    subgraph Draft[Drafting Stage]
        C1[Geometry Construction]
        C2[Sectioning Hatching]
        C3[Dimensioning]
    end
    subgraph Output[Output Stage]
        D1[Title Block]
        D2[Bill of Materials]
        D3[Final Plot PDF]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Component Relationship Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Knuckle Pin d1] --> SE[Single Eye d3 hole]
    SE --> FE[Fork Prong 1 t1]
    SE --> FE2[Fork Prong 2 t1]
    FE --> P
    FE2 --> P
    P --> PH[Pin Head d4]
    PH --> TP[Taper Pin d6]
    TP -.locks.-> P
    C[Collar on Single Eye] -.fits between.-> FE
```

## 4.4 Sectional View Convention (KTU Board Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Front View Original] --> B[Cutting Plane Line A-A]
    B --> C[Remove Front Half]
    C --> D[Reveal Internal Features]
    D --> E[Hatch Cut Surfaces 45 degrees]
    E --> F[Show Hidden Features with Dashed Lines]
    F --> G[Final Half Section View]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**List any SIX components of a knuckle joint and state the function of the knuckle pin.**

**Model Answer:**

The six main components of a knuckle joint are:
1. **Single Eye Rod** — Holds one end with a single forged eye.
2. **Double Eye (Fork) Rod** — Two prongs to receive the single eye.
3. **Knuckle Pin** — Locks the assembly and transmits load.
4. **Pin Head** — Prevents pin from sliding through.
5. **Taper Pin** — Locks the knuckle pin against rotation/withdrawal.
6. **Collar** — Provides axial location between fork prongs.

**Function of Knuckle Pin:** It is the load-carrying member of the joint. It transfers the tensile load from one rod to the other through **double shear** (since the single eye is sandwiched between two fork prongs). It is made larger than the rod diameter ($d_1 = 1.1d$) to prevent shear failure.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**What is a Bill of Materials (BOM)? State any four fields of a typical BOM used in engineering drawings.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is a structured tabular list attached to an engineering assembly drawing that itemizes all the components required to manufacture/assemble the product, along with their materials, quantities, and part numbers.

Four fields of a typical BOM are:
1. **Item / Part Number** — Unique identifier for each component.
2. **Description / Part Name** — Name of the part (e.g., Single Eye Rod).
3. **Material of Construction** — Grade/specification of material (e.g., Mild Steel IS 2062).
4. **Quantity Required** — Number of pieces per assembly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Draw the assembled view of a knuckle joint connecting two rods of diameter `d = 25 mm`. The joint should include a single eye, a fork end, a knuckle pin with head, and a taper pin. Show the half-sectional front view, all standard dimensions, and the Bill of Materials.**

**Assumed values for d = 25 mm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= 1.1 \times 25 = 27.5 \text{ mm} \approx 28 \text{ mm} \\
d_2 &= 2.0 \times 25 = 50 \text{ mm} \\
d_3 &= 1.5 \times 25 = 37.5 \text{ mm} \\
d_4 &= 1.6 \times 27.5 = 44 \text{ mm} \\
t   &= 1.25 \times 25 = 31.25 \text{ mm} \\
t_1 &= 0.75 \times 25 = 18.75 \text{ mm} \\
a   &= 1.75 \times 25 = 43.75 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Drafting Procedure

**Steps to be performed (write in exam):**

1. **Set up A2 sheet** with proper title block and 7 layers (Outline, Hidden, Centre, Hatch, Dim, Text, Border).
2. **Draw vertical centre-line** of length 200 mm representing rod axis.
3. **Construct Single Eye Rod (right side):**
   - Offset centre-line by `d/2 = 12.5 mm` and `t/2 = 15.625 mm` on both sides.
   - Mark eye centre at distance `a = 43.75 mm` from rod bottom.
   - Draw circles Ø50 (outer eye), Ø37.5 (inner eye), Ø28 (pin hole) concentric.
   - Trim rod portions entering the eye.
   - Apply FILLET `R = 2d = 50 mm` at the rod-eye transition.
4. **Construct Fork End (left side):**
   - Mirror the single eye profile.
   - Cut a slot of width `d₃ + 2 = 39.5 mm` and depth `t + 2 = 33.25 mm`.
   - Each prong thickness `t₁ = 18.75 mm`.
   - Slot terminates in semicircle of Ø39.5 mm.
5. **Insert Knuckle Pin (horizontal):**
   - Pin shaft Ø28, head Ø44, head thickness 14 mm.
   - Tapper pin hole Ø9.2 mm (`d₁/3`).
6. **Apply half-section:**
   - Hatch all cut surfaces at 45° using ANSI31 pattern.
   - Show the hidden half with dashed lines.
7. **Dimensioning:**
   - Linear: Ø25 (rod), Ø50, Ø37.5, Ø28.
   - Aligned: t = 31.25, t₁ = 18.75, a = 43.75.
   - Radius: R50 for fillet.
   - Add leader note: "ALL DIMENSIONS IN mm".

### Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: BOM and Title Block

**BOM Table (placed top-right of sheet):**

| ITEM | DESCRIPTION | MATERIAL | QTY |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | Fork End Rod | Mild Steel IS 2062 | 1 |
| 2 | Single Eye Rod | Mild Steel IS 2062 | 1 |
| 3 | Knuckle Pin | EN8 Carbon Steel | 1 |
| 4 | Pin Head | EN8 (Integral) | 1 |
| 5 | Taper Pin | Mild Steel | 1 |

**Title Block (bottom-right):**

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Title | KNUCKLE JOINT (Assembly) |
| Scale | 1:1 |
| Sheet | A2 |
| Drawn By | [Student Name] |
| Roll No | [Roll Number] |
| Date | [Date] |
| College | [College Name] |

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**For a knuckle joint with rod diameter `d = 40 mm`, calculate all standard dimensions using empirical formulas. List and explain any FOUR design considerations that justify why `d₁ = 1.1d` for the knuckle pin.**

### Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Numerical Calculation

**Given:** $d = 40 \text{ mm}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= 1.1 \times 40 = 44 \text{ mm} \\
d_2 &= 2.0 \times 40 = 80 \text{ mm} \\
d_3 &= 1.5 \times 40 = 60 \text{ mm} \\
d_4 &= 1.6 \times 44 = 70.4 \text{ mm} \approx 70 \text{ mm} \\
t   &= 1.25 \times 40 = 50 \text{ mm} \\
t_1 &= 0.75 \times 40 = 30 \text{ mm} \\
a   &= 1.75 \times 40 = 70 \text{ mm} \\
b   &= 2.25 \times 40 = 90 \text{ mm} \\
d_6 &= d_1/3 = 14.67 \text{ mm} \approx 15 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Value (mm) | Description |
|:---:|:---:|---|
| $d$  | 40  | Rod diameter |
| $d_1$ | 44  | Knuckle pin diameter |
| $d_2$ | 80  | Outer eye diameter |
| $d_3$ | 60  | Inner eye diameter |
| $d_4$ | 70  | Pin head diameter |
| $t$   | 50  | Single eye thickness |
| $t_1$ | 30  | Fork prong thickness |
| $a$   | 70  | Rod-to-eye distance |
| $b$   | 90  | Width of eye |

### Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Four Design Justifications for `d₁ = 1.1d`
