1. **Prevention of Shear Failure:** The knuckle pin carries the entire tensile load `P` from one rod to the other. If the pin were the same size as the rod (`d₁ = d`), it would be the weakest link. Making it 10% larger (`d₁ = 1.1d`) provides a safety margin against shear.

2. **Allowance for Wear and Tear:** In service, the pin-hole interface wears due to relative motion (oscillation). A larger pin ensures that even after some wear, the pin remains functional without immediate replacement.

3. **Manufacturing Tolerances:** Forging and machining cannot produce perfect dimensions. The 10% extra diameter accommodates machining allowances and ensures easy assembly.

4. **Stress Concentration at Hole Edge:** The pin hole is a stress raiser. A larger pin reduces the load per unit area transferred at the contact zone, lowering bearing pressure (`σ_bearing = P / (d₁ × t)`).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the empirical formula table** — Many students write random numbers. Always start with "Given: d = X mm" and derive step-by-step.
> 2. **Not mentioning "double shear"** — When asked why `t = 2t₁`, students forget the fundamental principle of shear distribution.
> 3. **Hatch angle inconsistency** — All hatched surfaces must be at the **same 45° angle** with the **same spacing**. Inconsistent hatching = direct loss of 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Missing BOM or wrong column order** — Standard order: Item → Description → Material → Qty → Remarks.
> 5. **No centre lines on circles** — Every circle MUST have a centre line passing through it. This is the most common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision for KTU Board Exam / Lab Viva — Read This Twice.**

### A. Numerical / Formula Points

- Rod diameter `d` is **always given**; everything else is derived.
- $d_1 = 1.1d$ (Pin) — Remember "1.1" — it is the most asked number.
- $d_2 = 2.0d$ (Outer Eye) — "Double of rod".
- $d_3 = 1.5d$ (Inner Eye) — "1.5 times rod".
- $d_4 = 1.6 \times d_1$ (Pin Head).
- $t = 1.25d$ (Single Eye thickness).
- $t_1 = 0.75d$ (Fork Prong thickness).
- $a = 1.75d$ (Eye centre distance from rod end).
- $b = 2.25d$ (Outer eye width).
- $d_6 = d_1/3$ (Taper pin diameter).

### B. Drawing / Drafting Points

- **7 layers minimum**: Outline, Hidden, Centre, Hatch, Dim, Text, Border.
- **Linetypes**: Continuous (thick/visible), Dashed (thin/hidden), Centre (chain thin).
- **Hatch**: 45°, ANSI31 pattern for ferrous metals.
- **Title block**: Bottom-right of sheet.
- **BOM**: Top-right of sheet, **above** title block.
- **Sheet size**: A2 preferred for full knuckle joint with BOM.
- **Scale**: 1:1 (knuckle joint is a small component, fits 1:1).

### C. Conceptual Points

- Knuckle joint is a **pin joint**, **NOT** a cotter joint (cotter uses a flat key, knuckle uses a round pin).
- Pin is in **double shear** because single eye is sandwiched.
- Taper pin is **NOT** load-bearing — it only prevents pin from falling out.
- Knuckle joint permits **angular motion** in the plane of the joint — it is NOT a universal joint.
- Material: **Mild Steel (IS 2062)** for rods; **EN8 / Medium Carbon Steel** for knuckle pin (hardened).

### D. Common Viva Questions & One-Line Answers

| Question | One-Line Answer |
|---|---|
| Why is the pin larger than the rod? | To prevent shear failure under load. |
| Why is fork prong thinner than single eye? | Because load is shared between two prongs. |
| What is the role of the taper pin? | To lock the knuckle pin against rotation and axial movement. |
| Why is the collar used? | To maintain the gap between fork prongs and locate the single eye axially. |
| Can knuckle joint transmit torque? | No, it transmits only tensile / compressive axial loads. |
| What is the difference between knuckle and cotter joint? | Knuckle uses a round pin (double shear); cotter uses a flat key (single shear). |

### E. KTU Exam Day Strategy

1. **Read the question carefully** — Note the rod diameter (most common: 25, 30, 40 mm).
2. **Compute dimensions first** in the rough sheet.
3. **Plan layer setup** before drawing.
4. **Draw in this order**: Centre-lines → Outer profile → Inner features → Hatching → Dimensioning → BOM → Title Block.
5. **Save as `.dwg`** AND export `.pdf` (most KTU submissions require both).

> [!TIP]
> **Bonus Tip:** Add a **detail view** of the knuckle pin in a separate corner of the sheet. This shows the examiner that you understand the assembly hierarchy and is worth 1–2 bonus marks in the viva.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced 2D Models Using Drawing and Editing Tools (Flanged Coupling with Bill of Materials)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced 2D Models Using Drawing and Editing Tools (Flanged Coupling with Bill of Materials)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flanged Coupling (IS 6661 / IS 2693 Compliant):** A *flanged coupling* is a **rigid, torsionally stiff shaft coupling** used to connect two coaxial shafts for the purpose of transmitting power (torque) without any relative motion between the driving and driven shafts. It consists of two **separate flanges (or flanged hubs)** keyed or shrunk onto the respective shaft ends, which are then brought face-to-face and rigidly bolted together through a series of **high-tensile bolts, nuts, and washers** arranged on a Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme CAD Laboratory**, an "Advanced 2D Model" refers to a multi-view orthographic production drawing created in **AutoCAD (or equivalent 2D CAD software)** using a combination of primitive drawing primitives (Lines, Circles, Arcs, Polylines) and advanced modification tools (Offset, Mirror, Array, Fillet, Chamfer, Trim, Extend, Copy, Rotate, Block, Attribute). The model must conform to **First Angle Projection (as per IS 696)** or **Third Angle Projection** standards and must include a fully populated **Bill of Materials (BOM)** table and **Title Block** conforming to **IS 15021 / SP 46** drafting conventions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation – "The Sandwich Analogy":**
> Imagine you have **two rotating sticks** (shafts) and you want to attach them end-to-end so they spin together perfectly. You cannot simply glue them (glue melts, screws slip). So, you take **two thick metal discs** (flanges), hammer one onto each stick, push the two discs face-to-face like a *sandwich*, and then drill matching holes through both discs and bolt them tightly.
> - The **discs** = Flanges (carry torque)
> - The **bolts** = Glue (holds them together against twisting force)
> - The **key** = Safety pin (prevents the disc from slipping on the stick)
> - The **Bill of Materials** = The "shopping list" — listing every part, its size, quantity, and material, so the workshop knows exactly what to manufacture or purchase.

The **CAD Model** is essentially the *engineering blue print* of this sandwich assembly, drawn to exact scale on a computer with measurements that any machinist can use to fabricate the real part.

### 1.3 Standard Design Proportions (Empirical Relationships)

For a shaft of nominal diameter **d**, the standard empirical proportions (as per **Khurmi / Shigley design data books**) used in KTU lab exercises are:

| Component | Standard Empirical Formula | Typical Multiplier of d |
|---|---|---|
| Flange Outer Diameter (D) | $D = 2.5\,d$ to $3.0\,d$ | **2.5d** (commonly used) |
| Flange Thickness (t_f) | $t_f = 0.5\,d$ | **0.5d** |
| Hub Diameter (D_h) | $D_h = 1.5\,d$ to $1.7\,d$ | **1.6d** |
| Hub Length (L_h) | $L_h = 1.5\,d$ to $2.0\,d$ | **1.5d** |
| Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD) | $D_1 = 1.6\,D$ to $1.8\,D$ | **1.7D** |
| Bolt Hole Diameter (d_b) | $d_b = 0.5\,d$ to $0.6\,d$ | **0.5d** |
| Number of Bolts (n) | Empirical | **4, 6, or 8** (function of D) |
| Key Width (w) | $w = d/4$ | **0.25d** |
| Key Depth in Shaft (t) | $t = d/6$ | **0.166d** |
| Key Depth in Hub (t_1) | $t_1 = d/7$ | **0.143d** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Convention:** In the KTU lab, students are typically assigned a **shaft diameter d = 40 mm, 50 mm, or 60 mm**. The entire model is then derived using the above empirical multipliers, with the final dimensions rounded to the nearest standard **mm** value.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the Flanged Coupling

The functioning of a flanged coupling can be broken down into **5 sequential engineering logic steps**:

1. **Torque Transmission Path:** The driving shaft receives torque from the prime mover (motor/engine). This torque is transferred to the **key** (a small rectangular steel insert) which is partially embedded in a keyway cut into the shaft.
2. **Key-to-Hub Transfer:** The key slides into a matching keyway machined inside the **hub** of the flange. Thus, torque is transmitted from shaft → key → hub → flange body.
3. **Flange-to-Flange Transfer (Bolt Shear):** The two flanges are pressed together by **tightened bolts**. Torque is transferred from one flange to the other through the **shearing action of the bolts**, which sit on the Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD).
4. **Axial Clamping Force:** The bolts must be tightened to generate a high **clamping (preload) force** to prevent the flange faces from slipping against each other (friction grip).
5. **Coupling to Driven Shaft:** The second flange is keyed to the driven shaft, completing the torque transmission path.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Proportion

- **Why D = 2.5d?** → A larger flange diameter provides a greater **PCD (Pitch Circle Diameter)**, which increases the **moment arm** for bolt clamping. Larger PCD = Lower bolt stress for the same torque.
- **Why t_f = 0.5d?** → Sufficient thickness to prevent **flange warping or bending** under bolt preload, but not so thick as to add unnecessary weight/inertia.
- **Why D_h = 1.6d?** → Hub must be thick enough to accommodate the **keyway** and provide adequate **hub shear strength** against torsional failure.
- **Why PCD = 1.7D?** → Positioned so bolts lie in the **strong outer annulus** of the flange (between hub and rim) for maximum structural benefit.
- **Why d_b = 0.5d?** → Bolts must be strong enough to resist the shear stress caused by transmitted torque, yet small enough to fit within the available flange area.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA BANK** for the Flanged Coupling design and drawing experiment. Memorize this for the University Lab Exam viva and written part.

| S.No. | Parameter | Symbol | Empirical Formula | Unit | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shaft Diameter (given) | $d$ | — | mm | Reference dimension |
| 2 | Flange Outer Diameter | $D$ | $D = 2.5\,d$ | mm | Outer boundary of flange disc |
| 3 | Flange Thickness | $t_f$ | $t_f = 0.5\,d$ | mm | Thickness of flange plate |
| 4 | Hub Diameter | $D_h$ | $D_h = 1.6\,d$ | mm | Diameter of boss fitted on shaft |
| 5 | Hub Length | $L_h$ | $L_h = 1.5\,d$ | mm | Length of hub along shaft axis |
| 6 | Pitch Circle Diameter | $D_1$ | $D_1 = 1.7\,D$ | mm | Circle on which bolt centers lie |
| 7 | Bolt Hole Diameter | $d_b$ | $d_b = 0.5\,d$ | mm | Clearance hole for bolt shank |
| 8 | Number of Bolts | $n$ | 4 (for D ≤ 100), 6 (100 < D ≤ 200) | — | Quantity of fasteners |
| 9 | Key Width | $w$ | $w = d / 4$ | mm | Width of rectangular key |
| 10 | Key Depth (in shaft) | $t$ | $t = d / 6$ | mm | Depth of keyway cut into shaft |
| 11 | Key Depth (in hub) | $t_1$ | $t_1 = d / 7$ | mm | Depth of keyway cut into hub |
| 12 | Key Total Height | $h$ | $h = t + t_1 = \dfrac{13d}{42}$ | mm | Overall key stock height |
| 13 | Chamfer on Flange Edge | $c$ | $c = 0.05\,D$ to $0.1\,D$ | mm | Edge break (typically 2–5 mm) |
| 14 | Fillet Radius at Hub-Flange Junction | $r$ | $r = 0.05\,D$ | mm | Stress relief fillet |
| 15 | Fillet Radius at Shaft-Hub Junction | $r_1$ | $r_1 = 0.02\,d$ | mm | Stress relief fillet |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Flanged Couplings:**
> - **Pumps and Compressors:** Connecting motor shaft to pump impeller shaft.
> - **Marine Propulsion Shafts:** Large-scale flanged couplings join shaft sections in ship propeller lines.
> - **Steel Rolling Mills:** Heavy-duty flanged couplings handle massive torques in rolling stand drives.
> - **Power Generation:** Turbo-generator couplings.
> - **Machine Tool Spindles:** Connecting spindle to gearbox output.
> - **Pipeline Flanges:** The same flange-and-bolt principle is used universally to join pipe sections (ASME B16.5 / B16.47).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Standard Worked Example: d = 50 mm Shaft

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Evaluation:** Given a shaft diameter **d = 50 mm**, derive all standard dimensions of the flanged coupling using the empirical formulas. **This is the most common KTU 2024 Scheme lab assignment.**

#### Step 1: Given Data

$$
d = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

#### Step 2: Flange Outer Diameter (D)

$$
D = 2.5 \times d
$$

$$
D = 2.5 \times 50
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 125 \text{ mm}}
$$

#### Step 3: Flange Thickness (t_f)

$$
t_f = 0.5 \times d
$$

$$
t_f = 0.5 \times 50
$$

$$
\boxed{t_f = 25 \text{ mm}}
$$

#### Step 4: Hub Diameter (D_h)

$$
D_h = 1.6 \times d
$$

$$
D_h = 1.6 \times 50
$$

$$
\boxed{D_h = 80 \text{ mm}}
$$

#### Step 5: Hub Length (L_h)

$$
L_h = 1.5 \times d
$$

$$
L_h = 1.5 \times 50
$$

$$
\boxed{L_h = 75 \text{ mm}}
$$

#### Step 6: Pitch Circle Diameter (D_1)

$$
D_1 = 1.7 \times D
$$

$$
D_1 = 1.7 \times 125
$$

$$
\boxed{D_1 = 212.5 \text{ mm} \approx 213 \text{ mm}}
$$

#### Step 7: Bolt Hole Diameter (d_b)

$$
d_b = 0.5 \times d
$$

$$
d_b = 0.5 \times 50
$$

$$
\boxed{d_b = 25 \text{ mm}}
$$

#### Step 8: Number of Bolts (n)

$$
\text{Since } D = 125 \text{ mm (between 100 and 200 mm)}, \quad n = 6
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Logic:** The number of bolts is chosen based on empirical charts in design data books (Khurmi Table). For small flanges (D ≤ 100 mm), use 4 bolts. For medium flanges (100 < D ≤ 200 mm), use 6 bolts. For large flanges (D > 200 mm), use 8 bolts.

$$
\boxed{n = 6 \text{ bolts}}
$$

#### Step 9: Standard Bolt Size Selection

Since the **bolt clearance hole** $d_b = 25$ mm, the standard bolt (from IS 1363 / ISO 4014) is **M24** (nominal diameter 24 mm, clearance hole ≈ 26 mm). The **PCD (D_1) must be re-checked** with this nominal bolt size.

#### Step 10: Key Dimensions (Width w, Depth t, Depth t_1)

$$
w = \frac{d}{4} = \frac{50}{4} = 12.5 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{w = 12 \text{ mm (standard)}}
$$

$$
t = \frac{d}{6} = \frac{50}{6} = 8.33 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{t = 8 \text{ mm (standard)}}
$$

$$
t_1 = \frac{d}{7} = \frac{50}{7} = 7.14 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{t_1 = 7 \text{ mm (standard)}}
$$

#### Step 11: Key Total Height (h)

$$
h = t + t_1 = 8 + 7 = 15 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{h = 15 \text{ mm}}
$$

#### Step 12: Chamfer and Fillet Radii

$$
c = 0.05 \times D = 0.05 \times 125 = 6.25 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{c = 6 \text{ mm}}
$$

$$
r = 0.05 \times D = 0.05 \times 125 = 6.25 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{r = 6 \text{ mm}}
$$

$$
r_1 = 0.02 \times d = 0.02 \times 50 = 1 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{r_1 = 1 \text{ mm}}
$$

### 3.2 Final Consolidated Dimension Table (Analytical Matrix)

| S.No. | Parameter | Calculated Value | Rounded Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shaft diameter, $d$ | 50 mm | **50 mm** |
| 2 | Flange outer diameter, $D$ | 125 mm | **125 mm** |
| 3 | Flange thickness, $t_f$ | 25 mm | **25 mm** |
| 4 | Hub diameter, $D_h$ | 80 mm | **80 mm** |
| 5 | Hub length, $L_h$ | 75 mm | **75 mm** |
| 6 | Pitch circle diameter, $D_1$ | 212.5 mm | **213 mm** |
| 7 | Bolt hole diameter, $d_b$ | 25 mm | **26 mm (M24)** |
| 8 | Number of bolts, $n$ | — | **6** |
| 9 | Key width, $w$ | 12.5 mm | **12 mm** |
| 10 | Key depth in shaft, $t$ | 8.33 mm | **8 mm** |
| 11 | Key depth in hub, $t_1$ | 7.14 mm | **7 mm** |
| 12 | Key height, $h$ | 15 mm | **15 mm** |
| 13 | Chamfer, $c$ | 6.25 mm | **6 mm × 45°** |
| 14 | Fillet radius, $r$ | 6.25 mm | **6 mm** |
| 15 | Shaft-hub fillet, $r_1$ | 1 mm | **1 mm** |

### 3.3 Bill of Materials (BOM) Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Standard:** The BOM must be drawn as a table in the **top-right corner** of the drawing sheet (or bottom-right as per IS 15021). Each row lists one unique part, with **item number, quantity, part name, part number, material, and remarks**.

| ITEM | QTY | PART NAME | PART NO. | MATERIAL | REMARKS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | Flange | F-50 | C45 Steel (EN8) | As per IS 6661 |
| 2 | 2 | Shaft | S-50 | EN8 / EN24 | Driven and Driving |
| 3 | 6 | Hex Bolt M24 × L | B-M24 | 8.8 Grade HT Steel | Length = t_f + 5 mm |
| 4 | 6 | Hex Nut M24 | N-M24 | 8.8 Grade HT Steel | As per IS 1363 |
| 5 | 6 | Plain Washer M24 | W-M24 | Mild Steel | OD = 45 mm, ID = 25 mm |
| 6 | 2 | Parallel Key 12 × 8 × 7 × L | K-12 | C45 Steel | As per IS 2292 |
| 7 | 2 | Set Screw M8 × 10 | SS-M8 | Mild Steel | For key locking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolt Length Calculation:** The bolt must pass through both flanges. Length = 2 × t_f + nut thickness + washer thickness + 2 mm clearance.
> For our case: L = 2(25) + 19.6 + 3 + 2 = 74.6 mm → Standard bolt length = **75 mm (M24 × 75)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — AutoCAD Drawing Procedure

The following block-level functional architecture flow maps the **complete sequence of AutoCAD commands** required to generate the Flanged Coupling assembly drawing. This is a critical KTU lab exam question and is asked frequently in viva voce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Open AutoCAD New Drawing] --> B[Set Units: Decimal, mm, Precision 0.00]
```
