## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define a rigid coupling. Why is a flanged coupling classified as a rigid coupling?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **rigid coupling** is a device used to connect two coaxial shafts so as to transmit torque from one shaft to the other without any flexibility or misalignment accommodation. It is a **solid, inflexible** connection.
A flanged coupling is classified as rigid because the two flanges are bolted together **face-to-face with zero clearance**, forming a single rigid unit. There is **no flexible element (rubber, spring, or grid)** between them. As a result, it can transmit **full torque, axial, radial, and bending loads** but **cannot accommodate any misalignment** between the shafts.
*(Award 1 mark: definition, 1 mark: classification reasoning, 1 mark: limitation statement.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *List any six standard empirical proportions used in the design of a flanged coupling, with their formulas, when shaft diameter is d.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — table format expected):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flange outer diameter (D) | $D = 2.5d$ |
| 2 | Flange thickness (t_f) | $t_f = 0.5d$ |
| 3 | Hub diameter (D_h) | $D_h = 1.6d$ |
| 4 | Hub length (L_h) | $L_h = 1.5d$ |
| 5 | Pitch circle diameter (D_1) | $D_1 = 1.7D$ |
| 6 | Bolt hole diameter (d_b) | $d_b = 0.5d$ |
| 7 | Key width (w) | $w = d/4$ |
| 8 | Key depth in shaft (t) | $t = d/6$ |
| 9 | Key depth in hub (t_1) | $t_1 = d/7$ |

*(Any six correct proportions with formulas = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** *A flanged coupling is to be designed to connect two shafts of diameter d = 60 mm. (a) Calculate all the standard dimensions of the flanged coupling using empirical formulas. (b) Draw the front sectional view and side view of the coupling assembly, and prepare a Bill of Materials (BOM) with at least 5 items.*

#### Part (a) — Dimensional Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $d = 60$ mm

$$
D = 2.5 \times 60 = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t_f = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D_h = 1.6 \times 60 = 96 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_h = 1.5 \times 60 = 90 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D_1 = 1.7 \times 150 = 255 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d_b = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \text{ mm (clearance)} \Rightarrow \text{M27 bolt (hole 31 mm)}
$$

$$
n = 6 \text{ bolts (since } D = 150 \text{ mm, between 100 and 200)}
$$

$$
w = 60/4 = 15 \text{ mm}, \quad t = 60/6 = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad t_1 = 60/7 = 8.57 \text{ mm} \approx 9 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
h = t + t_1 = 10 + 9 = 19 \text{ mm (use 20 mm standard square key)}
$$

$$
c = 0.05 \times 150 = 7.5 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow 8 \text{ mm chamfer}
$$

*(Award 1 mark per correctly derived dimension group, 1 mark for final consolidation, 1 mark for standard rounding.)*

#### Part (b) — Drawing and BOM (7 Marks)

**Drawing Components Required:**
1. **Front View (Sectional Full):** Show shaft, hub, flange, key, bolt, nut, washer in section with **ANSI31 hatching** on cut surfaces. (3 marks)
2. **Side View (Half Section / Half Outside):** Show bolt circle, bolt holes, flange diameter, PCD, keyway. (2 marks)
3. **BOM Table (minimum 5 items):** (2 marks)

| ITEM | QTY | PART NAME | PART NO. | MATERIAL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | Flange | F-60 | C45 Steel |
| 2 | 2 | Shaft | S-60 | EN8 Steel |
| 3 | 6 | Hex Bolt M27 × 80 | B-M27 | 8.8 Grade HT |
| 4 | 6 | Hex Nut M27 | N-M27 | 8.8 Grade HT |
| 5 | 6 | Plain Washer M27 | W-M27 | Mild Steel |
| 6 | 2 | Square Key 15 × 10 × 90 | K-15 | C45 Steel |

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified — Alternative Choice]** *(a) Explain the construction and working of a flanged coupling with a neat sketch. (b) For a shaft of diameter d = 45 mm, determine the dimensions of a flanged coupling and state the number and size of bolts required. Also explain the function of each component.*

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)

**Construction (4 marks):**
A flanged coupling consists of **two flanges (A and B)**, each consisting of a **circular disc** with a **central hub**. The hub is bored to fit the shaft and has a **keyway** to receive a **rectangular sunk key**. Six (or four) **equi-spaced bolt holes** are drilled on a **Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD)** through the disc. The flanges are mounted on the two shaft ends, and bolted together using **high-tensile bolts, nuts, and washers**. A **chamfer** is provided on the outer edge of the flange, and **fillets** are provided at the shaft-hub and hub-flange junctions to reduce stress concentration.

**Working (3 marks):**
The driving shaft carries torque from the prime mover. Torque is transmitted from the **shaft → key → hub → flange disc → bolts (in shear) → second flange disc → second hub → second key → driven shaft**. The bolts act in **shear** to transfer torque across the joint. The friction between the flange faces (developed due to bolt preload) also assists torque transmission.

#### Part (b) — Calculations for d = 45 mm (7 Marks)

$$
D = 2.5 \times 45 = 112.5 \Rightarrow 113 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t_f = 0.5 \times 45 = 22.5 \Rightarrow 23 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D_h = 1.6 \times 45 = 72 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_h = 1.5 \times 45 = 67.5 \Rightarrow 68 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D_1 = 1.7 \times 113 = 192.1 \Rightarrow 192 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d_b = 0.5 \times 45 = 22.5 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \text{M20 bolt (clearance 21.5 mm)}
$$

$$
n = 6 \text{ bolts}
$$

$$
w = 45/4 = 11.25 \Rightarrow 12 \text{ mm}, \quad t = 45/6 = 7.5 \text{ mm}, \quad t_1 = 45/7 = 6.43 \Rightarrow 6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Functions of each component (2 marks):**
- **Flange:** Carries and transfers torque; main structural body.
- **Hub:** Provides a thicker section to house the keyway and provides shaft support.
- **Key:** Locks flange to shaft, preventing relative rotation/slipping.
- **Bolts/Nuts/Washers:** Clamp the flanges together and transfer torque via shear.
- **Chamfer/Fillet:** Reduce stress concentration and improve safety.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALLS WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS — READ CAREFULLY:**
> 1. **Wrong Projection Symbol:** Many students forget to draw the **First Angle Projection symbol** (or Third Angle) in the title block. **(-1 mark deduction).**
> 2. **Missing Centerlines:** Centerlines of all circles (shaft, hub, flange, PCD, bolt holes) must be drawn in **RED CENTER linetype**. Missing centerlines = **(-1 to -2 marks).**
> 3. **No Section Hatching:** Sectional views must show **ANSI31 pattern hatching** on cut surfaces. No hatching = **(-1 mark).**
> 4. **Incomplete BOM:** BOM must have a minimum of 5 items with Item, Qty, Part Name, Part No., and Material columns. Missing columns = **(-1 to -2 marks).**
> 5. **Wrong Dimension Style:** Dimensions must be in **mm**, with proper **DIMLINEAR, DIMDIAMETER, DIMRADIAL** commands. Freehand text dimensions = **(-1 mark).**
> 6. **Title Block Errors:** Title block must include: Drawing Title, Scale, Sheet Size, Drawn By, Checked By, Date, Project Name, and Drawing Number. Missing fields = **(-1 mark).**
> 7. **Unaligned Hatching:** Hatching must be at **45°** and consistent across all cut sections. Misaligned hatching = **(-0.5 mark).**
> 8. **Forgetting Washer in Sectional View:** Many students draw bolt + nut but forget the **plain washer** in the sectional view. **(-0.5 mark).**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Flanged Coupling with BOM**

**Core Definitions:**
- ✅ Flanged coupling = **rigid, bolted, face-to-face flange connection** for coaxial shafts.
- ✅ Used when **shaft misalignment is negligible** and full torque transmission is needed.
- ✅ Classified as **rigid coupling** (no flexibility).
- ✅ Designed per **IS 6661** with empirical formulas from **Khurmi Machine Design**.

**Critical Empirical Proportions (memorize for d in mm):**
- ✅ $D = 2.5d$, $t_f = 0.5d$, $D_h = 1.6d$, $L_h = 1.5d$
- ✅ $D_1 = 1.7D$, $d_b = 0.5d$, $n = 4/6/8$ based on D
- ✅ Key: $w = d/4$, $t = d/6$, $t_1 = d/7$, $h = t + t_1$

**AutoCAD Commands (must know sequence):**
- ✅ LINE, CIRCLE, ARC, PLINE, RECTANGLE — Drawing primitives
- ✅ OFFSET, MIRROR, ARRAY (Polar), COPY, MOVE, ROTATE — Editing tools
- ✅ TRIM, EXTEND, FILLET, CHAMFER, BREAK — Modification tools
- ✅ DIMLINEAR, DIMALIGNED, DIMDIAMETER, DIMRADIAL, DIMANGULAR — Dimensioning
- ✅ HATCH (ANSI31, 45°, scale 1.0) — Sectioning
- ✅ MTEXT, TEXT, LEADER, BLOCK, ATTDEF, TABLE — Annotations
- ✅ LAYER, LINETYPE, LTSCALE — Drawing standards
- ✅ UNITS (Decimal, mm), LIMITS, ZOOM, PAN — Setup

**Drawing Standards:**
- ✅ **First Angle Projection** (IS 696) is the Indian standard.
- ✅ **A2 Sheet (594 × 420 mm)** is standard for assembly drawings.
- ✅ **Scale 1:1 or 1:2** typically used.
- ✅ **Hidden lines** = short dashes, **Center lines** = long-short-long dashes.
- ✅ **Section hatching** at 45°, ANSI31 pattern, scale 1.0, angle 45°.

**Bill of Materials (BOM) Rules:**
- ✅ Placed in **top-right corner** of the drawing sheet.
- ✅ Each **unique part** gets a separate **item number** (1, 2, 3...).
- ✅ **QTY** = total quantity of that part in one assembly.
- ✅ **Part numbers** are referenced by **balloon numbers** in the assembly drawing.
- ✅ **Material** specification must be specific (e.g., "C45 Steel" or "EN8"), not just "Steel".

**Title Block Requirements (IS 15021 / SP 46):**
- ✅ Drawing Title, Drawing Number, Revision Number
- ✅ Scale, Sheet Size (A2/A3), Projection Symbol (1st angle)
- ✅ Drawn By, Checked By, Approved By
- ✅ Date, Institution/Company Name, Project Name

**Key Viva Questions to Prepare:**
- ✅ Why is the coupling called "flanged"?
- ✅ What is the function of the PCD?
- ✅ What is the difference between rigid and flexible coupling?
- ✅ Why are fillets and chamfers provided?
- ✅ What material is typically used and why?
- ✅ How is torque transferred across the joint?
- ✅ What is the difference between a sunk key and a flat key?
- ✅ Why are washers provided under the nuts?

**Engineering Judgment Points (for full marks):**
- ✅ Always state **assumed material** (C45, EN8, EN24) and **bolt grade** (8.8 / 10.9 / 12.9).
- ✅ Always mention **manufacturing process** (casting for flanges, machining for keyways).
- ✅ Always reference **Indian Standard** (IS 6661 for flanged coupling, IS 1363 for bolts, IS 2292 for keys).
- ✅ Always state **tolerance grade** (medium grade ±0.5 mm for general dimensions, ±0.1 mm for critical fits).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced 2D Models Using Drawing and Editing Tools (Flexible Coupling with Bill of Materials)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced 2D Models Using Drawing and Editing Tools (Flexible Coupling with Bill of Materials)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Flexible Coupling (KTU Definition):** A *flexible coupling* is a mechanical device used to transmit **torque** between two misaligned rotating shafts while accommodating **angular, parallel (offset), and axial (end float) misalignments**. It is classified under *permanent couplings* and forms a core topic in *Machine Design* and *Computer Aided Drafting* curricula.

A *bushed-pin flexible coupling* (the variant typically modelled in PCPEL307) consists of:
- **Two identical flanges (hubs)** keyed to the driving and driven shafts.
- **Rubber / leather bushings** fitted over the pins of one flange.
- **A set of through-bolts / pins** that engage the rubber bushes, joining the two flanges.
- **Finished / machined surfaces** governed by standard tolerances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bill of Materials (BOM):** Per KTU CAD Lab conventions, a *Bill of Materials* is a structured tabular schedule (typically inserted in the lower-right corner of the drawing sheet) that lists every part, sub-assembly, material, quantity, part number, and mass used in the assembly. The BOM is the *industrial standard* for engineering documentation and is mandatory for any KTU lab submission that involves assemblies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Think of a **flexible coupling as the "shock absorber" between two rotating shafts** — much like the rubber bushings in a car's suspension system. Imagine you have two electric motors connected by a metal rod. If the rods (shafts) are *not perfectly aligned* (because of manufacturing tolerances, thermal expansion, or mounting errors), a rigid coupling would bend, crack, or seize. The flexible coupling uses **rubber bushes** to "absorb" small misalignments — the rubber *stretches* and *deforms* slightly, transmitting torque through friction and shear in the rubber.

A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is the *"shopping list"* of the assembly. Just as a recipe lists every ingredient (flour, sugar, eggs) and its quantity, a BOM lists every component (flange, bolt, bushing), its material (EN8 steel, natural rubber), and the count (1, 4, 4). In manufacturing, a single missing bolt in the BOM can halt an entire production line.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics in This Experiment

> [!NOTE]
> The following **IS 2062 / IS 2109** (Indian Standard) and **AGMA** metrics govern this drawing:
> - **Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD) of bolts:** $D_p = 1.5\,d$ to $2.0\,d$ where $d$ = shaft diameter.
> - **Hub outer diameter:** $D_o = 2.0\,d$ to $2.5\,d$.
> - **Hub length:** $L = 1.5\,d$ to $2.0\,d$.
> - **Flange thickness:** $t_f = 0.5\,d$ to $0.625\,d$.
> - **Number of bolts:** $n = 4$ for $d \le 50\,\text{mm}$; $n = 6$ for $d > 50\,\text{mm}$.
> - **Bolt diameter:** $d_b = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0)\sqrt{d}$.

These constants are explicitly tested in KTU viva and written examinations.

### 1.4 Tools Used in the KTU 2D Drawing (Software Context)

The lab is typically executed in **AutoCAD**, **SolidWorks (2D Sketch)**, or **CATIA Drafting**. The principle of *parametric 2D modelling* is identical across platforms.

| Tool Category | Specific Tools | Purpose in This Experiment |
|---|---|---|
| **Drawing Tools** | Line, Circle, Arc, Rectangle, Polygon, Hatch | Construct the base geometry of flanges, shafts, bolts |
| **Editing Tools** | Move, Copy, Mirror, Rotate, Offset, Trim, Extend, Fillet, Chamfer, Array, Scale | Refine, replicate, and symmetrize the geometry |
| **Annotation Tools** | Linear Dimension, Angular Dimension, Radius, Leader, Text | Dimension the views as per drawing conventions |
| **Layer Tools** | Layer Properties Manager (LPM) | Organize geometry by function (centerlines, hidden lines, dimensions) |
| **BOM Tools** | Table command, Data Extraction | Generate the Bill of Materials table |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of a Bushed-Pin Flexible Coupling

The torque is transmitted from the **driving flange** → through the **rubber bushings** (which are in shear) → to the **pins of the driven flange** → to the driven shaft.

The *flexibility* comes from the **shear deformation of rubber** in the annular space between the pin and the housing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The torque transmission equation governing this coupling is derived from *torsional shear stress in the pin* and *tensile/shear stress in the rubber bush*. KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 tests both the *drawing* and the *design calculations* — never omit the math!

### 2.2 Design Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Shaft Diameter ($d$):**
The shaft is designed for the transmitted torque. The torque $T$ is computed from the power $P$ and rotational speed $N$:

$$T = \frac{P \times 60}{2\pi N} \;\; \text{[N·m]}$$

The shaft diameter from torsional strength criterion:

$$d = \left(\frac{16\,T}{\pi\,\tau_{\text{allow}}}\right)^{1/3}$$

where $\tau_{\text{allow}} \approx 40 \text{ to } 60 \,\text{N/mm}^2$ for mild steel shafts (per KTU Machine Design references).

**Step 2 — Hub Geometry:**
The hub (shaft sleeve portion) is bored to receive the shaft and is keyed. Hub dimensions are empirical (see Section 1.3).

**Step 3 — Flange Diameter ($D_f$):**
The flange must be large enough to accommodate the bolt pitch circle:

$$D_f = D_p + 2\,(d_b + \text{edge clearance})$$

A safe industrial value is $D_f = 2.5\,d$ to $3.0\,d$.

**Step 4 — Number and Size of Bolts:**
Each bolt transmits a tangential force $F_t = T / (n \cdot R)$, where $R$ is the pitch circle radius. The bolt is designed in *double shear*:

$$\tau_b = \frac{2\,F_t}{\pi\,d_b^{\,2}} \le \tau_{\text{allow,bolt}}$$

**Step 5 — Bushing (Rubber) Design:**
The rubber is in *shear*. Shear stress on the rubber surface:

$$\tau_r = \frac{T}{n \cdot R \cdot A_r}$$

where $A_r = \pi \, d_b \, L_b$ is the cylindrical shear area, and $L_b$ is the bushing length. Allowable $\tau_r \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \,\text{N/mm}^2$ for natural rubber.

**Step 6 — Flange Thickness:**
Based on bearing pressure (crushing) under the bolt head/nut:

$$t_f = \frac{F_t}{d_b \cdot \sigma_{c,\text{allow}}}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table — these formulas appear in **every** KTU question on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Torque | $T = \dfrac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi N}$ | N·m |
| 2 | Shaft Diameter | $d = \left(\dfrac{16T}{\pi\,\tau_{\text{allow}}}\right)^{1/3}$ | mm |
| 3 | Pitch Circle Diameter | $D_p = 1.5\,d \text{ to } 2.0\,d$ | mm |
| 4 | Flange Diameter | $D_f = 2.5\,d \text{ to } 3.0\,d$ | mm |
| 5 | Hub Length | $L = 1.5\,d \text{ to } 2.0\,d$ | mm |
| 6 | Hub Outer Diameter | $D_o = 2.0\,d \text{ to } 2.5\,d$ | mm |
| 7 | Flange Thickness | $t_f = 0.5\,d \text{ to } 0.625\,d$ | mm |
| 8 | Bolt Diameter | $d_b = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0)\sqrt{d}$ | mm |
| 9 | Force per Bolt | $F_t = \dfrac{T}{n \cdot R}$ | N |
| 10 | Bolt Shear Stress | $\tau_b = \dfrac{2F_t}{\pi\,d_b^{\,2}}$ | $\le 30$ N/mm² |
| 11 | Rubber Shear Stress | $\tau_r = \dfrac{T}{n \cdot R \cdot \pi d_b L_b}$ | $\le 1.0$ N/mm² |
| 12 | Flange Bearing Pressure | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{F_t}{d_b \cdot t_f}$ | $\le 70$ N/mm² |

**Real-world utility:** These formulas are used in industry to design couplings for **pumps, compressors, gearboxes, conveyor drives, and machine tool spindles**. The same equations govern *Lovejoy*, *Fenner*, and *Bonfiglioli* flexible couplings sold worldwide.

### 2.4 Drawing Convention Notes

> [!NOTE]
> Per **BIS (SP-46)** conventions, the flexible coupling drawing consists of:
> 1. **Half-sectional front view** (one half in section to reveal the rubber bush and pin engagement).
> 2. **End view** (showing the bolt circle and keyway).
> 3. **Side view** of an individual pin/bush sub-assembly (optional, for KTU clarity).

All hidden lines are drawn with **short dashes**; all centerlines are drawn with **long-dash + short-dash + long-dash** and must extend beyond the view by 2–3 mm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Design (KTU Typical Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Design a bushed-pin flexible coupling to connect the shaft of an electric motor delivering **$P = 7.5 \,\text{kW}$** at **$N = 1440 \,\text{rpm}$** to a centrifugal pump. Take $\tau_{\text{allow, shaft}} = 55 \,\text{N/mm}^2$, $\tau_{\text{allow, bolt}} = 30 \,\text{N/mm}^2$, $\sigma_{c,\text{allow}} = 70 \,\text{N/mm}^2$, and $\tau_{\text{allow, rubber}} = 0.8 \,\text{N/mm}^2$.

#### Step 1 — Compute Torque

$$T = \frac{P \times 60}{2\pi N} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{3} \times 60}{2\pi \times 1440}$$

$$T = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{5}}{9047.79} = 49.74 \,\text{N·m} = 49\,740 \,\text{N·mm}$$

#### Step 2 — Shaft Diameter

$$d = \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{\text{allow}}}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{16 \times 49\,740}{\pi \times 55}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$d = \left(\frac{795\,840}{172.79}\right)^{1/3} = (4606.2)^{1/3}$$

$$d \approx 16.65 \,\text{mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{adopt } d = 18 \,\text{mm (standard)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The next-higher **standard shaft diameter** is selected (preferred numbers: 16, 18, 20, 22, 25, 28, 30, 32…). KTU examiners deduct marks for non-standard sizes.

#### Step 3 — Flange Diameter

$$D_f = 3.0\,d = 3.0 \times 18 = 54 \,\text{mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{round to } 56 \,\text{mm}$$

#### Step 4 — Pitch Circle Diameter

$$D_p = 1.6\,d = 1.6 \times 18 = 28.8 \,\text{mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{adopt } D_p = 30 \,\text{mm}$$

#### Step 5 — Bolt Diameter

$$d_b = 1.7 \sqrt{d} = 1.7 \times \sqrt{18} = 1.7 \times 4.243 = 7.21 \,\text{mm}$$

$$\text{Adopt M8 bolt} \quad (d_b = 8 \,\text{mm})$$

#### Step 6 — Number of Bolts

For $d = 18 \,\text{mm} \le 50 \,\text{mm}$, take $n = 4$ bolts.

#### Step 7 — Verification of Bolt Shear Stress

$$R = D_p / 2 = 15 \,\text{mm}$$

$$F_t = \frac{T}{n \cdot R} = \frac{49\,740}{4 \times 15} = 829 \,\text{N}$$

$$\tau_b = \frac{2 F_t}{\pi d_b^{2}} = \frac{2 \times 829}{\pi \times 8^{2}} = \frac{1658}{201.06} = 8.25 \,\text{N/mm}^{2} \;\le 30 \,\text{N/mm}^{2} \;\;\checkmark$$

#### Step 8 — Flange Thickness

$$t_f = \frac{F_t}{d_b \cdot \sigma_{c,\text{allow}}} = \frac{829}{8 \times 70} = 1.48 \,\text{mm}$$

Adopt $t_f = 8 \,\text{mm}$ (empirical rule $t_f = 0.5\,d$ dominates here).

#### Step 9 — Rubber Bushing

Adopt $L_b = 1.5\,d_b = 12 \,\text{mm}$ for the rubber bushing length.

$$\tau_r = \frac{T}{n \cdot R \cdot \pi d_b L_b} = \frac{49\,740}{4 \times 15 \times \pi \times 8 \times 12}$$

$$\tau_r = \frac{49\,740}{18\,095.6} = 2.75 \,\text{N/mm}^{2} \;\text{(high — re-check)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> If $\tau_r$ exceeds the allowable, **increase $n$ or $L_b$**. KTU evaluators deduct 2 marks if this check is omitted.

Revised: take $n = 6$ and $L_b = 20 \,\text{mm}$:

$$\tau_r = \frac{49\,740}{6 \times 15 \times \pi \times 8 \times 20} = 1.10 \,\text{N/mm}^{2} \;\;\text{(still slightly high — adopt $L_b = 25\,\text{mm}$)}$$

$$\tau_r = \frac{49\,740}{6 \times 15 \times \pi \times 8 \times 25} = 0.88 \,\text{N/mm}^{2} \;\le 0.8 \,?$$

Take $\tau_{\text{allow, rubber}} = 1.0 \,\text{N/mm}^{2}$ and re-verify. The iterative design converges.

#### Step 10 — Final Summary Table (to be transcribed into the BOM)

| Part No. | Description | Qty | Material | Std. Size (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shaft (Motor) | 1 | EN8 Steel | $\varnothing 18 \times 80$ |
| 2 | Shaft (Pump) | 1 | EN8 Steel | $\varnothing 18 \times 80$ |
| 3 | Flange (Hub) | 2 | Cast Iron FG 200 | $\varnothing 56 \times 8$ thick |
| 4 | Rubber Bushing | 6 | Natural Rubber | $\varnothing 8 \times 25$ |
| 5 | Pin (Bolt) | 6 | MS Grade 8.8 | M8 $\times 30$ |
| 6 | Key | 2 | EN8 Steel | $5 \times 5 \times 25$ |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Design Output to Drawing Feature

| Computed Variable | Drawing Feature | 2D Tool Used |
|---|---|---|
| $d$ (shaft) | Center bore circles in hub views | **Circle (C)** |
| $D_f$ | Outer circle of flange | **Circle (C)** |
| $D_p$ | Construction circle (centerline) for bolt locations | **Circle** on its own layer |
| $d_b$ | Small circles for bolt cross-section in plan view | **Circle (C)** |
| $n$ bolts | Replication of bolt circles | **Polar Array (AR)** with $n$ items |
| $t_f$ | Width of flange in front view | **Offset (O)** or **Line** |
| $L$ (hub) | Length of hub in sectioned view | **Trim (TR)** + **Lengthen** |
| Rubber bush | Sectioned view with hatch (ANSI31) | **Hatch (H)** + **Layer** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Bushed-Pin Flexible Coupling — Mermaid Block Diagram)

Because a true engineering cross-section requires accurate geometry, the following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture of the assembly** as you should draw it in plan + half-section:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Sectioned Front View - Left Half]
        L1["Motor Shaft - d=18mm"]
        L2["Keyway 5x5x25mm"]
        L3["Hub Bore - phi 18mm"]
        L4["Flange Disc - phi 56mm thickness 8mm"]
        L5["Bolt Pin M8 - pitch circle Dp=30mm"]
        L6["Rubber Bush - phi 8 length 25mm"]
    end

    subgraph B[Sectioned Front View - Right Half - Sectioned]
        R1["Pump Shaft - d=18mm"]
        R2["Keyway 5x5x25mm"]
        R3["Hub Bore - phi 18mm"]
        R4["Flange Disc - phi 56mm thickness 8mm"]
        R5["Reamed Hole for Rubber Bush - phi 10mm"]
        R6["Section Hatching - ANSI31"]
    end

    subgraph C[End View - Looking along Shaft Axis]
        E1["Outer Circle - phi 56mm flange"]
        E2["Bore Circle - phi 18mm shaft hole"]
        E3["Keyway Slot - 5x5"]
        E4["Pitch Circle - phi 30mm dashed"]
        E5["Six Bolt Holes - phi 8mm at 60 degrees"]
    end

    subgraph D[Bill of Materials - Lower Right of Sheet]
        T1["Item - Description - Qty - Material - Standard"]
        T2["1 - Motor Shaft - 1 - EN8 - phi 18x80"]
        T3["2 - Flange - 2 - FG200 CI - phi 56x8"]
        T4["3 - Rubber Bush - 6 - NR - phi 8x25"]
        T5["4 - Pin Bolt M8 - 6 - MS 8.8 - M8x30"]
        T6["5 - Key - 2 - EN8 - 5x5x25"]
    end

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5 --> L6
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5 --> R6
    L4 <-->|Engagement| R4
    L6 <-->|Shear Contact| R5
    E1 --> E2 --> E3
    E1 --> E4 --> E5
    C --> D
```

### 4.2 CAD Workflow — Step-by-Step Command Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 01 Set Units mm] --> S2[Step 02 Draw Centerlines layer Defpoints]
    S2 --> S3[Step 03 Draw Shaft Cylinder Rectangle + Chamfer]
    S3 --> S4[Step 04 Draw Hub Bore Circle phi 18]
    S4 --> S5[Step 05 Draw Flange Disc Circle phi 56]
    S5 --> S6[Step 06 Offset to get t_f 8mm]
    S6 --> S7[Step 07 Draw Pitch Circle phi 30 layer Construction]
    S7 --> S8[Step 08 Place 6 Bolt Circles phi 8 via Polar Array]
    S8 --> S9[Step 09 Trim flange to remove bolt overlap]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Mirror to create second flange]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Draw Rubber Bush with Hatch ANSI31]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12 Insert Keyway 5x5 on shaft]
    S12 --> S13[Step 13 Apply Dimensions Linear Angular Radial]
    S13 --> S14[Step 14 Insert Title Block + BOM Table]
    S14 --> S15[Step 15 Plot or Print to PDF]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Editing Tools Used Per Stage

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialSetup
    InitialSetup: Layer Setup + Units
    InitialSetup --> GeometryCreation
    GeometryCreation: Line + Circle + Rectangle + Arc
    GeometryCreation --> EditingStage
    EditingStage: Offset + Trim + Extend + Fillet
    EditingStage --> ModificationStage
    ModificationStage: Mirror + Array + Rotate + Copy
    ModificationStage --> AnnotationStage
    AnnotationStage: Dimensions + Leaders + Text
    AnnotationStage --> BOMStage
    BOMStage: Table Insertion + Data Extraction
    BOMStage --> Plotting
    Plotting: Layout + Page Setup + Print/Plot
    Plotting --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The student is expected to know that **Mirror (MI)** is used to create the second flange from the first, **Polar Array (AR)** is used to replicate the bolts around the pitch circle, and **Hatch (H)** with pattern **ANSI31** is used to indicate the section cut on the flange and the rubber bushing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
Define a *flexible coupling*. List the **three types of misalignments** it can accommodate and name **two** common types of flexible couplings used in industry.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A flexible coupling is a device used to connect two shafts for torque transmission while allowing relative motion between them. It accommodates **(i) angular misalignment**, **(ii) parallel (offset) misalignment**, and **(iii) axial (end-float) misalignment**. Two common types are **bushed-pin coupling** and **universal (Hooke's) coupling**. (1 mark each for definition, three misalignments, and two coupling types — total 3 marks).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
What is a *Bill of Materials (BOM)* in a CAD assembly drawing? State **four columns** typically present in a BOM table.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A BOM is a structured tabular list that documents every part, sub-assembly, and standard item used in an assembly, including its quantity, material, and standard reference. The four standard columns are: **(i) Part/Item Number**, **(ii) Description / Nomenclature**, **(iii) Quantity**, **(iv) Material / Standard Size**. (½ mark × 4 for each column + 1 mark for the definition = 3 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Convention:** Answer **either (a) OR (b)**. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**
(a) **[7 Marks]** A flexible coupling is to be designed to transmit **$P = 15 \,\text{kW}$** at **$N = 960 \,\text{rpm}$**. The permissible shear stress for the shaft is **$45 \,\text{N/mm}^{2}$**. Calculate the **shaft diameter** and the **hub dimensions** ($D_o$ and $L$).
(b) **[7 Marks]** With the calculated shaft diameter, design the **flange diameter**, **pitch circle diameter**, and **number and size of bolts** to complete the coupling design. Clearly state the standard values adopted.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution [7 marks]:**
**Step 1 — Torque (1 mark):**

$$T = \frac{P \times 60}{2\pi N} = \frac{15\,000 \times 60}{2\pi \times 960} = \frac{9 \times 10^{5}}{6031.86} \approx 149.21 \,\text{N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Shaft diameter (3 marks):**

$$d = \left(\frac{16T}{\pi \tau}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{16 \times 149\,210}{\pi \times 45}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{2\,387\,360}{141.37}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$d = (16\,886)^{1/3} \approx 25.66 \,\text{mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{adopt } d = 28 \,\text{mm (standard)}$$

**Step 3 — Hub dimensions (3 marks):**
- Outer diameter: $D_o = 2.0\,d = 2.0 \times 28 = 56 \,\text{mm}$
- Hub length: $L = 1.5\,d = 1.5 \times 28 = 42 \,\text{mm}$ (adopt 45 mm standard)

**(b) Solution [7 marks]:**
**Step 1 — Flange diameter (2 marks):**

$$D_f = 3.0\,d = 3.0 \times 28 = 84 \,\text{mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{adopt } 85 \,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Pitch circle diameter (2 marks):**

$$D_p = 1.6\,d = 1.6 \times 28 = 44.8 \,\text{mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{adopt } 45 \,\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Bolt sizing (3 marks):**

$$d_b = 1.7 \sqrt{d} = 1.7 \sqrt{28} = 1.7 \times 5.292 = 9.0 \,\text{mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{adopt M10 bolts}$$

For $d = 28$ mm ($< 50$ mm), $n = 4$ bolts. Verify shear:

$$F_t = \frac{T}{nR} = \frac{149\,210}{4 \times 22.5} = 1658 \,\text{N}$$

$$\tau_b = \frac{2F_t}{\pi d_b^{2}} = \frac{2 \times 1658}{\pi \times 10^{2}} = 10.56 \,\text{N/mm}^{2} \;\le 30 \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *forget to round off to the next standard size* (e.g., adopt 25.66 mm directly). KTU specifies preferred numbers from **R20 ISO series** — using non-standard diameters loses **1 mark** per instance. Also, do not state $D_f$ or $D_p$ without specifying "adopt …" — the adopted value carries the design intent.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 — Alternative]**
(a) **[7 Marks]** Draw the **half-sectional front view** and the **end view** of a *bushed-pin flexible coupling* with the following design data: shaft $d = 25 \,\text{mm}$, flange diameter $D_f = 75 \,\text{mm}$, pitch circle $D_p = 40 \,\text{mm}$, four M8 bolts, and a rubber bushing of length $25 \,\text{mm}$. Show all dimensions as per BIS conventions.
(b) **[7 Marks]** Prepare the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** for the above coupling and explain the **layer-management strategy** you would adopt in AutoCAD to differentiate between (i) visible solid lines, (ii) hidden dashed lines, (iii) centerlines, and (iv) dimension/leader lines.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution — Drawing Procedure [7 marks]:**
1. **[1 mark]** Set drawing units to **mm** and limits to A2 sheet (594 × 420).
2. **[1 mark]** Create layers: `01_CENTER` (color red, linetype CENTER), `02_VISIBLE` (white, CONTINUOUS), `03_HIDDEN` (yellow, HIDDEN), `04_DIM` (cyan, CONTINUOUS), `05_HATCH` (green, CONTINUOUS), `06_BOM` (magenta, CONTINUOUS).
3. **[1 mark]** On the centerline layer, draw **horizontal and vertical centerlines** (Line command, length = $D_f + 30 = 105$ mm).
4. **[1 mark]** Draw the **shaft portion** (rectangle 25 mm wide × 80 mm long) and the **flange** (outer rectangle = $75 \times 16$ mm thick). Use **Offset (O)** for the inner flange face.
5. **[1 mark]** Draw the **pitch circle** (Circle, dia 40 mm) on a construction layer and place **4 bolt circles** of dia 16 mm (M8 clearance hole) using **Polar Array (AR) → 4 items, 360°**.
6. **[1 mark]** Apply **Hatch (H)** with pattern **ANSI31** and scale 25 to the sectioned half of the flange and the rubber bushing. Use **Mirror (MI)** to complete the second flange.
7. **[1 mark]** Add **dimensions** — overall length, shaft $\varnothing 25$, flange $\varnothing 75$, $D_p \varnothing 40$, $4 \times \varnothing 8$, rubber bush length $25$, and the keyway $5 \times 5$.

**(b) Solution — BOM and Layer Strategy [7 marks]:**

**Bill of Materials (4 marks):**

| Item | Description | Qty | Material | Standard / Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Driving Shaft | 1 | EN8 Steel | $\varnothing 25 \times 80$ |
| 2 | Driven Shaft | 1 | EN8 Steel | $\varnothing 25 \times 80$ |
| 3 | Flange (Hub) | 2 | Cast Iron FG 200 | $\varnothing 75 \times 16$ |
| 4 | Rubber Bushing | 4 | Natural Rubber (Shore A 70) | $\varnothing 8 \times 25$ |
| 5 | Hex Bolt M8 | 4 | MS Grade 8.8 | M8 × 30 |
| 6 | Parallel Key | 2 | EN8 Steel | $5 \times 5 \times 22$ |

**Layer Management Strategy (3 marks):**

| Layer Name | Color | Linetype | Lineweight | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `01_CENTER` | Red | CENTER | 0.20 mm | Centerlines of circles, axes of shafts |
| `02_VISIBLE` | White | CONTINUOUS | 0.50 mm | All visible solid edges |
| `03_HIDDEN` | Yellow | HIDDEN | 0.25 mm | Hidden edges (e.g., bore behind flange) |
| `04_DIM` | Cyan | CONTINUOUS | 0.18 mm | Dimension lines, witness lines, text |
| `05_HATCH` | Green | CONTINUOUS | 0.18 mm | Section hatching (ANSI31) |
| `06_BOM` | Magenta | CONTINUOUS | 0.30 mm | Title block and BOM table borders |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (BOM):** Many students place the BOM table on the *drawing area* without reserving the lower-right corner — this violates **SP-46** and incurs a **2-mark deduction**. Also, never use a single "default" layer for everything; the layer strategy is *explicitly* a KTU grading criterion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ Flexible coupling is classified as a **permanent coupling**; sub-classes are **rigid**, **flexible**, and **universal**.
- ✅ Three misalignments accommodated: **angular**, **parallel (radial)**, and **axial (end float)**.
- ✅ Bushed-pin coupling uses **rubber in shear** to transmit torque and absorb shocks.
- ✅ The torque formula $T = \dfrac{P \times 60}{2\pi N}$ is the **starting point** of *every* coupling design problem.
- ✅ Empirical ratios: $D_f = 3d$, $D_p = 1.6d$, $D_o = 2d$, $L = 1.5d$, $t_f = 0.5d$, $d_b = 1.7\sqrt{d}$.
- ✅ **Standardization is mandatory** — always round to preferred ISO/R20 numbers.
- ✅ Number of bolts: $n = 4$ for $d \le 50$ mm; $n = 6$ for $d > 50$ mm.
- ✅ Bolt is in **double shear**; rubber is in **single shear**; flange is in **bearing (crushing)**.
- ✅ Drawing must show **half-sectional front view + end view**, all dimensions, hidden lines, centerlines, and a **BOM table** in the lower-right corner.
- ✅ Section hatch pattern is **ANSI31** with scale factor **20–30** for cast iron / steel and **ANSI32** for rubber.
- ✅ Layer management: at least **4–6 layers** (centerline, visible, hidden, dimension, hatch, BOM).
- ✅ Editing tools used: **Mirror, Polar Array, Offset, Trim, Fillet, Chamfer, Copy, Rotate**.
- ✅ Drawing tools used: **Line, Circle, Rectangle, Arc, Polyline, Hatch, Table**.
- ✅ **BOM columns:** Item No., Description, Qty, Material, Standard/Size (5 columns standard; 6th optional Part No.).
- ✅ Tolerance grades per **IS 2109**; surface finish symbols per **IS 2333**; sectioning per **SP-46**.
- ✅ Viva question to expect: *"Why is the rubber bushing designed in shear and not in compression?"* — **Answer:** because torque is *tangential*, producing shear; compression would only absorb axial load, not transmit torque.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Flexible Coupling with Bill of Materials**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isometric Drawings Using 2D Modelling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Isometric Drawings Using 2D Modelling

# Isometric Drawings Using 2D Modelling

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PCPEL307 / CAD Lab)**
> An *Isometric Drawing* is a 2D pictorial representation of a 3D object in which the three principal axes of the object are equally foreshortened and angled at **120°** from each other on the drawing plane, producing a non-perspective, parallel projection. In AutoCAD 2D modelling, isometric drawings are constructed by toggling the **ISODRAFT / Isometric Drafting mode** combined with the *Isoplane* (Top/Right/Left) feature to place entities along the three isometric axes of **30° (right), 150° (left), and 90° (vertical)**.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a transparent glass cube on your palm. Now tilt the cube **forward by approximately 35.264°** (arctan(1/√2)) so that you can see the top face, the front face, and the right-side face **all at the same time** with equal clarity. That is exactly the view an *isometric projection* gives you — but flattened onto paper. Unlike a photograph (perspective), the lines do not converge into a vanishing point; instead, all parallel edges of the cube remain parallel in the drawing.

In the context of a CAD lab, instead of building a true 3D solid and then rendering it, we *trick* the 2D environment into laying down lines along the three isometric angles (30°, 150°, 90°). Think of it as drawing a "fake 3D" using only 2D tools — like an artist sketching a 3D illusion on flat paper.

### 1.2 Key Isometric Angles (Quick Reference)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Right isometric axis angle from horizontal | **30°** |
| Left isometric axis angle from horizontal | **150°** |
| Vertical axis angle from horizontal | **90°** |
| Angle between any two isometric axes | **120°** |
| Foreshortening factor (true isometric) | **≈ 0.8165** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For *engineering isometric drawings* (not the strict mathematical isometric projection), a simplified scale of **1 : 1** is used along the axes — meaning the axes are drawn at 30°, 30°, and 90° but the lengths are *not* foreshortened. This is what you produce in AutoCAD 2D drafting.

### 1.3 Why Isometric Drawings Are Used in 2D CAD

- **Speed:** No 3D model needs to be built; draftsmen can quickly generate pictorials.
- **Clarity:** All three principal dimensions (Length, Width, Height) are visible on a single view.
- **Interchangeability:** Standard sheets, dimensioning practices, and printing formats remain 2D.
- **Manufacturing aids:** Assembly instructions, exploded pictorials, and pipe-routing schematics often use isometric sketches.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Isometric Projection

The mathematical foundation of isometric projection begins with the **rotation of a 3D coordinate system** until the projection of its three axes onto the 2D view plane become equally spaced. Let the original 3D axes be $(X, Y, Z)$ of unit length. The transformation that achieves this is:

### 2.1.1 Rotation Matrices

The standard derivation applies two sequential rotations:

**Step 1 — Rotation about the $Z$-axis by angle $\theta$** so that the $X$-axis lands on the line $y = x$ (i.e., 45° from horizontal):

$$
R_z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Rotation about the horizontal axis by angle $\phi$** to tilt the view until all three projected axes are equal:

$$
R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

When $\theta = 45°$ and $\phi = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \approx 35.264°$, the projected lengths of the three unit axes are identical — this defines the **isometric condition**.

### 2.2 Isometric Scale and Foreshortening

The true isometric scale is the projected length of a unit vector on any principal axis after these rotations:

$$
\text{Isometric scale} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}} \approx 0.8165
$$

Therefore, in *true* isometric projection, a line of true length $L$ should be drawn as $0.8165\,L$. However, in **engineering isometric drawings** (the practical AutoCAD version), this scale is taken as **1 : 1** for ease of dimensioning.

## 2.2.1 Direction Cosines of Isometric Axes

The angles that the three isometric axes make with the horizontal plane are:

| Axis | Angle from horizontal | Direction cosines $(l, m, n)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Isometric $X'$ (right) | $+30°$ | $(0.7071,\ -0.4082,\ 0.5774)$ |
| Isometric $Y'$ (left)  | $-30°$ (i.e. 150° from +X) | $(-0.7071,\ -0.4082,\ 0.5774)$ |
| Isometric $Z'$ (vertical) | $90°$ | $(0,\ 0,\ 0.8165)$ |

These values are the foundation for the AutoCAD polar-tracking angles.

## 2.3 Conversion of True Lengths to Isometric Lengths

For any line of true length $L$ lying on a principal isometric axis:

$$
L_{\text{iso}} = L \times 0.8165 \quad \text{(true isometric, not used in 2D CAD)}
$$

$$
L_{\text{iso}} = L \times 1 \quad \text{(engineering isometric, used in AutoCAD 2D)}
$$

## 2.4 Isometric Projection of a Circle

When a circle is projected isometrically, it becomes an **ellipse**. The major and minor axes are related to the original circle's diameter $D$:

- **Major axis** (perpendicular to the isometric axis on which the circle lies): $D_{\text{major}} \approx 1.22\,D$
- **Minor axis** (along the isometric axis): $D_{\text{minor}} \approx 0.71\,D$

> [!NOTE]
> In AutoCAD 2D, the `ELLIPSE` command automatically uses the *Isocircle* option to draw these ellipses with the correct major/minor axis ratio when the ISODRAFT mode is active.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Isometric scale (true) | $k$ | $k = \sqrt{2/3} \approx 0.8165$ | Drawing true projection |
| 2 | Engineering scale | $k_e$ | $k_e = 1$ | Practical AutoCAD drawing |
| 3 | Right-axis angle | $\alpha$ | $\alpha = 30°$ | Polar tracking |
| 4 | Left-axis angle | $\beta$ | $\beta = 150°$ | Polar tracking |
| 5 | Vertical-axis angle | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = 90°$ | Polar tracking |
| 6 | Tilt angle (true iso) | $\phi$ | $\phi = \arcsin(1/\sqrt{3}) \approx 35.264°$ | Math derivation |
| 7 | Yaw angle | $\theta$ | $\theta = 45°$ | Math derivation |
| 8 | Ellipse major axis | $D_M$ | $D_M = 1.22\,D$ | Isocircle drawing |
| 9 | Ellipse minor axis | $D_m$ | $D_m = 0.71\,D$ | Isocircle drawing |
| 10 | Angle between any two iso axes | — | $120°$ | Drawing setup |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Pictorial assembly drawings, exploded views of engines, gearboxes.
- **Civil / Architectural Engineering:** Plumbing isometric layouts, HVAC duct routing, isometric site plans.
- **Piping Industry:** Standard practice for piping isometric drawings (ISO drawings) used in fabrication.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Wiring diagrams in control panels.
- **Manufacturing:** Sheet-metal unfolding references, weldments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The lab viva frequently asks *"Why is the engineering isometric scale 1:1 and not 0.8165:1?"* The correct answer is: practical convenience — foreshortening makes dimensioning complicated and is unnecessary when the drawing is only for visualization, not for measurement of true 3D distances.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Isometric Projection Matrix

We begin with a unit vector $\vec{e}_X = (1, 0, 0)$ along the $X$-axis and apply two rotations to obtain its projected 2D coordinates $(X_p, Y_p)$ on the view plane.

### Step 1 — Rotate about $Z$ by $\theta = 45°$

Apply $R_z(45°)$ to $(1, 0, 0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= \cos 45° \cdot 1 - \sin 45° \cdot 0 = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071 \\
y_1 &= \sin 45° \cdot 1 + \cos 45° \cdot 0 = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071 \\
z_1 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So the rotated vector is $\vec{v}_1 = (0.7071,\ 0.7071,\ 0)$.

### Step 2 — Rotate about the *new* horizontal axis by $\phi = 35.264°$

For a tilt-down rotation, we apply $R_x(\phi)$ to $\vec{v}_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2 &= 1 \cdot 0.7071 - 0 \cdot 0.7071 + 0 = 0.7071 \\
y_2 &= 0 \cdot 0.7071 + \cos\phi \cdot 0.7071 - \sin\phi \cdot 0 = 0.7071\cos\phi \\
z_2 &= 0 \cdot 0.7071 + \sin\phi \cdot 0.7071 + \cos\phi \cdot 0 = 0.7071\sin\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\cos\phi = \sqrt{2/3} \approx 0.8165$ and $\sin\phi = 1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.5774$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2 &= 0.7071 \\
y_2 &= 0.7071 \times 0.8165 \approx 0.5774 \\
z_2 &= 0.7071 \times 0.5774 \approx 0.4082
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Project onto the 2D view plane (the $X_p$–$Y_p$ plane)

Drop the $z_2$ coordinate (orthographic projection) and keep $(x_2, y_2)$:

$$
\vec{e}_X^{\text{iso,2D}} = (0.7071,\ 0.5774)
$$

**Magnitude check (foreshortening):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
|\vec{e}_X^{\text{iso,2D}}| &= \sqrt{0.7071^2 + 0.5774^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.5 + 0.3333} \\
&= \sqrt{0.8333} \\
&\approx 0.9129
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this is the projected magnitude in the *3D intermediate frame*. The classical result $0.8165$ comes from a slightly different ordering (rotation first about the *horizontal* axis, then about the vertical). The 2D screen length of the unit $X$-axis after both rotations and projection is:

$$
L_X = |\vec{e}_X^{\text{iso,2D}}| = \sqrt{0.7071^2 + 0.5774^2} = \sqrt{0.8333} \approx 0.9129
$$

But the *true* isometric foreshortening convention used in textbooks — where the unit cube's edge has projected length $\sqrt{2/3} \approx 0.8165$ — arises when we consider the **screen-plane projection** after the *final* rotation places the rotated $Y$-axis perpendicular to the viewer's line of sight. Adopting that convention:

$$
\boxed{\; L_{\text{iso}} = \sqrt{2/3} \approx 0.8165 \times L_{\text{true}} \;}
$$

### Step 4 — Derive the Isometric Angle on Paper

The angle $\alpha$ that the projected $X$-axis makes with the horizontal screen axis is:

$$
\tan\alpha = \frac{y_2}{x_2} = \frac{0.5774}{0.7071} \approx 0.8165
$$

$$
\alpha = \arctan(0.8165) \approx 39.23° \quad \text{(true isometric, 3D-mapped)}
$$

However, in **engineering isometric** the angle is *idealised* to **30°** so that the three axes are evenly spaced at 120° intervals. This idealisation simplifies drafting and is the convention used in AutoCAD's snap angles.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Converting a 3D Point to Its 2D Isometric Drawing Coordinates

Given a 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ in the object space, its 2D isometric screen coordinates $(X_p, Y_p)$ — using the **engineering 30° convention** (no foreshortening) — are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_p &= (X - Y) \cdot \cos 30° = (X - Y) \cdot 0.8660 \\
Y_p &= (X + Y) \cdot \sin 30° - Z = (X + Y) \cdot 0.5 - Z
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** $\cos 30° \approx 0.8660$ is the horizontal projection of a unit vector along the right isometric axis. $\sin 30° = 0.5$ is the vertical projection of a unit vector along either isometric axis. $Z$ is subtracted because "up" in the object becomes "up minus the isometric plane's rise" in the screen.

### Worked Numerical Example

Draw the isometric view of a rectangular block of dimensions **Length = 60 mm**, **Width = 40 mm**, **Height = 50 mm**, with one corner at the origin $O = (0, 0, 0)$.

The eight vertices are:

$$
O,\ A=(60,0,0),\ B=(60,40,0),\ C=(0,40,0),\ D=(0,0,50),\ E=(60,0,50),\ F=(60,40,50),\ G=(0,40,50)
$$

Compute the 2D screen coordinates of each vertex using the formulas above.

For vertex $O = (0, 0, 0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_p &= (0 - 0) \cdot 0.8660 = 0 \\
Y_p &= (0 + 0) \cdot 0.5 - 0 = 0 \\
\Rightarrow O_{2D} &= (0, 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

For vertex $A = (60, 0, 0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_p &= (60 - 0) \cdot 0.8660 = 51.96 \\
Y_p &= (60 + 0) \cdot 0.5 - 0 = 30.00 \\
\Rightarrow A_{2D} &= (51.96,\ 30.00)
\end{aligned}
$$

For vertex $B = (60, 40, 0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_p &= (60 - 40) \cdot 0.8660 = 17.32 \\
Y_p &= (60 + 40) \cdot 0.5 - 0 = 50.00 \\
\Rightarrow B_{2D} &= (17.32,\ 50.00)
\end{aligned}
$$

For vertex $C = (0, 40, 0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_p &= (0 - 40) \cdot 0.8660 = -34.64 \\
Y_p &= (0 + 40) \cdot 0.5 - 0 = 20.00 \\
\Rightarrow C_{2D} &= (-34.64,\ 20.00)
\end{aligned}
$$

For vertex $D = (0, 0, 50)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_p &= (0 - 0) \cdot 0.8660 = 0 \\
Y_p &= (0 + 0) \cdot 0.5 - 50 = -50.00 \\
\Rightarrow D_{2D} &= (0, -50.00)
\end{aligned}
$$

For vertex $E = (60, 0, 50)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_p &= 51.96 \\
Y_p &= 30.00 - 50 = -20.00 \\
\Rightarrow E_{2D} &= (51.96,\ -20.00)
\end{aligned}
$$

For vertex $F = (60, 40, 50)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_p &= 17.32 \\
Y_p &= 50.00 - 50 = 0.00 \\
\Rightarrow F_{2D} &= (17.32,\ 0.00)
\end{aligned}
$$

For vertex $G = (0, 40, 50)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_p &= -34.64 \\
Y_p &= 20.00 - 50 = -30.00 \\
\Rightarrow G_{2D} &= (-34.64,\ -30.00)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Resulting Analytical Table

| Vertex | 3D Coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ | 2D Isometric $(X_p, Y_p)$ |
|---|---|---|
| $O$ | $(0, 0, 0)$ | $(0.00,\ 0.00)$ |
| $A$ | $(60, 0, 0)$ | $(51.96,\ 30.00)$ |
| $B$ | $(60, 40, 0)$ | $(17.32,\ 50.00)$ |
| $C$ | $(0, 40, 0)$ | $(-34.64,\ 20.00)$ |
| $D$ | $(0, 0, 50)$ | $(0.00,\ -50.00)$ |
| $E$ | $(60, 0, 50)$ | $(51.96,\ -20.00)$ |
| $F$ | $(60, 40, 50)$ | $(17.32,\ 0.00)$ |
| $G$ | $(0, 40, 50)$ | $(-34.64,\ -30.00)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Tip:** In AutoCAD, you do *not* compute these by hand. You enable the *Drafting Settings → Snap and Grid → Isometric snap*, then use `ELLIPSE → Isocircle` and `LINE` with the `F5` key to cycle between **Top / Right / Left** isoplanes. The above table, however, is the *expected* numerical answer if the KTU lab exam asks you to *manually plot* the isometric coordinates on graph paper before typing the LINE coordinates in AutoCAD.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Isometric Axis System (Conceptual Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vertical Axis: 90 degrees] --> B[Origin Point O]
    C[Right Axis: 30 degrees up from horizontal] --> B
    D[Left Axis: 150 degrees from positive X] --> B
    B --> E[All three axes at 120 degrees to each other on screen]
```

## 4.2 AutoCAD 2D Isometric Drafting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Open AutoCAD and Start New Drawing] --> B[Type DSETTINGS or Right-click on Snap Toggle]
    B --> C[Select Snap and Grid Tab]
    C --> D[Set Snap type to Isometric Snap]
    D --> E[Set Polar Tracking Angles: 30, 90, 150, 210, 270, 330]
    E --> F[Press F5 to switch Isoplane: Top, Right, Left]
    F --> G[Use LINE command with Polar Tracking for edges]
    G --> H[Use ELLIPSE then Isocircle option for circular features]
    H --> I[Use COPY command to replicate features along iso axes]
    I --> J[Use TRIM and EXTEND to clean intersections]
    J --> K[Dimension using ALIGNED or OBLIQUE dimstyles]
    K --> L[Plot or Print the Isometric Sheet]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Isometric Drawing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        P1[3D Object Description: Length, Width, Height, Features]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Processing Layer]
        Q1[Axis Transformation Module: 30 deg, 150 deg, 90 deg]
        Q2[Coordinate Mapper: 3D point to 2D screen point]
        Q3[Isocircle Generator: For circular features]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        R1[2D Isometric Sheet: Lines, Ellipses, Hidden Lines]
        R2[Dimensioned Pictorial View]
    end
    P1 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
```

## 4.4 Comparison: 2D Isometric vs 3D Isometric View in AutoCAD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S{Choose Drafting Strategy} -->|No 3D Model Available| T1[2D Isometric Drafting]
    S -->|3D Solid Available| T2[3D Isometric View via VIEW or VPOINT]
    T1 --> U1[Use ISODRAFT, F5 Isoplane, Isocircle, Polar 30/90/150]
    T2 --> U2[Rotate 3D View, then use FLATSHOT to export 2D snapshot]
    U1 --> V[Final Isometric Drawing]
    U2 --> V
```

## 4.5 Function-Key Reference for Isometric Drafting in AutoCAD

| Function Key | Action | Use in Isometric |
|---|---|---|
| **F5** | Cycle isoplanes (Top, Right, Left) | Aligns drawing plane to the chosen face of the 3D object |
| **F8** | Toggle Ortho mode | Constrains lines to 0°, 30°, 60°, 90°, 120°, 150° automatically when isometric snap is on |
| **F9** | Toggle Snap mode | Enables precise isometric grid snapping |
| **F10** | Toggle Polar tracking | Shows 30°, 90°, 150° tracking lines |
| **F11** | Toggle Object snap tracking | Useful for aligning ellipses to existing lines |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Caution:** Many students forget that **F5 has no effect** unless *Isometric snap* is enabled in DSETTINGS. They press F5 and conclude that isometric drafting is broken. Always verify the snap type first.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define an Isometric Drawing. Mention the angles used.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **Model Answer:**
> An *isometric drawing* is a 2D pictorial representation of a 3D object in which the three principal axes are equally inclined to the plane of projection. The three axes are drawn at angles of **30° (right)**, **150° (left)** and **90° (vertical)** from the horizontal, with **120°** between any two of them. The engineering isometric scale used in 2D CAD is **1 : 1** (no foreshortening) to simplify dimensioning. *(3 marks)*

### Q2. What is an Isoplane? How is it toggled in AutoCAD?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Model Answer:**
> An *isoplane* is one of the three 2D drawing planes (Top, Right, Left) in AutoCAD's isometric drafting mode. Each isoplane aligns the cursor grid to one of the three isometric axes. It is toggled in AutoCAD by pressing the **F5** function key, which cycles through **Top → Right → Left → Top** sequentially. The current isoplane is indicated on the status bar at the bottom of the AutoCAD window. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the principle of isometric projection. Derive the relationship between true length and isometric length. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Principle:** In isometric projection, the object is rotated about two axes so that its three principal axes make equal angles with the plane of projection. Equal foreshortening of all three axes is the defining feature.
>
> **Sketch:** A cube viewed in isometric, showing three axes at 30°, 150°, 90° meeting at the origin.
>
> **Derivation of Isometric Scale:**
>
> Consider a unit cube edge along the $X$-axis. After rotating the object by $\theta = 45°$ about the $Z$-axis and then by $\phi = 35.264°$ about the horizontal axis, the projected length on the 2D screen is:
>
> $$
> L_{\text{iso}} = \cos\phi \cdot \cos\theta = \cos(35.264°) \cdot \cos(45°)
> $$
>
> Substituting values:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \cos(35.264°) &\approx 0.8165 \\
> \cos(45°) &\approx 0.7071 \\
> L_{\text{iso}} &\approx 0.8165 \times 0.7071 \approx 0.5774
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> The factor $0.5774$ is the *true* foreshortening of a 3D unit vector on a single axis. Since the projected axes are then scaled on the drawing sheet to their nominal length of 1 unit, the **isometric scale** becomes:
>
> $$
> k = \frac{1}{0.5774} \times 0.5774 = 1 \quad \text{(engineering convention)}
> $$
>
> whereas in *strict* isometric projection the scale is $k = \sqrt{2/3} \approx 0.8165$. Thus:
>
> $$
> \boxed{\; L_{\text{iso}} = L_{\text{true}} \times k \;}
> $$
>
> where $k = 1$ in engineering practice and $k \approx 0.8165$ in true mathematical isometric projection. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to draw the isometric view of a hexagonal prism of base side **30 mm** and height **50 mm** in AutoCAD 2D. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> 1. **Setup:** Open AutoCAD → type `DSETTINGS` → Snap and Grid tab → Snap type: **Isometric Snap** → OK.
> 2. **Polar Tracking:** Enable polar tracking with angles **30°, 90°, 150°** and increments of 30°.
> 3. **Set Limits:** Use the `LIMITS` command to define a suitable drawing area (e.g., 0,0 to 420,297 for an A3 sheet).
> 4. **Draw Base Hexagon:** Press `F5` to select **Top isoplane**. Use the `POLYGON` command with 6 sides. To inscribe a hexagon of side 30 mm, the circumradius is $R = 30 =$ side length. The `POLYGON` command in AutoCAD always inscribes in a circle by default. The center is placed at a suitable point; the centre-to-vertex distance (apothem for inscribed circle) is $r = 30 \cdot \cos(30°) \approx 25.98$ mm. Specify the centre, then choose **Inscribed in circle**, and provide radius 30 mm.
> 5. **Switch Isoplane:** Press `F5` to **Right isoplane** (or **Left**). Use the `LINE` command to draw vertical lines of length 50 mm upwards from each hexagon vertex. The Ortho mode (`F8`) combined with isometric snap constrains these lines to the vertical iso axis.
> 6. **Project Top Hexagon:** Press `F5` back to **Top isoplane**. Use the `COPY` command with isometric mode to duplicate the bottom hexagon vertically to the top of the prism (offset of 50 mm along the vertical iso axis). Use a base point at one vertex and a second point 50 mm up — the snap grid will guide it.
> 7. **Join Vertices:** Use `LINE` to connect the corresponding vertices of the bottom and top hexagons.
> 8. **Hidden Lines:** Use `LINE` with the **Dashed linetype** to draw the three hidden edges passing through the interior of the prism.
> 9. **Cleanup:** Use `TRIM` and `EXTEND` to remove overlapping or stray segments.
> 10. **Dimensioning:** Use the `DIMALIGNED` or `DIMLINEAR` command. For dimensioning along iso axes, set the **oblique angle** in the dimension style to **30°** so that dimension text appears parallel to the iso edge.
> 11. **Title Block & Border:** Insert a standard A3 title block and complete the drawing. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**Q. (a)** Explain how circles are represented in isometric drawings. With a neat sketch, show the construction of an isocircle in AutoCAD. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> A circle in 3D space, when projected isometrically, becomes an **ellipse** on the 2D drawing plane. The major and minor axes of this ellipse depend on the diameter of the original circle and on which face of the 3D object the circle lies.
>
> **Key Ratios for Isocircle (True Isometric Projection):**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> D_{\text{major}} &= 1.22 \times D_{\text{true}} \\
> D_{\text{minor}} &= 0.71 \times D_{\text{true}}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Construction in AutoCAD 2D:**
>
> 1. Enable **Isometric Snap** in DSETTINGS.
> 2. Use the `ELLIPSE` command → AutoCAD prompts for an arc, center, or **Isocircle**. Type `I` for Isocircle.
> 3. Specify the **center point** of the isocircle.
> 4. Specify the **radius** (or diameter) — AutoCAD automatically draws the ellipse with the major axis perpendicular to the current isoplane and the minor axis parallel to it.
> 5. The `F5` key switches the isoplane, which rotates the isocircle's major axis accordingly (Top → ellipse lies flat; Right → ellipse on the right face; Left → ellipse on the left face).
>
> **Sketch:** Show a cube with circular holes on the top, right, and left faces — each hole drawn as an ellipse with the correct major/minor axis orientation. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** Draw the isometric view of a stepped cylindrical block of dimensions: lower cylinder diameter **50 mm** and height **30 mm**, upper cylinder diameter **30 mm** and height **40 mm**, both coaxial. List the AutoCAD commands used. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Steps in AutoCAD 2D:**
>
> 1. `DSETTINGS` → enable Isometric Snap; set polar angles to 30°, 90°, 150°.
> 2. Press `F5` to set isoplane to **Top**.
> 3. **Lower cylinder base ellipse:** `ELLIPSE` → `I` (Isocircle) → centre at $(0, 0)$ → radius `25` (half of 50 mm).
> 4. **Upper cylinder base ellipse:** `ELLIPSE` → `I` → centre at same point $(0, 0)$ → radius `15` (half of 30 mm).
> 5. Press `F5` to switch to **Right** isoplane.
> 6. **Lower cylinder vertical edges:** `LINE` → draw two vertical lines (tangent to the lower isocircle) of length `30` mm. Use the `QUADRANT` object snap to pick the leftmost and rightmost points of the lower isocircle.
> 7. **Top ellipse of lower cylinder:** `ELLIPSE` → `I` → centre at the midpoint between the two vertical lines at height 30 mm → radius `25`. Alternatively, use `COPY` with base point at the centre of the lower ellipse and second point `30` mm vertically up.
> 8. **Upper cylinder vertical edges:** `LINE` from the two `QUADRANT` points of the upper base ellipse to a height `40` mm above (i.e., total 70 mm from the bottom).
> 9. **Top ellipse of upper cylinder:** `COPY` the upper base ellipse to a height of 40 mm vertically.
> 10. **Hidden lines:** Draw a vertical centreline (using `LINE` with `CENTER` linetype) through both cylinders, plus the hidden interior tangent line of the upper cylinder's lower ellipse.
> 11. **Trim:** Use `TRIM` to remove the rear half of the lower and upper base ellipses (these are typically drawn as hidden/dashed lines if the cylinder is hollow, or omitted if solid).
> 12. **Dimensions:** Add diameter dimensions using `DIMDIAMETER` (oblique) for both cylinders, and a vertical height dimension of 30 mm and 40 mm on the right side.
>
> **List of Commands Used:**
>
> `DSETTINGS`, `ELLIPSE` (with Isocircle option), `LINE`, `COPY`, `TRIM`, `EXTEND`, `MOVE`, `DIMDIAMETER`, `DIMLINEAR`, `LTSCALE`, `LAYER`. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in the lab record and viva:**
> 1. **Forgetting to enable Isometric Snap** in DSETTINGS before pressing F5 — F5 is non-functional without it. *(-1 mark)*
> 2. **Drawing the isocircle on the wrong isoplane** — the major axis of the ellipse must be perpendicular to the current isoplane's vertical axis. *(-1 mark)*
> 3. **Using CIRCLE instead of ELLIPSE → Isocircle** for circular features — `CIRCLE` will produce a true circle, which is geometrically wrong for an isometric view. *(-1 mark)*
> 4. **Confusing *isometric projection* (0.8165 scale) with *isometric drawing* (1:1 scale)** — AutoCAD uses 1:1; the 0.8165 factor is only for true mathematical isometric. *(-1 to 2 marks)*
> 5. **Forgetting to switch the dimension style's oblique angle to 30°** — dimension text will appear horizontal and look out of place. *(-1 mark)*
> 6. **Not drawing the hidden lines** as dashed lines with the correct linetype — KTU expects three hidden edges to be shown for a standard rectangular block. *(-1 mark)*
> 7. **Mixing the left and right isometric axes** — lines that should slant up to the right are drawn slanting up to the left. *(-2 marks)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Isometric Drawing = 2D pictorial using 3 axes at 30°, 150°, 90°.**
- **Engineering isometric scale = 1:1** (no foreshortening) — used in AutoCAD 2D drafting.
- **True isometric scale = 0.8165:1** — used in mathematical/strict projection.
- **Three Isoplanes in AutoCAD: Top, Right, Left** — toggled using **F5**.
- **Isometric Snap must be enabled in DSETTINGS** for F5 to be meaningful.
- **Circles in isometric become ellipses** — use the `ELLIPSE → Isocircle` command in AutoCAD.
- **Ellipse axis ratio:** Major = **1.22 D**, Minor = **0.71 D** (true isometric).
- **Polar Tracking Angles:** **30°, 90°, 150°, 210°, 270°, 330°** must be enabled.
- **Hidden lines** must be drawn in **dashed/center linetype** as per KTU drafting standards.
- **Dimensioning:** Use *aligned* dimensions; set the **oblique angle** to **30°** for text to align with iso edges.
- **F8 (Ortho) + Isometric Snap** automatically restricts lines to the three iso axes — this is the fastest way to draft.
- **No 3D model is required** — the entire drawing is produced using 2D primitives (LINE, ELLIPSE, COPY, TRIM, etc.).
- **Real-world applications:** piping isometric drawings, assembly pictorials, exploded views, HVAC layouts.
- **Difference from 3D Isometric View in AutoCAD:** In 2D isometric drafting, you do *not* build a solid; in 3D isometric, you build the solid and use `VPOINT` or the `View Cube` to look at it, then optionally export via `FLATSHOT`.
- **Conversion formulas:**
  - $X_p = (X - Y) \cos 30°$
  - $Y_p = (X + Y) \sin 30° - Z$
- **KTU Viva Favourites:**
  - "What is the difference between isometric projection and isometric drawing?"
  - "Why is the engineering scale 1:1 and not 0.8165:1?"
  - "How do you draw a circle in isometric in AutoCAD?"
  - "What does the F5 key do in isometric drafting?"

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Engineering Parts Using 3D Modelling Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Engineering Parts Using 3D Modelling Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPEL307 – Computer Aided Design Lab)**, **"Simple Engineering Parts Using 3D Modelling Software"** refers to the systematic, feature-based creation of elementary mechanical components (such as blocks, brackets, flanges, bushings, shafts, and plates) using a parametric **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** platform such as **SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo Parametric, Autodesk Inventor, Siemens NX, or Fusion 360**.

A *simple engineering part* is defined as a single, monolithic solid body whose geometry can be completely described by:

- A **2D Sketcher Profile** (a closed, constraint-driven sketch on a datum plane).
- One or more **Geometric Features** (Extrude, Revolve, Sweep, Loft, Hole, Fillet, Chamfer, Shell).
- A set of **Parametric Dimensions** that govern its size and proportions.
- Optional **Material/Appearance** assignment and **Mass Property** evaluation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module Experiment 2 emphasizes **parametric, feature-based, history-driven modeling** rather than direct mesh editing. The student must demonstrate the use of the **Sketcher → Feature → Modification** workflow with proper application of **geometric constraints** and **dimensional constraints**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Virtual Lego + Smart Clay"

Imagine you are building a toy car with **LEGO bricks**, but each brick can be:
1. **Reshaped** before snapping on (sketcher profile with constraints).
2. **Stretched** in any direction to any length (extrude with parametric depth).
3. **Twisted** around an axis (revolve feature).
4. **Glued, subtracted, or intersected** with other bricks (Boolean operations: Add, Cut, Common).

The CAD software is essentially a **smart, parametric LEGO workshop**:
- The *sketch* is the 2D blueprint of each brick.
- The *feature* is the operation that turns a flat sketch into a 3D solid.
- The *model tree* is the **construction logbook** — it remembers every step, in order, so you can edit any earlier step and the entire part rebuilds automatically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** A simple bracket designed in CAD can be directly **3D-printed (Additive Manufacturing)**, **CNC-machined (Subtractive Manufacturing)**, or fed into **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** for stress simulation — all from the **same single parametric model**. This is the foundation of **Digital Twin** technology in Industry 4.0.

## 1.3 Standard CAD Terminology Aligned to KTU 2024

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Datum Plane** | A flat reference surface used to anchor sketches. Default: Front, Top, Right. |
| **Sketch** | A 2D profile drawn on a datum plane, composed of entities (line, circle, arc, spline). |
| **Constraint** | A rule that controls sketch geometry (Horizontal, Vertical, Tangent, Coincident, Equal). |
| **Dimension** | A numerical parameter that sizes a sketch entity. |
| **Feature** | A 3D operation (Extrude, Revolve, etc.) that adds or removes material. |
| **Boss** | An additive feature (adds material). |
| **Cut** | A subtractive feature (removes material). |
| **Fillet** | A rounded internal or external edge. |
| **Chamfer** | A beveled edge (45° or custom angle). |
| **Model Tree** | Chronological list of features in the FeatureManager. |
| **Mass Properties** | Computed Volume, Mass, Centre of Gravity, Moments of Inertia. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Reference Units (SI):** Linear dimension in **millimetres (mm)**, angular in **degrees (°)**, volume in **mm³**, mass in **kilograms (kg)** when material density is assigned.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Parametric Feature-Based Modeling Philosophy

Modern 3D CAD follows a strict **declarative** paradigm. Instead of instructing the software *how* to draw every vertex (as in 2D drafting), the user declares:

- **What** shape to create (sketch profile).
- **What** feature to apply (Extrude Boss, Revolve Cut).
- **What** parameters govern it (depth, angle, draft).
- **What** relations exist between features (concentric, coincident, equal).

The software's **Kernal / Solver** then evaluates this declarative tree and regenerates the B-Rep (Boundary Representation) solid. This is why it is called **history-driven, parametric modeling**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** The most frequently asked conceptual question is — *"Why is parametric modeling superior to direct modeling for engineering design?"* The answer must highlight **design intent capture**, **automatic regeneration**, and **change propagation**.

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of 3D Solid Modeling

### Pillar 1 — **Sketcher Discipline**
- A sketch must be **closed** (no gaps) to form a valid profile for Boss/Cut.
- A sketch must be **fully constrained** (black entities) or **under-constrained with no over-constraints** (blue entities indicate under-defined, red indicates over-defined).
- **Golden Rule:** Never dimension to a hard-coded origin. Always dimension **between entities** to preserve design intent.

### Pillar 2 — **Feature Operations**

#### 2.2.1 Extrude (Prismatic Feature)
Converts a 2D profile into a 3D solid by translating it along a vector normal to the sketch plane.

$$V_{\text{extrude}} = A_{\text{sketch}} \times L$$

where $A_{\text{sketch}}$ is the enclosed area of the closed profile (in $\text{mm}^2$) and $L$ is the extrusion depth (in $\text{mm}$).

**Conditions:** Profile must be closed, non-self-intersecting, and single-region (no nested loops unless intended for holes).

#### 2.2.2 Revolve (Axially Symmetric Feature)
Sweeps a 2D profile around a centerline (axis) by a given angle.

$$V_{\text{revolve}} = 2\pi \int_{a}^{b} r(h) \cdot A(h) \, dh$$

For a simple solid of revolution with constant cross-section $A$ between radii $r_1$ and $r_2$:

$$V_{\text{revolve}} = 2\pi \bar{r} \cdot A \cdot \theta_{\text{rad}}$$

where $\bar{r}$ is the centroidal radius, $A$ is the swept area, and $\theta_{\text{rad}}$ is the sweep angle in radians.

#### 2.2.3 Sweep (Profile Along Path)
Translates a profile along a 2D or 3D guide curve.

#### 2.2.4 Loft (Multi-Section Feature)
Connects two or more dissimilar cross-sections with ruled or smoothed surfaces.

### Pillar 3 — **Boolean Operations**

| Operation | Symbol | Effect | Volume Formula |
|-----------|--------|--------|----------------|
| **Union (Add/Boss)** | $A \cup B$ | Combines two solids into one | $V = V_A + V_B - V_{A \cap B}$ |
| **Subtract (Cut)** | $A - B$ | Removes volume of B from A | $V = V_A - V_{A \cap B}$ |
| **Intersect (Common)** | $A \cap B$ | Keeps only overlapping volume | $V = V_{A \cap B}$ |
| **Assemble** | $A \oplus B$ | Keeps A and B as separate bodies | $V = V_A + V_B$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For non-overlapping bodies, $V_{A \cap B} = 0$, and Union = Assembled sum.

### Pillar 4 — **Modification Features**
- **Fillet:** Replaces a sharp edge with a cylindrical/conical fillet of radius $R$.

$$r_{\text{fillet}} = R, \quad \text{transition surface is a quarter cylinder}$$

- **Chamfer:** Replaces an edge with a beveled face, typically $45° \times C$ (chamfer distance) or $C_1 \times C_2$ (asymmetric).
- **Shell:** Hollows the solid by removing material from selected faces, leaving walls of thickness $t$.

$$V_{\text{shelled}} = V_{\text{solid}} - t \cdot A_{\text{inner surface}}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | KTU Use Case |
|---------|---------|-------|--------------|
| **Volume of Extrude** | $V = A \cdot L$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Compute mass of prismatic block |
| **Volume of Revolve** | $V = 2\pi \bar{r} A \theta$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Shafts, flanges, pulleys |
| **Volume of Sphere** | $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Spherical bosses |
| **Volume of Cylinder** | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Boss features, holes |
| **Volume of Box** | $V = L \times B \times H$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Base plate, block |
| **Volume of Torus** | $V = 2\pi^2 R r^2$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Ring/groove features |
| **Mass** | $m = \rho \cdot V$ | $\text{kg}$ | Mass property report |
| **Density (Steel)** | $\rho = 7.85 \times 10^{-6}$ | $\text{kg/mm}^3$ | Material: AISI 1020 |
| **Density (Aluminium 6061)** | $\rho = 2.70 \times 10^{-6}$ | $\text{kg/mm}^3$ | Lightweight parts |
| **Centre of Gravity (Composite)** | $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum m_i x_i}{\sum m_i}$ | $\text{mm}$ | Multi-body part CG |
| **Moment of Inertia (Box)** | $I_{xx} = \frac{m(B^2 + H^2)}{12}$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{mm}^2$ | Rotational dynamics |
| **Fillet Surface Area** | $A_f = \frac{\pi R L}{2}$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Painting/coating estimate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The pipe symbol $\vert$ is intentionally replaced with `\vert` in formulas. Always use the **centroidal radius** $\bar{r}$ in revolve formulas — *not* the inner or outer radius — to apply the **Pappus Second Theorem** correctly.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real Industries

- **Automotive:** A simple engine bracket modeled parametrically can be resized for 5 different car models in minutes.
- **Aerospace:** Revolved turbine disk → directly exported as STEP/AP214 file for CAM toolpath generation.
- **Biomedical:** Custom orthopedic plate → mass properties checked for surgical weight limits.
- **Architecture:** Decorative baluster (revolved) → rendered in real-time, 3D-printed in resin.

> [!NOTE]
> The single most important takeaway: **A well-built parametric model is a re-usable engineering asset, not just a one-time drawing.**
