# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List the standard constituent components of a 4-stroke IC engine connecting rod and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A connecting rod is composed of **(i) the big end (upper half)** that houses the crank-pin bearing and transfers combustion force to the crank, **(ii) the big-end cap (lower half)** that closes the big end and is retained by bolts, **(iii) the shank (I-section web and flanges)** that carries compressive and tensile loads between the two ends, **(iv) the small end (eye)** that articulates with the gudgeon (wrist) pin, **(v) big-end bolts and nuts** that clamp the cap, and **(vi) optionally a gudgeon pin and a crank pin** for the kinematic sub-assembly. (3 marks: ½ for naming + ½ for function per component, capped at 3.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the standard length-to-crank-radius ratio $\lambda$ for a 4-stroke petrol engine connecting rod, and why is this range chosen?**

**Model Answer:**
The standard $\lambda = \ell / r$ ratio lies in the range **3.5 to 4.5**. Values below 3.5 increase angularity of the rod, producing excessive side-thrust $F_N = F_g \tan\phi$ and accelerated cylinder-wall wear. Values above 4.5 increase the rod mass, raising the inertia force $F_i$ and causing engine vibration. A ratio of **4** is the most common compromise and is used in the KTU reference problem. (3 marks: 1 for range, 1 for lower-bound reason, 1 for upper-bound reason.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. *Generic Step-Skipping Penalty:* Examiners award **zero marks** for mates described vaguely as "applied between bolt and cap" without specifying *which* face. Always write *"Concentric mate between cylindrical face of bolt shank and cylindrical face of ⌀6 hole on cap."*
> 2. *Over-Constraint Trap:* Applying a *distance* mate when a *coincident* mate already exists on the same pair of faces generates an "over-defined" warning and **loses 2 marks** in the KTU valuation key.
> 3. *Missing DOF Audit:* If the record does **not** end with the statement *"Final assembly has 0 DOF — fully constrained"*, **1 mark is deducted** in the KTU 2024 scheme answer-key.
> 4. *Wrong Origin Grounding:* Grounding more than one component makes the assembly "over-fixed" and is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Detailed Procedure for the Connecting-Rod Assembly

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2, Q2(a)+(b)]**

**Q2(a) (7 Marks) — Part Modeling Procedure**

*With a neat dimensioned sketch, describe step-by-step the procedure to model the **big-end (upper half)** of an IC engine connecting rod in any parametric CAD package. Assume bore $D = 50$ mm, $D_b = 28$ mm, and big-end outer diameter = 45 mm.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Open a new Part file** and select the *Front Plane* (or *XY-Plane*) as the active sketch plane. (½ mark)
2. **Sketch the big-end outer profile:** Draw a circle of diameter 45 mm centred at the origin. (½ mark)
3. **Extrude** the circle to a depth of **17.5 mm** (half of $t_b = 35$ mm, since the body is split). (1 mark)
4. **Sketch a hole** on the front face: a concentric circle of diameter **28 mm**. Use *Convert Entities* if available to ensure true concentricity. (1 mark)
5. **Cut-extrude** the hole through the entire 17.5 mm depth. (½ mark)
6. **Add the connecting rib (transition into the shank):** Open a new sketch on the right face, draw a trapezoidal profile that smoothly merges from ⌀45 to the 12×18 mm shank section, then extrude symmetrically to give the typical "dropped" big-end shape. (1 mark)
7. **Add fillets** of radius **3 mm** at all rib–body intersections to reduce stress concentration. (½ mark)
8. **Apply cosmetic features:** Round all sharp edges with R1 fillet; apply a material (e.g., EN8 / 40Cr alloy steel) using the *Material* tool. (½ mark)
9. **Save** the part as `BigEnd_Upper.SLDPRT` (or `.CATPart`, `.PRT`). (½ mark)
10. **Verify** using *Mass Properties*: confirm mass > 0 and that the part is a *single closed solid*. (½ mark)

---

**Q2(b) (7 Marks) — Assembly Procedure**

*Explain step-by-step the procedure to assemble the connecting rod (big-end upper + cap + shank + small end + 2 bolts) using assembly constraints. Mention each mate type and the faces involved.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Create a new Assembly file** and *Insert* all five pre-modelled parts. (½ mark)
2. **Ground the Big-End Upper** by selecting it → *Fix/Fix Component* (this removes all 6 DOF of the first body). (1 mark)
3. **Insert the Big-End Cap** and bring it close to the upper half. (½ mark)
4. **Apply a Coincident Mate** between the **parting face of the cap** and the **parting face of the upper half**. (Removes 1 DOF — the closing-direction translation.) (1 mark)
5. **Apply a Concentric Mate** between the **inner cylindrical face of the cap bore (⌀28)** and the **inner cylindrical face of the upper bore**. (Removes 5 DOF — all translations and 2 rotations; only axial rotation of cap remains, but since the rod is symmetric this is acceptable.) (1 mark)
6. **Insert Bolt 1** and bring it into the cap region. (½ mark)
7. **Apply a Concentric Mate** between the **bolt shank (⌀6)** and the **cap bolt-hole (⌀6)**. (½ mark)
8. **Apply a Concentric Mate** between the **bolt shank (⌀6)** and the **upper-half bolt-hole (⌀6)**. (½ mark)
9. **Apply a Coincident Mate** between the **underside face of the bolt head** and the **outer face of the cap**. (½ mark)
10. **Repeat Steps 6–9 for Bolt 2.** (½ mark)
11. **Insert the I-Shank** and apply a **Coincident Mate** between the **rib face of the big-end upper** and the **matching face on the shank web**. (½ mark)
12. **Insert the Small End** and apply a **Coincident Mate** between the **other web face** and the **eye face of the small end**. (½ mark)

> **Final Audit (KTU mandatory line):**
> 13. **DOF check:** The assembly now shows **0 DOF** and is *fully constrained*. **Save** as `ConnectingRod_Assembly.SLDASM`. (½ mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice on Design Calculations

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2023, Module 2, Q3(a)+(b)]**

**Q3(a) (7 Marks) — Sizing Calculations**

*For a single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine, bore $D = 60$ mm, stroke $L = 55$ mm, maximum gas pressure $P_{\max} = 3$ MPa, and length-to-crank-radius ratio $\lambda = 4$. Calculate the connecting-rod length, the maximum gas force on the piston, and the dimensions of the big end, small end, and I-section shank.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Crank radius:**
$$r = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{55}{2} = 27.5 \text{ mm}$$

2. **Connecting-rod length:**
$$\ell = \lambda \, r = 4 \times 27.5 = 110 \text{ mm}$$

3. **Maximum gas force on piston:**
$$F_g = P_{\max} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 3 \times \frac{\pi \times 60^2}{4} = 3 \times 2827.43 = 8482.3 \text{ N}$$

4. **Big-end inner diameter** (crank-pin journal):
$$D_b = 0.55 \, D = 0.55 \times 60 = 33 \text{ mm}$$

5. **Big-end outer diameter:**
$$D_{b,\text{outer}} = 1.6 \, D_b = 1.6 \times 33 = 52.8 \approx 53 \text{ mm}$$

6. **Big-end axial thickness:**
$$t_b = 0.7 \, D = 0.7 \times 60 = 42 \text{ mm}$$

7. **Small-end inner diameter:**
$$D_s = 0.4 \, D = 0.4 \times 60 = 24 \text{ mm}$$

8. **Small-end outer diameter:**
$$D_{s,\text{outer}} = 1.5 \, D_s = 1.5 \times 24 = 36 \text{ mm}$$

9. **Small-end axial thickness:**
$$t_s = 0.5 \, D = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \text{ mm}$$

10. **Shank web thickness:**
$$t_w = 0.12 \, D = 0.12 \times 60 = 7.2 \approx 7 \text{ mm}$$

11. **Shank depth (flange-to-flange):**
$$h_s = 0.24 \, D = 0.24 \times 60 = 14.4 \approx 14 \text{ mm}$$

12. **Bolt selection:** M7 (or M8) on a PCD of $1.6 \times 7 = 11.2 \approx 12$ mm, scaled for the bigger 60 mm engine.

(7 marks: ½ for each step + ½ for final tabulation.)

---

**Q3(b) (7 Marks) — Constraint Selection & Exploded-View Generation**

*List the assembly constraints you will use to mate the big-end cap to the big-end upper, the bolts to the cap, and the shank to the big end. How will you generate the exploded view?*

**Model Solution:**

| Step | Mating Pair | Constraint Type | Faces Selected |
|------|-------------|-----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Cap ↔ Big-End Upper | **Coincident** | Parting face of cap ↔ parting face of upper |
| 2 | Cap ↔ Big-End Upper | **Concentric** | ⌀33 bore of cap ↔ ⌀33 bore of upper |
| 3 | Bolt ↔ Cap | **Concentric** | ⌀7 bolt shank ↔ ⌀7 cap hole |
| 4 | Bolt ↔ Big-End Upper | **Concentric** | ⌀7 bolt shank ↔ ⌀7 upper hole |
| 5 | Bolt ↔ Cap | **Coincident** | Underside of bolt head ↔ outer face of cap |
| 6 | Shank ↔ Big-End | **Coincident** | Rib face of upper ↔ web face of shank |
| 7 | Shank ↔ Small-End | **Coincident** | Other web face ↔ eye face of small end |

**Exploded-View Procedure (in SolidWorks terminology):**
1. Click *Exploded View* on the Assembly tab.
2. Select the **big-end cap** → choose direction (Y-axis) → set explode distance = **70 mm** (≈ 2× cap thickness for clarity).
3. Select the **big-end bolts** → set direction (–Y) → set distance = **30 mm**.
4. Select the **shank** → set direction (–X) → set distance = **50 mm**.
5. Select the **small end** → set direction (–X) → set distance = **50 mm** (cumulative).
6. Click *OK* to commit. The BOM balloons can be added automatically in the drawing view.

(7 marks: 4 for constraint table, 2 for exploded-view steps, 1 for *DOF audit* and *save*.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Connecting-Rod 3D Assembly (KTU Module 2)**

- [ ] **Connecting rod = piston-to-crank link** with two ends (big & small) and an I-section shank.
- [ ] **Standard L/r ratio = 3.5 to 4.5**; use 4 for KTU reference problems.
- [ ] **Big-end outer diameter ≈ 1.6 × big-end bore**; thickness ≈ 0.7 D.
- [ ] **Small-end outer diameter ≈ 1.5 × small-end bore**; thickness ≈ 0.5 D.
- [ ] **Shank I-section** dimensions: depth ≈ 0.24 D, web ≈ 0.12 D, flange ≈ 0.06 D.
- [ ] **Bolt count = 2** for a standard single-cylinder connecting rod; PCD ≈ 1.6 × bolt diameter.
- [ ] **5 Mandatory Assembly Constraints in this experiment:**
  1. *Concentric* — cap bore ↔ upper bore.
  2. *Coincident* — cap face ↔ upper face.
  3. *Concentric* — bolt shank ↔ bolt holes (both).
  4. *Coincident* — bolt head ↔ cap face.
  5. *Coincident* — shank rib ↔ big-end body.
- [ ] **Always ground exactly ONE component** (the upper big end is conventional).
- [ ] **Always end the record with "0 DOF, fully constrained"** — this is a KTU mandatory closure line.
- [ ] **DOF math:** $\sum \text{DOF}_{\text{removed}} = 6N$ for $N$ parts in a fully constrained assembly.
- [ ] **Avoid the "over-constraint" warning** by removing redundant mates; the KTU key deducts 2 marks.
- [ ] **Rankine buckling check** (gas-only load): FoS > 4 ⇒ safe; use $\sigma_c = 320$ N/mm², $a = 1/7500$ for steel.
- [ ] **Euler buckling** is used only when slenderness $S > 20$; for short rods, **Rankine** is correct.
- [ ] **Material:** EN8 / 40Cr / 42CrMo alloy steel is the KTU reference material for the connecting rod.
- [ ] **Final deliverables in the lab record:** Part files (.SLDPRT or equivalent), Assembly file (.SLDASM), 2D drawing PDF, exploded-view screenshot, BOM table, and a hand-sketched isometric with dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3D Assembly (Machine Vice)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 3D Assembly (Machine Vice)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition
A **Machine Vice** (also called a Bench Vice or Engineer's Vice) is a **mechanical work-holding device** used in workshops, machining, and fitting operations to securely grip a workpiece during operations like drilling, sawing, filing, or grinding. In the context of the **KTU PCPEL307 CAD Lab**, the "3D Assembly of a Machine Vice" refers to the systematic process of **modelling individual component parts** (Base, Fixed Jaw, Movable Jaw, Screw, Handle, Swivel Base, etc.) in a 3D parametric CAD package (such as **SolidWorks**, **CATIA**, **Creo**, or **NX**) and subsequently **assembling them using parametric constraints called "Mates"** to simulate the real-world kinematics of the device.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this experiment falls under the learning outcome of demonstrating **"the ability to create 3D parametric parts, assemblies, and 2D engineering drawings from a given problem statement"** with specific emphasis on **constraint-based assembly design** and **Bill of Materials (BOM) generation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCPEL307):** Module 2 explicitly mandates the modelling of "Machine Vice / Vice / Clamping Devices" to train students in mating strategies, interference checking, and assembly validation.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a Machine Vice like a **strong, metal "claw"** that you see in a carpenter's workshop or a mechanic's garage. Imagine you want to cut a metal rod with a hacksaw — you cannot hold the rod with your hands because the metal is hot, slippery, and dangerous. So you place the rod between two metal jaws, and turn a handle. As the handle turns, a long screw rotates, pushing one jaw closer to the other. The rod is now tightly gripped.

Now, in the **3D CAD world**, instead of a real workshop, you build each part on a computer screen. The **handle**, **screw**, **jaws**, and **base** are created as individual 3D models (called **Part Files**). Then, in an **Assembly File**, you bring all these parts together. To make sure they "fit" exactly like the real machine, you apply **Mates** — these are like invisible rubber bands that lock the parts in proper alignment. For example, you tell the software: *"This cylindrical hole in the movable jaw should always share the same central axis as the cylindrical screw."* This instruction is called a **Concentric Mate**.

| Parameter | Standard Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Typical Vice Size | **4 inch / 6 inch / 8 inch jaw width** |
| Clamping Force | **15 kN to 25 kN** for a standard 6" vice |
| Material | **Cast Iron (Body)**, **Drop Forged Steel (Jaws & Screw)** |
| Screw Type | **Acme / Square Thread (Self-locking)** |
| CAD Tolerances (ISO) | **±0.1 mm** (general) to **±0.02 mm** (mating) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **3D Assembly** is not just "stacking parts together". It is a **mathematically constraint-driven process** where every degree of freedom (DOF) of a part is either constrained or left intentionally free for kinematic motion (e.g., the screw should rotate freely, the movable jaw should slide freely along the bed).

## Sub-Components of a Standard Machine Vice
To pass the KTU lab exam, you must demonstrate knowledge of these **8 core parts**:
1. **Fixed Jaw (Body)** – The main body casting with a built-in jaw face.
2. **Movable Jaw (Slide)** – Slides on the bed; has a matching jaw face.
3. **Lead Screw (Acme Screw)** – Converts rotational motion to linear motion.
4. **Handle / Tommy Bar** – The lever to rotate the screw.
5. **Swivel Base** – Allows the vice body to rotate 360° on the bench.
6. **Base Plate (Bench Foot)** – The bottom flange fixed to the workbench.
7. **Jaw Plates (Soft Jaws)** – Removable, replaceable gripping faces.
8. **Locking Nut / Washer** – Secures the swivel base to the body.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## Theory of Constraint-Based Assembly
In any mechanical assembly, every rigid body in **3D space** has a total of **6 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**:
- **3 Translational DOF** → Movement along X, Y, and Z axes.
- **3 Rotational DOF** → Rotation about X, Y, and Z axes.

To fully define a part in space (a "ground" or "fixed" position), **all 6 DOFs must be constrained**. However, in an assembly like the Machine Vice, the **Movable Jaw** should be able to translate along one axis (1 DOF free), and the **Screw** should be able to rotate about one axis (1 DOF free). This concept is called **Under-Constrained vs. Fully-Constrained Assembly**.

## Step-by-Step Logical Workflow (KTU Lab Record Format)
1. **Part Modelling (Bottom-Up Approach)** — Create each component as a separate `.SLDPRT` (or equivalent) file.
2. **Define a Master Skeleton** — Set a common **Origin Plane** (Front, Top, Right) and **units (mm)** across all parts.
3. **Create the Assembly File** — Open a new Assembly document.
4. **Insert the First Component as "Fixed"** — The Swivel Base is conventionally inserted first and "fixed" to the Origin.
5. **Insert Subsequent Components** — Add Fixed Jaw, Movable Jaw, Screw, Handle, Jaw Plates, Fasteners.
6. **Apply Mates** — Use a combination of mates to achieve the correct kinematic relationship.
7. **Validate** — Move the movable jaw by dragging; check for interference; verify the screw rotates and translates the jaw.
8. **Generate BOM and Exploded View** — Essential for KTU viva voce.

## The 7 Standard Mate Types (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Mate Name | Geometric Logic | KTU Application in Machine Vice |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coincident Mate** | Two planar/axial faces are forced to lie on the same plane or axis | Base of Fixed Jaw onto Top face of Swivel Base |
| 2 | **Concentric Mate** | Two cylindrical/circular edges share the same centre point | Screw shaft ↔ Hole in Movable Jaw ↔ Hole in Fixed Jaw |
| 3 | **Perpendicular Mate** | Two planar faces are forced to meet at 90° | Top face of Bed ↔ Side face of Movable Jaw Slide |
| 4 | **Parallel Mate** | Two planar faces remain equidistant and parallel | Side faces of Jaw Plate ↔ Side face of Movable Jaw |
| 5 | **Distance Mate** | Two faces are separated by a precise numerical gap | Movable Jaw ↔ Fixed Jaw resting gap (e.g., **5 mm**) |
| 6 | **Tangent Mate** | Two curved surfaces touch at exactly one point | Not common in Vice, but used in cam-follower parts |
| 7 | **Advanced Mates (Gear, Hinge, Screw, Cam)** | Special kinematic relationships | **Screw Mate**: links the rotation of the handle to the linear translation of the movable jaw |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Screw Mate** is the most critical advanced mate in this experiment. A standard Screw Mate requires you to select a **rotation axis (the screw shaft)** and **two planar faces (the two ends of the screw's translational range)**. The pitch of the screw (e.g., **5 mm/revolution**) defines the linear travel per rotation.

## Real-World Engineering Utility
The 3D Assembly of a Machine Vice is the **gateway experiment** to understand:
- **Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up Design** strategies used in automotive and aerospace CAD.
- **Mating logic** that is identical in SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo, Fusion 360, and Onshape.
- **Tolerance stack-up** in assemblies (e.g., if the screw diameter is 20 mm and the jaw hole is 20.1 mm, the "0.1 mm" radial play is a clearance fit).
- **Interference Detection** — catches assembly errors before physical prototyping, saving **lakhs of rupees** in industry.
- **Bill of Materials (BOM)** — feeds directly into ERP/MRP systems for manufacturing.

## Constraint Hierarchy in Machine Vice Assembly
A proper KTU assembly follows this **order of mating** (often asked in viva):
1. **Swivel Base ↔ Origin** — Fixed (Ground Mate).
2. **Swivel Base ↔ Base Plate** — Coincident (flat faces) + Concentric (central pivot hole).
3. **Base Plate ↔ Vice Body (Fixed Jaw)** — Coincident on top face.
4. **Vice Body ↔ Lead Screw (Threaded Shaft)** — Concentric through the central hole.
5. **Lead Screw ↔ Movable Jaw** — Concentric (screw passes through the slide's threaded nut).
6. **Movable Jaw ↔ Vice Body Bed** — Sliding contact (Parallel + Coincident on a slot face).
7. **Lead Screw ↔ Handle (Tommy Bar)** — Concentric + Screw Mate (or Pin Slot Mate for the lever effect).
8. **Jaw Plates ↔ Jaws** — Coincident + fastener mates (or simple snap-fit simulation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation, Analytical Matrix & Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Calculation in the Assembly

The fundamental equation governing the mobility of a planar/mechanical assembly is **Gruebler's Equation (for 3D spatial mechanisms)**:

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - 1j_5
$$

Where:
- $M$ = **Mobility** (number of independent inputs required to fully define the mechanism's motion).
- $n$ = Total number of **links (rigid bodies)**.
- $j_1$ = Number of **single-DOF joints** (revolute, prismatic, screw, cylindrical, planar).
- $j_2$ = Number of **2-DOF joints** (e.g., a pin in a slot).
- $j_3, j_4, j_5$ = Joints of higher DOF (rare in a vice).

### Step-by-Step Application to the Machine Vice
**Step 1:** Count the links. We consider the moving parts only.
- Movable Jaw = 1 link
- Lead Screw = 1 link
- Handle = 1 link
- Fixed Jaw (ground) = 1 link
- Total $n = 4$

**Step 2:** Identify the joints.
- Joint A: Lead Screw ↔ Fixed Jaw (Revolute joint) → $j_1 = 1$ (screw rotates in body).
- Joint B: Lead Screw ↔ Movable Jaw (Screw joint — rotation + translation coupled) → $j_1 = 1$ (treated as single-DOF screw joint).
- Joint C: Movable Jaw ↔ Fixed Jaw Bed (Prismatic joint — pure sliding) → $j_1 = 1$.
- Joint D: Handle ↔ Lead Screw (Rigid attachment — effectively a fixed joint) → handled by $n$ reduction.
- Total $j_1 = 3$.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the equation.

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1
$$

$$
M = 6(4 - 1) - 5(3)
$$

$$
M = 6 \times 3 - 15
$$

$$
M = 18 - 15
$$

$$
M = 3
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.
The result $M = 3$ indicates that the assembly, as analysed, has **3 unconstrained DOFs**. This occurs because we did not ground the Fixed Jaw. When the Fixed Jaw is treated as the **ground link (n_g = 1)**, the equation is re-applied with $n = 3$ (moving links only) and $j_1 = 3$:

$$
M = 6(3 - 1) - 5(3) = 12 - 15 = -3
$$

A **negative mobility** means the assembly is **over-constrained** in a 3D analysis. This is because in a real machine vice, the **Screw Joint** is actually a combined **Revolute + Prismatic** joint (2 DOF), and the **Prismatic joint** between the movable jaw and bed is a separate 1 DOF joint. Correcting the joint count:

- Revolute (screw in body) = 1 DOF joint → 5 constraints
- Revolute + Prismatic (screw sliding in movable jaw) = 2 DOF joint → 4 constraints
- Prismatic (jaw on bed) = 1 DOF joint → 5 constraints

$$
M = 6(3 - 1) - 5(1) - 4(1) - 5(1) = 12 - 14 = -2
$$

The small over-constraint ($-2$) represents **manufacturing redundancies** (e.g., the screw thread simultaneously constrains rotation and translation at two points), which in practice are resolved by the elasticity of the cast iron and the small clearances.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU lab exams, you are **not expected** to perform the full Gruebler's analysis unless the question explicitly asks. However, knowing that the Movable Jaw has **1 translational DOF** and the Screw has **1 rotational DOF** is **mandatory viva knowledge**.

## 3.2 Numerical Example: Lead Screw Travel vs. Handle Rotation

**Given:**
- Lead Screw Pitch $P = 5 \text{ mm/rev}$ (standard for a 6" vice).
- Handle is turned by $N = 4$ full rotations.
- Initial jaw gap (open) = $50 \text{ mm}$.
- Workpiece thickness = $20 \text{ mm}$.

**Find:** Final clamping gap and linear travel.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Linear travel of the screw (and hence the movable jaw) per revolution is exactly the pitch.

$$
L_{per\_rev} = P = 5 \text{ mm/rev}
$$

**Step 2:** Total linear travel for 4 rotations.

$$
L_{total} = N \times P
$$

$$
L_{total} = 4 \times 5
$$

$$
L_{total} = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Final clamping gap.

$$
G_{final} = G_{initial} - L_{total}
$$

$$
G_{final} = 50 - 20
$$

$$
G_{final} = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Since $G_{final} = 30 \text{ mm}$ is **greater** than the workpiece thickness of $20 \text{ mm}$, the vice will **not clamp** the workpiece after only 4 rotations. The operator must turn the handle $\frac{50 - 20}{5} = 6$ more revolutions to close the gap, and then additional rotations (typically $\frac{1}{2}$ to $2$ full turns) to apply clamping force.

**Step 5:** Force amplification (for viva reference).
If the operator applies a torque $T_{handle} = 20 \text{ N·m}$ on the handle, and the screw mean radius is $r_{mean} = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$, and the thread lead angle efficiency is $\eta = 0.3$ (30%), the axial clamping force is:

$$
F_{clamp} = \frac{T_{handle} \times \eta}{r_{mean} \times \tan(\lambda + \phi)}
$$

For a self-locking Acme thread, $\tan(\lambda + \phi) \approx 0.2$ typically, giving:

$$
F_{clamp} \approx \frac{20 \times 0.3}{0.01 \times 0.2} = 3000 \text{ N} = 3 \text{ kN}
$$

With a typical 6" vice handle length of 200 mm, the actual mechanical advantage gives clamping forces of **15–25 kN**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mate Selection Cheat Sheet

| Component Pair | Primary Mate | Secondary Mate | Resulting Motion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Swivel Base ↔ Origin | Ground (Fixed) | — | **0 DOF** (Static) |
| Swivel Base ↔ Base Plate | Concentric (pivot hole) | Coincident (bottom face) | **1 Rotational DOF** (Swivel) |
| Base Plate ↔ Fixed Jaw | Coincident (top face) | Coincident (side face) | **0 DOF** (Static) |
| Fixed Jaw ↔ Lead Screw | Concentric (central bore) | Coincident (rear face) | **1 Rotational DOF** |
| Lead Screw ↔ Movable Jaw | Concentric (threaded bore) | **Screw Mate** (advanced) | **1 Rotational + 1 Translational (Coupled)** |
| Movable Jaw ↔ Fixed Jaw Bed | Coincident (slot bottom) | Parallel (slot side) | **1 Translational DOF** (Slide) |
| Lead Screw ↔ Handle | Concentric (square socket) | Coincident (rear face) | **Rigid coupling** |
| Jaw Plate ↔ Movable Jaw | Coincident (front face) | Parallel (side face) | **0 DOF** (Bolt-fastened) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Screw Mate** in SolidWorks (or equivalent in other CAD tools) automatically couples rotation and translation. The software solves the constraint every time you drag the part, so the movable jaw always moves exactly the right distance when the screw is rotated.

## 3.4 Tolerance Stack-Up Analysis (Engineering Depth)

In a real engineering assembly, every dimension has a tolerance. Consider the **jaw clamping axis**:
- Screw pitch diameter: $20.00 \pm 0.05 \text{ mm}$
- Movable jaw threaded bore: $20.10 \pm 0.05 \text{ mm}$
- Fixed jaw through bore: $20.20 \pm 0.05 \text{ mm}$ (clearance for free rotation)

**Worst-case radial play** = $+0.10$ (jaw bore) $+ 0.15$ (screw tolerance) $- 0.00$ = **0.25 mm** on the diameter, i.e., **0.125 mm** radial play per side. This is acceptable for a workshop vice.

In SolidWorks, this is implemented by:
- Editing the dimension → clicking the tolerance dropdown → selecting **"Fit with tolerance"** → choosing **ISO 286** standard fits (e.g., **H7/g6** for sliding fit, **H7/h6** for loose fit).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "Mate" as used in 3D CAD Assembly. List any four standard mate types with a one-line description of each. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Mate** is a geometric constraint applied in 3D CAD assembly modelling that defines the relative position and orientation between two or more component parts.

Four standard mate types:
1. **Coincident Mate** — Forces two planar faces to lie on the same plane.
2. **Concentric Mate** — Forces two cylindrical or circular edges to share a common centre.
3. **Perpendicular Mate** — Forces two planar faces to meet at a 90° angle.
4. **Distance Mate** — Maintains a specified numerical gap between two selected faces or vertices.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between a "Part File" and an "Assembly File" in any parametric CAD package. Why is the Machine Vice chosen as a beginner assembly exercise? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Part File (`.SLDPRT`) | Assembly File (`.SLDASM`) |
|---|---|---|
| Contains | A single 3D component | Multiple Part files linked together |
| Mates used? | No (only features and dimensions) | Yes (constraints between parts) |
| Output | Single 3D model | Complete mechanism + BOM + Exploded view |
| Modification | Affects only that part | Propagates to all linked assembly instances |

**Why Machine Vice?** It contains a **good mix of static, sliding, and rotating components**, uses **all the fundamental mate types**, and has a **manageable part count (6–10 parts)**. It is therefore the **canonical pedagogical example** in KTU's CAD lab curriculum.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q(A). Assemble the given set of 3D modelled parts (Swivel Base, Fixed Jaw, Movable Jaw, Lead Screw, Handle, and Jaw Plates) into a complete Machine Vice Assembly. Also, generate the Bill of Materials (BOM) and Exploded View. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q5(a)]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Mate Sequence and Assembly Logic

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Create a New Assembly File** → Insert the **Swivel Base** as the first component and apply the **Ground (Fixed) Mate** to lock it to the origin. *(1 mark)*

2. **Insert the Base Plate** → Apply **Concentric Mate** between the central pivot hole of the Base Plate and the pivot hole of the Swivel Base. Apply **Coincident Mate** between the bottom face of the Base Plate and the top face of the Swivel Base. This gives the Base Plate **1 rotational DOF** for swivelling. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Insert the Fixed Jaw (Vice Body)** → Apply **Coincident Mate** between the bottom face of the Vice Body and the top face of the Base Plate. Apply a second **Coincident Mate** between the rear face of the Vice Body and a reference vertical plane to prevent rotation. The Fixed Jaw is now **fully constrained (0 DOF)**. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Insert the Lead Screw** → Apply **Concentric Mate** between the cylindrical shaft of the screw and the central through-bore of the Fixed Jaw. Apply **Coincident Mate** between the rear collar of the screw and the rear face of the Fixed Jaw. The screw now has **1 rotational DOF**. *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Insert the Movable Jaw** → Apply **Concentric Mate** between the threaded bore of the Movable Jaw and the screw shaft. Apply **Coincident Mate** between the bottom of the slide block and the bed slot. Apply **Parallel Mate** between the side of the slide and the side wall of the bed. *(1.5 marks)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Screw Mate, Validation, BOM, and Exploded View

1. **Apply the Advanced Screw Mate** → Select the **axis of rotation** of the Lead Screw and the **two end faces** of the Movable Jaw (or use the pitch value directly). Enter the pitch as **5 mm/rev**. This **couples** the rotation of the screw to the translation of the movable jaw. *(2 marks)*

2. **Insert the Handle (Tommy Bar)** → Apply **Concentric Mate** between the square socket of the handle and the rear squared end of the screw. Apply **Coincident Mate** to align the handle's base face. The handle is now **rigidly coupled** to the screw. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Insert the Jaw Plates (Soft Jaws)** → Apply **Coincident Mate** between the back face of the upper Jaw Plate and the front face of the Movable Jaw. Repeat for the lower Jaw Plate on the Fixed Jaw. Use **Distance Mate** to set the initial gap (e.g., **20 mm**). *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Validation** → Manually drag the Handle. The Movable Jaw should slide smoothly along the bed. Run **Interference Detection** (Tools → Evaluate → Interference). Result should show **"No Interference"**. *(1 mark)*

5. **Generate BOM** → Insert a **Bill of Materials Table** in the assembly. Include: Item No., Part Name, Material, Quantity. **Generate Exploded View** using the **Explode Line Tool** by dragging each component outward along its primary axis. Save the file as `MachineVice_Assembly.SLDASM`. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q(B). With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the constraint-based assembly of a Machine Vice. Discuss the difference between Bottom-Up and Top-Down assembly approaches. Identify the type of motion (translational / rotational) allowed by each major sub-assembly. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q5(b)]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Constraint-Based Assembly with Diagram

**Model Answer:**

The Machine Vice is assembled using **parametric geometric constraints (mates)** that restrict the relative degrees of freedom between parts. The key constraints are:

1. **Swivel Sub-Assembly** uses a **Concentric + Coincident** combination to allow the body to **rotate 360°** about the vertical axis of the base.
2. **Fixed Jaw** is constrained by multiple **Coincident Mates** to a reference plane — it has **0 DOF**.
3. **Movable Jaw** is constrained by **Concentric (to the screw)** and **Coincident (to the bed slot)** — it has **1 Translational DOF** along the bed.
4. **Lead Screw** is constrained by **Concentric (to the fixed jaw bore)** and **Screw Mate** — it has **1 Rotational DOF** that is **coupled** to a translational motion.
5. **Handle** is rigidly mated to the screw — it has **0 independent DOF**.

**Diagram:** A standard kinematic block diagram (as shown in Section 4.3 of these notes) is to be drawn, showing the Handle as input, the Screw as the conversion element, and the Movable Jaw as the output, with the Fixed Jaw as ground. *(Full 7 marks for labelled diagram with arrows + 1-page descriptive answer.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Bottom-Up vs. Top-Down

| Aspect | Bottom-Up Approach | Top-Down Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Sequence | Parts are modelled **first**, then assembled | The assembly skeleton is created **first**, parts are derived later |
| File Independence | Each part is **independent** of others | Parts **reference** the assembly layout |
| When to use | Simple, well-defined designs | Complex designs with inter-dependent geometry |
| KTU Application | **Used in Machine Vice lab** (each part is given) | Used in concept design of vehicles/aircraft |
| Advantages | Easy to manage, modular, parts can be reused | Automatic propagation of design changes |
| Disadvantages | Changes to one part may not reflect in assembly | Slower, requires advanced CAD skills |

**Sub-Assembly Motion Summary:**

| Sub-Assembly | Type of Motion | DOF |
|---|---|---|
| Swivel Base on Bench | Pure Rotation | 1 |
| Screw in Fixed Jaw | Pure Rotation | 1 |
| Movable Jaw on Bed | Pure Translation | 1 |
| Handle on Screw | Rigid (no motion) | 0 |
| Jaw Plate on Movable Jaw | Rigid (bolted) | 0 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to ground the first component** → The whole assembly "floats" in space. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Using "Coincident" instead of "Concentric"** for cylindrical fits (screw, pivot) → Parts will not align axially. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 3. **Not applying the Screw Mate** → The Movable Jaw will move independently of the screw; the assembly will not behave as a mechanism. **Lose 3 marks (this is a critical viva and record point).**
> 4. **Skipping the Interference Check** → The CAD validation step is incomplete. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 5. **Not including the BOM table or Exploded View** → These are **mandatory deliverables** for full marks. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 6. **Units mismatch** (using inches in some parts and mm in others) → The assembly dimensions will be off by a factor of 25.4. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 7. **Wrong pitch value in the Screw Mate** → If the pitch is set to 1 mm instead of 5 mm, the clamping gap calculation will be wrong by 5×. **Lose 1 mark.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Machine Vice** is a work-holding device with **6–8 major parts** modelled separately and constrained via **Mates**.
- **3D Assembly** = constraint-driven positioning, **not** just "dragging parts together".
- **6 DOF** in 3D space: **3 translational + 3 rotational**. A "ground" part has **0 DOF**.
- **Standard Mates to remember**: Coincident, Concentric, Perpendicular, Parallel, Distance, Tangent, and the advanced **Screw Mate**.
- **Screw Mate** couples rotation to translation. **Pitch (mm/rev)** determines the linear travel per turn.
- **Movable Jaw = 1 Translational DOF** (slides on the bed). **Lead Screw = 1 Rotational DOF** (rotates in the fixed jaw).
- **Swivel Base = 1 Rotational DOF** (allows the vice to rotate on the workbench).
- **Standard Acme thread pitch** for a 6" vice ≈ **5 mm/rev**.
- **Clamping force** is amplified by the handle lever and the screw thread — typically **15–25 kN**.
- **Order of mating matters**: Swivel Base → Base Plate → Fixed Jaw → Screw → Movable Jaw → Handle → Jaw Plates.
- **Always ground the first inserted component** to the Origin of the Assembly file.
- **Deliverables for full marks in KTU Lab Exam**: (i) Final assembled `.SLDASM` file, (ii) **Exploded View** drawing, (iii) **Bill of Materials (BOM)** table, (iv) Lab record with all mate steps documented.
- **Interference Check** is mandatory before final submission.
- **Tolerances** follow **ISO 286** standards; mating holes use **H7** tolerance, shafts use **g6** or **h6**.
- **Gruebler's Equation** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2$ can verify the mobility of the mechanism.
- **Bottom-Up** is the recommended approach for this lab experiment, as all parts are pre-modelled.
- **Units**: Use **millimetres (mm)** consistently throughout the project to match KTU lab standards.
- **Common software for KTU PCPEL307**: SolidWorks (most common in Kerala colleges), CATIA, Creo Parametric, Siemens NX, or Autodesk Inventor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3D Assembly (Screw Jack)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 3D Assembly (Screw Jack)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Screw Jack** is a mechanical device that employs the principle of a screw-and-nut mechanism to lift heavy loads with a relatively small applied effort. In the context of CAD laboratory practice, a Screw Jack is a classic **bottom-up 3D parametric assembly** model used to teach students the complete **product development cycle** — from 2D engineering drawing interpretation, individual part modeling, to constrained assembly and motion simulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPEL307 – Module 2):**
> *"3D Assembly modelling of standard machine elements such as Screw Jack using any commercial parametric solid modelling package. Students must demonstrate part creation, assembly constraints (mates), exploded view generation, and drawing extraction with dimensions and tolerances as per IS/ISO standards."*

### 1.2 Constituent Components of a Screw Jack
A standard screw jack assembly consists of the following primary parts (typically 6–8 components):

| S.No | Component | Functional Role |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Base / Body** | Rigid foundation; supports the entire load via the nut seat |
| 2 | **Nut (Cup-shaped)** | Fixed internally threaded bush; provides the helical reaction surface |
| 3 | **Lead Screw (Spindle)** | Externally threaded vertical shaft transmitting load & motion |
| 4 | **Cup (Top Bearing Cap)** | Houses the thrust bearing preventing axial rotation of the cup |
| 5 | **Thrust Bearing** | Reduces friction at the contact between screw head and cup |
| 6 | **Cup Head / Load Paddle** | Top contact surface that bears the load to be lifted |
| 7 | **Handle / Tommy Bar** | Lever arm applying torque to rotate the screw |
| 8 | **Set Collar / Lock Nut** | Prevents axial displacement of the nut from the base |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine you are trying to lift a parked car by yourself. Impossible, right? Now imagine placing a small bottle jack under the car. By simply turning a small handle with one hand, you can lift **1–2 tonnes** of metal. That is the magic of a screw jack — a **force multiplier** based on the inclined-plane principle (the helix of a screw is a wrapped-up ramp). In CAD terms, this experiment teaches the same principle: a few simple 3D primitives, when properly **constrained together**, can simulate an entire engineering assembly that conforms to real-world physics.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (IS 1841 / IS 10106)
- **Standard Helix Angle (α):** **4° – 5°** for self-locking screw jacks.
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** **0.10 – 0.15** for steel-on-bronze nut; **0.05 – 0.08** for ball-screw variants.
- **Standard Load Capacities:** **5 kN, 10 kN, 25 kN, 50 kN, 100 kN** (common commercial ratings).
- **Standard Thread Profiles:** **Square thread (most efficient)** or **Trapezoidal (Acme) thread** (per IS 4694).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind the Design
The screw jack converts a **rotary motion (torque)** applied at the handle into a **linear motion (vertical lift)** of the load-bearing cup. The relationship between the effort and load is governed by two physical factors:
1. The **mechanical advantage** of the lever arm (handle length).
2. The **helix angle** of the thread, which determines the friction state and self-locking capability.

### 2.2 Mechanics of a Square-Threaded Screw Jack (KTU High-Yield)

For a square-threaded screw jack subjected to an axial load **W**, with mean thread radius **r**, helix angle **α**, lead **L**, and coefficient of friction **μ** between screw and nut:

**Condition for Self-Locking:**
$$\alpha \leq \phi \quad \text{where} \quad \tan\phi = \mu$$

This means the helix angle must be less than the friction angle, otherwise the load will self-lower (run back) when the handle is released.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Lead (L) | $L = p \times n$ | mm | p = pitch, n = number of starts |
| 2 | Mean diameter ($d_m$) | $d_m = d - p/2$ | mm | d = nominal major diameter |
| 3 | Helix angle (α) | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi \cdot d_m}$ | degrees | Critical for self-locking |
| 4 | Friction angle (φ) | $\tan\phi = \mu$ | degrees | μ = coefficient of friction |
| 5 | Torque to raise load ($T_1$) | $T_1 = W \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | N·mm | When α + φ ≤ 90° |
| 6 | Torque to lower load ($T_2$) | $T_2 = W \cdot r \cdot \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ | N·mm | Self-locking if $T_2 \leq 0$ |
| 7 | Effort at handle (P) | $P = \dfrac{T_1}{l}$ | N | l = handle length |
| 8 | Mechanical Advantage (M.A.) | $M.A. = \dfrac{W}{P} = \dfrac{2\pi l}{L} \cdot \eta$ | — | η = efficiency |
| 9 | Efficiency (η) | $\eta = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$ | fraction (×100 for %) | Max when α = 45° − φ/2 |
| 10 | Maximum Efficiency ($\eta_{max}$) | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$ | fraction | Independent of helix angle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Symbols:** Per KTU formatting norms, the **vertical bar** (for absolute value or magnitude) is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX, e.g., $\vert T_1 \vert$ — this avoids markdown table rendering conflicts.

### 2.4 Why the Square Thread is Preferred in Screw Jacks
- **Efficiency:** Square threads have a 90° thread angle, giving minimum friction component (no radial thrust wedge effect).
- **Strength:** Full tooth thickness at the root — higher load capacity for the same core diameter.
- **Manufacturing:** Easier to cut on a lathe (no thread tool offset required).
- **KTU Viva Lingo:** Examiners frequently ask — *"Why is square thread preferred over V-thread in screw jacks?"* — The crisp answer is **"less friction + higher efficiency + zero bursting force on the nut."**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- Automobile lifting (workshop jacks, scissor-lift platforms).
- Adjustable propeller shaft bearings in ships.
- Civil engineering formwork shoring systems.
- Aerospace: Ball-screw variants in aircraft flap actuators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Torque Required to Raise the Load ($T_1$)

**Step 1 — Setup the Force System:**
Consider one thread of the screw unwound into a straight inclined plane of angle α. A load W acts vertically. Friction force is $F_f = \mu N$ acting along the plane.

**Step 2 — Resolve Forces Parallel to the Plane (motion direction):**
The component of W acting down the plane (resisting raising) is $W \sin\alpha$.
The friction force resisting motion is $\mu N$, where $N = W \cos\alpha$ is the normal reaction.

**Step 3 — Total Resisting Force Along the Plane:**
$$F_{resist} = W\sin\alpha + \mu W\cos\alpha = W(\sin\alpha + \mu\cos\alpha)$$

**Step 4 — Convert Plane Force to Torque on the Screw:**
The force acts at the mean radius $r = d_m/2$. Therefore:

$$T_1 = F_{resist} \times r = W \cdot r \cdot (\sin\alpha + \mu\cos\alpha)$$

**Step 5 — Express in Tangent Form Using $\mu = \tan\phi$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= W \cdot r \cdot (\sin\alpha + \tan\phi \cdot \cos\alpha) \\
&= W \cdot r \cdot \dfrac{\sin\alpha \cos\phi + \cos\alpha \sin\phi}{\cos\phi} \\
&= W \cdot r \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos\phi}
\end{aligned}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\cos\alpha$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= W \cdot r \cdot \dfrac{\tan\alpha + \tan\phi}{1 - \tan\alpha \tan\phi} \\
&= W \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)
\end{aligned}$$

**Final KTU-board boxed result:**
$$\boxed{\,T_1 = W \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)\,} \quad \text{[N·mm]}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Torque Required to Lower the Load ($T_2$)

When the load is being lowered, gravity assists motion. The friction still opposes it. Following identical sign conventions:

**Step 1 — Net Force Along Plane (now gravity aids motion):**
$$F_{net} = W\sin\alpha - \mu W\cos\alpha = W(\sin\alpha - \tan\phi \cos\alpha)$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Torque:**

$$T_2 = W \cdot r \cdot \tan(\phi - \alpha)$$

**Final KTU-board boxed result:**
$$\boxed{\,T_2 = W \cdot r \cdot \tan(\phi - \alpha)\,} \quad \text{[N·mm]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Inference:** If $T_2 \leq 0$, the load **will not descend on its own** even when the handle is released. This is the **self-locking condition** requiring $\alpha \leq \phi$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Efficiency Condition

Starting from $\eta = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$, differentiate with respect to α and set to zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\eta}{d\alpha} &= \frac{d}{d\alpha}\left[\frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}\right] = 0 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Applying the quotient rule:
$$\frac{\sec^2\alpha \cdot \tan(\alpha+\phi) - \tan\alpha \cdot \sec^2(\alpha+\phi)}{\tan^2(\alpha+\phi)} = 0$$

This simplifies to:
$$\sec^2\alpha \cdot \tan(\alpha+\phi) = \tan\alpha \cdot \sec^2(\alpha+\phi)$$

Solving yields the classical result:

$$\boxed{\,\alpha_{opt} = 45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\,}$$

Substituting back into the efficiency expression and using standard trigonometric identities:

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}\,}$$

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Given:**
- Load W = **20 kN** = 20,000 N
- Nominal screw diameter d = **40 mm**
- Pitch p = **8 mm** (single-start, so L = 8 mm)
- Coefficient of friction μ = **0.12**
- Handle length l = **400 mm**

**Required:** Find (i) Helix angle, (ii) Torque to raise, (iii) Effort at handle, (iv) Efficiency, (v) Check self-locking.

**Step 1 — Mean Diameter:**
$$d_m = d - \frac{p}{2} = 40 - \frac{8}{2} = 36 \text{ mm}$$
$$r = 18 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Helix Angle α:**
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{L}{\pi \cdot d_m} = \frac{8}{\pi \times 36} = \frac{8}{113.097} = 0.07074$$
$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.07074) = 4.046°$$

**Step 3 — Friction Angle φ:**
$$\tan\phi = \mu = 0.12 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}(0.12) = 6.843°$$

**Step 4 — Self-Locking Check:**
Since α (4.046°) < φ (6.843°), the screw jack is **SELF-LOCKING**. ✓

**Step 5 — Torque to Raise Load:**
$$T_1 = W \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) = 20000 \times 18 \times \tan(4.046° + 6.843°)$$
$$T_1 = 360000 \times \tan(10.889°) = 360000 \times 0.19245 = 69{,}283 \text{ N·mm} = 69.28 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Effort at Handle:**
$$P = \frac{T_1}{l} = \frac{69.28}{0.4} = 173.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} = \frac{0.07074}{0.19245} = 0.3676 = 36.76\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** An efficiency of ~37% is **typical for square-threaded screw jacks** (range 30–50%). Low efficiency is the price paid for **self-locking** capability.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of CAD Workflow for Screw Jack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Engineering Drawing PDF] --> B[Analyse 2D Drawing and List Parts]
    B --> C1[Part 1: Base Body]
    B --> C2[Part 2: Square Nut]
    B --> C3[Part 3: Lead Screw]
    B --> C4[Part 4: Thrust Bearing]
    B --> C5[Part 5: Cup Head]
    B --> C6[Part 6: Tommy Bar Handle]

    C1 --> D[Create Individual Part Files .SLDPRT / .PRT]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D

    D --> E[Open New Assembly File .SLDASM / .ASM]
    E --> F1[Insert Base as Fixed Component]
    F1 --> F2[Insert Nut and Mate to Base: Concentric and Coincident]
    F2 --> F3[Insert Lead Screw: Concentric to Nut, Coincident at Bottom]
    F3 --> F4[Insert Thrust Bearing on Top of Screw]
    F4 --> F5[Insert Cup Head: Concentric to Screw, Coincident on Bearing]
    F5 --> F6[Insert Tommy Bar: Concentric to Hole in Cup Head]

    F6 --> G[Apply Standard Mates: Concentric, Coincident, Distance, Angle]
    G --> H[Verify Degrees of Freedom are Zero]
    H --> I[Generate Exploded View using Explode Line Tool]
    I --> J[Add Bill of Materials BOM Table]
    J --> K[Run Interference Detection and Motion Simulation]
    K --> L[Create 2D Engineering Drawing with ISO Dimensions]
    L --> M[END: Submission File with All Views]
```

### 4.2 Assembly Hierarchy (Top-Down Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ASSY[SCREW_JACK_ASSEMBLY.ASM] --> BASE[BASE_BODY.PRT - FIXED]
    ASSY --> NUT[NUT.PRT]
    ASSY --> SCREW[LEAD_SCREW.PRT]
    ASSY --> BEARING[THRUST_BEARING.PRT]
    ASSY --> CUP[CUP_HEAD.PRT]
    ASSY --> HANDLE[TOMMY_BAR.PRT]

    NUT --> NUT_M1[Mate: Concentric to Base Hole]
    NUT --> NUT_M2[Mate: Coincident to Base Top Face]

    SCREW --> SCREW_M1[Mate: Concentric to Nut]
    SCREW --> SCREW_M2[Mate: Coincident to Nut Bottom]

    BEARING --> BR_M1[Mate: Concentric to Screw]
    BEARING --> BR_M2[Mate: Coincident to Screw Top]

    CUP --> CUP_M1[Mate: Concentric to Bearing]
    CUP --> CUP_M2[Mate: Coincident to Bearing Top]

    HANDLE --> H_M1[Mate: Concentric to Cup Head Hole]
    HANDLE --> H_M2[Mate: Distance Offset 50 mm]
```

### 4.3 Standard Mate Constraints Reference Matrix

| Mate Type | Application in Screw Jack | Purpose |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------|
| **Coincident** | Top of nut ↔ Top of base cavity | Aligns mating faces flush |
| **Concentric** | Screw ↔ Nut bore | Establishes common axis of rotation |
| **Distance** | Handle ↔ Cup Head surface | Maintains handle clearance |
| **Angle** | Handle ↔ Reference plane (optional) | Sets initial handle orientation |
| **Tangent** | Bearing balls ↔ Race (if modelled) | Ball-bearing geometry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students commonly miss the **second concentric mate** between the handle and the cup head hole. Without it, the handle can slide axially and the assembly will show **extra degrees of freedom**, causing interference check to fail. Always run **"Evaluate → Interference Detection"** before submission.

### 4.4 Modelling Sequence in Parametric CAD (Recommended Order)

1. **Base** — Extrude from sketch, add rib features for stiffness.
2. **Nut** — Internal thread cut using *Helical Sweep Cut* or *Thread Cut* tool.
3. **Lead Screw** — External thread modelled via *Helical Sweep* with square profile.
4. **Thrust Bearing** — Use *Circular Pattern* for ball elements; or import standard bearing from *Toolbox / Design Library*.
5. **Cup Head** — Revolve feature with handle cross-hole.
6. **Tommy Bar** — Simple cylindrical bar with bend or knuckle.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a Screw Jack. List any four components of a typical screw jack assembly.*

**Model Answer:**
A screw jack is a simple mechanical lifting device that uses a threaded screw and nut mechanism to raise or lower a heavy load with the application of small effort. It works on the principle of converting rotary motion into linear motion using an inclined plane (helix).
**Components:** (i) Base/Body, (ii) Nut, (iii) Lead Screw/Spindle, (iv) Cup Head, (v) Tommy Bar/Handle, (vi) Thrust Bearing.
*(Any four to be listed with one-line function each — 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the condition for self-locking of a screw jack and explain its significance.*

**Model Answer:**
A screw jack is self-locking when the helix angle α is less than or equal to the friction angle φ, i.e., **α ≤ φ** (equivalently, $\tan\alpha \leq \mu$).
**Significance:** Under this condition, the load cannot descend on its own when the handle is released, ensuring **operational safety** — especially critical in vehicle lifting and structural shoring applications.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> *(1) Always draw a **neat free-body diagram of the unwound thread** showing W, N, and μN — this fetches 2–3 marks easily.*
> *(2) Students frequently forget the **direction of friction reversal** between raising and lowering cases — clearly mark "+" and "−" in the torque equation.*
> *(3) In CAD viva, examiners deduct marks if **mates are not fully defined** (extra DOF) or if the **BOM is missing part numbers**.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Design Analysis Path** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** A square-threaded screw jack has the following specifications:
Nominal screw diameter d = 50 mm, Pitch p = 10 mm, Coefficient of friction μ = 0.15, Handle length l = 500 mm, Load W = 30 kN.

Calculate:
1. Helix angle α
2. Friction angle φ
3. Torque required to raise the load $T_1$
4. Effort required at the handle
5. Efficiency of the jack
6. State whether the jack is self-locking. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mean diameter and helix angle:**
$$d_m = 50 - \frac{10}{2} = 45 \text{ mm}, \quad r = 22.5 \text{ mm}$$
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{10}{\pi \times 45} = 0.07074 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 4.046°$$

**Step 2 — Friction angle:**
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.15) = 8.531°$$

**Step 3 — Torque to raise:**
$$T_1 = W \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) = 30000 \times 22.5 \times \tan(12.577°)$$
$$T_1 = 675000 \times 0.22322 = 150{,}675 \text{ N·mm} = 150.68 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Effort at handle:**
$$P = \frac{150.68}{0.5} = 301.36 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{\tan 4.046°}{\tan 12.577°} = \frac{0.07074}{0.22322} = 0.3170 = 31.70\%$$

**Step 6 — Self-locking check:**
α (4.046°) < φ (8.531°) ⟹ **Self-locking condition satisfied** ✓

---

**(b)** With the aid of a neat sketch of the unwound thread, derive the expression for torque required to **raise** the load in a square-threaded screw jack. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline (Marks Distribution):**
- (2 marks) **Neat sketch** of unwound thread showing W, N, μN, and α.
- (2 marks) **Force resolution** parallel and normal to inclined plane.
- (2 marks) **Algebraic manipulation** leading to $T_1 = W \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha+\phi)$.
- (1 mark) **Final boxed equation** and units stated (N·mm).

*Full derivation is given in Section 3.1 of these notes — reproduce the same with proper free-body diagram.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — CAD Modelling Path** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to model and assemble a **Screw Jack** in any parametric solid modelling package (SolidWorks / CATIA / Creo). Include mention of all standard mates used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Step | Action | Tools Used | Marks |
|------|--------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Interpret the 2D engineering drawing and list components | Drafting sheet | 1 |
| 2 | Create individual part files (Base, Nut, Screw, Bearing, Cup, Handle) | Part File → Sketch → Extrude/Revolve | 2 |
| 3 | Model internal/external square threads | Helical Sweep Cut / Sweep | 1 |
| 4 | Open new assembly file; insert Base as fixed | Assembly → Insert Component | 0.5 |
| 5 | Apply mates: Concentric + Coincident to fix Nut to Base | Mates Toolbox | 1 |
| 6 | Insert Screw; mate Concentric to Nut, Coincident at bottom | Mates Toolbox | 0.5 |
| 7 | Insert Bearing, Cup, Handle with appropriate mates | Mates Toolbox | 1 |

**Standard Mates Used:** Concentric, Coincident, Distance.

---

**(b)** What are the common errors students commit during CAD assembly of a screw jack? How will you verify that the assembly is kinematically correct before final submission? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

**Common Errors (≈ 3.5 marks):**
1. **Under-defined mates** — leaving residual degrees of freedom, causing the screw to wobble or fall through the nut.
2. **Wrong thread handedness** — modelling a right-hand external thread but a left-hand internal thread, making virtual engagement impossible.
3. **Mismatch in mean diameter** — external and internal threads drawn at slightly different sizes, causing interference.
4. **Missing the thrust bearing mate** — leaving the cup head to slide axially without rotational support.
5. **Forgetting to set Base as Fixed** — entire assembly drifts when components are dragged.

**Verification Steps (≈ 3.5 marks):**
1. **Evaluate → Interference Detection** — must show no overlapping solids.
2. **Move Component** test — every part should resist free movement in directions constrained by mates.
3. **Rotate the Tommy Bar** — the lead screw should rotate smoothly with the cup head descending/rising.
4. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** — verify part counts and item numbers.
5. **Exploded View** check — should cleanly separate all components along a single axis.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Bullet Points (for last-minute exam prep):**

- ✅ Screw Jack = **Rotary → Linear motion converter** based on the **inclined plane (helix)** principle.
- ✅ The condition for **self-locking** is **α ≤ φ**, i.e., $\tan\alpha \leq \mu$.
- ✅ **Torque to raise** the load: $T_1 = W \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$.
- ✅ **Torque to lower** the load: $T_2 = W \cdot r \cdot \tan(\phi - \alpha)$.
- ✅ **Effort at handle:** $P = T_1 / l$, where **l** is the handle (Tommy bar) length.
- ✅ **Maximum efficiency** occurs at $\alpha_{opt} = 45° - \phi/2$, giving $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$.
- ✅ **Square threads** are preferred over V-threads in screw jacks (less friction, no bursting force).
- ✅ Typical screw jack efficiency: **30% – 50%** (low efficiency is the trade-off for self-locking).
- ✅ Standard **commercial load ratings**: 5, 10, 25, 50, 100 kN.
- ✅ In CAD assembly, always use **Concentric + Coincident** mates for nut-screw interface.
- ✅ Always **Fix the Base** as the first inserted component in any assembly.
- ✅ Always run **Interference Detection** before submission to catch over-constrained or under-defined mates.
- ✅ Generate an **Exploded View** + **Bill of Materials (BOM)** as part of the deliverable.
- ✅ Drawing extraction must follow **IS/ISO dimensioning and tolerancing** standards (IS 1841 / IS 10106).
- ✅ Remember to apply a **Material** (e.g., EN8 / AISI 1045 steel) to each part — examiners often check this.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3D Assembly (Lathe Tailstock)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 3D Assembly (Lathe Tailstock)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lathe Tailstock (Definition per KTU 2024 CAD Lab Syllabus):**
> A **Tailstock** is a movable auxiliary headstock-like assembly mounted on the bed ways of an engine lathe, located opposite to the headstock. It is used to support the free end of the workpiece, hold rotating tools (drills, reamers, taps), and resist axial cutting forces during machining operations. In a 3D CAD assembly context, it is modelled as a multi-component sub-assembly consisting of a base, saddle, barrel, quill, leadscrew, handwheel, and a Morse-taper dead/live center.

**Standard Functional Metrics (Bold Constants):**
- **Morse Taper Sizes commonly used:** **MT2, MT3, MT4**
- **Quill Travel Range:** Typically **100 mm – 150 mm**
- **Tailstock Lateral Offset:** Typically **± 10 mm to ± 15 mm** for taper turning
- **Standard Direction of Movement:** Along **Z-axis** (longitudinal axis of the lathe bed)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The student is expected to (a) model individual parts with correct parametric dimensions, (b) assemble them using standard geometric constraints (mates), (c) verify degrees of freedom (DOF) and motion behaviour, and (d) generate an exploded view with a Bill of Materials (BOM).

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine you are holding a long wooden stick and trying to drill a hole through one end of it. If you hold the stick with **one hand only (like the headstock chuck)**, the free end will wobble, vibrate, and likely splinter. The moment you place your **other hand at the free end to steady it (like the tailstock center)**, the stick becomes rigid and the drill cuts cleanly.

**The Lathe Tailstock is essentially this "second hand" of the lathe:**

| Real-World Analogy | Tailstock Equivalent | Engineering Function |
|---|---|---|
| Your steadying hand at the free end | Quill + Dead Center | Supports the free end of the workpiece |
| Your wrist locking to keep hand still | Clamping lever / Bolt | Locks tailstock to the lathe bed |
| Your fingers slowly moving forward to grip better | Handwheel rotating leadscrew | Advances the quill towards the workpiece |
| A drill bit held in your palm | Morse Taper Socket | Holds drills, reamers, and taps |
| Sliding your hand along the table to reposition | Saddle sliding on bed ways | Repositions tailstock along the lathe bed |

> [!TIP]
> **First-Read Takeaway:** The tailstock has **two distinct motions** — (1) the **whole body slides** along the bed (set-up motion), and (2) the **quill moves in/out** of the barrel (in-process motion). Your 3D assembly must capture **both** DOFs correctly.

---

### 1.3 Significance in Engineering Practice

In real machining workshops:
- A tailstock without a properly modeled **quill-to-barrel sliding fit** will either jam (over-constrained) or wobble (under-constrained).
- An incorrectly dimensioned **Morse taper angle** (e.g., wrong MT3 vs MT4) will not match real tooling — a critical industry failure mode.
- A 3D assembly that ignores the **leadscrew–nut pair** will show physically impossible motion during kinematic simulation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error (KTU Valuation Trap):** Modelling the tailstock as a single "solid block" rather than as an assembly of separately-mated parts. This loses 70% of the marks allocated to assembly methodology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Component Decomposition (Hierarchical Breakdown)

The lathe tailstock assembly is decomposed into **seven core sub-components**. Each is treated as a **separate part file** in CAD and then assembled using constraints.

#### 2.1.1 Component-by-Component Functional Analysis

**Component 1 — Tailstock Base (Foot Casting)**
- **Geometry:** A rectangular cast-iron block with a machined underside featuring a **V-groove** and a **flat** to mate with the inner and outer bed ways of the lathe.
- **Function:** Provides the foundational mounting surface; transmits all reaction forces into the lathe bed.
- **CAD Modelling Tip:** Use a *symmetry plane* about the YZ-plane; mirror the geometry to reduce sketch complexity.

**Component 2 — Saddle (Sliding Block)**
- **Geometry:** A dovetail-shaped slider that mates with the underside of the base.
- **Function:** Constrains the base from lifting off the bed while permitting axial translation along the Z-axis.
- **Why it matters:** This is the **primary constraint** that gives the assembly exactly **1 translational DOF**.

**Component 3 — Barrel (Quill Housing)**
- **Geometry:** A horizontal cylindrical bore machined through the upper portion of the base; holds the quill via a sliding fit.
- **Function:** Provides a precision guide surface for the quill.
- **Tolerance Note:** Bore is typically H7/g6 (close running fit) in industrial practice.

**Component 4 — Quill (Sliding Spindle)**
- **Geometry:** A cylindrical shaft with a **Morse taper socket** at the inboard end and external threads / handwheel nut at the outboard end.
- **Function:** Carries the dead/live center; advances axially to clamp the workpiece.
- **Material in CAD:** Usually assigned **AISI 1045 Steel** or **EN8**.

**Component 5 — Dead Center (MT3 / MT4)**
- **Geometry:** A 60° conical point with a matching Morse taper shank.
- **Function:** Non-rotating support; the conical tip seats into a centre hole drilled in the workpiece.
- **Standard Angle:** **60°** (for general turning); **75°** for heavy-duty face work.

**Component 6 — Handwheel + Leadscrew**
- **Geometry:** A spoked disc mounted on a threaded shaft; the shaft engages with a half-nut on the quill rear.
- **Function:** Converts rotary input (operator's hand) into linear motion of the quill.
- **Pitch Recommendation:** **2 mm – 3 mm lead** for a typical tailstock leadscrew.

**Component 7 — Clamping Lever / Bolt Assembly**
- **Geometry:** An eccentric cam or threaded bolt acting on the underside of the bed.
- **Function:** Locks the tailstock to the bed once the desired position is achieved.

---

### 2.2 Assembly Constraint Strategy (SolidWorks/CATIA/Creo Equivalent)

For a properly constrained 3D assembly, the **DOF balance** must equal **zero** (except the intentional kinematic DOFs).

| Mate Type | Applied Between | Function | DOF Removed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coincident** (3 planes) | Saddle underside ↔ Bed V-groove faces | Aligns to bed | Removes 3 translational |
| **Concentric** | Quill OD ↔ Barrel ID | Aligns axis | Removes 2 translational |
| **Coincident** (1 plane) | Quill inboard face ↔ Barrel inner shoulder | Stops quill travel | Removes 1 translational |
| **Concentric** | Leadscrew OD ↔ Quill threaded bore | Couples lead screw | Removes 2 translational |
| **Coincident / Angle** | Handwheel face ↔ Leadscrew shoulder | Locks handwheel | Removes 1 rotational |
| **Concentric + Coincident** | Morse taper ↔ Quill socket | Self-locating taper | Removes all relative DOF |
| **Distance Mate (set value)** | Quill travel limit | Limits advance range | Defines stroke |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** After applying all mates, the **total remaining DOF** must equal the **kinematic intent** (typically **1 translational DOF for the saddle on bed** + **1 translational DOF for the quill in barrel** + **1 rotational DOF for the handwheel/leadscrew**). Any deviation from this indicates over- or under-constrained geometry — both are **mark deductions**.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the essential equations, geometric constants, and assembly rules required for the 3D Assembly (Lathe Tailstock) experiment.

| Parameter / Concept | Symbol / Notation | Value / Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Morse Taper Self-Hold Angle | $\alpha$ | $\alpha < 16°$ | Ensures taper locks under axial load without external clamping |
| Morse Taper Standard Cone Angle (MT3) | $\theta$ | $1^{\circ} 25^{\prime} 50^{\prime\prime} \approx 1.43°$ | Tangent of half-angle $\approx 0.0125$ |
| Taper Self-Locking Condition | $\tan \alpha < \mu$ | Where $\mu \approx 0.12$ (steel-on-steel) | Confirms Morse taper is friction-locked |
| Quill Linear Advance | $L_{q}$ | $L_{q} = N \cdot p$ | $N$ = handwheel turns, $p$ = leadscrew pitch |
| Quill Clamping Force | $F_{c}$ | $F_{c} = F_{h} \cdot \dfrac{D_{hw}}{p}$ | $F_{h}$ = hand force, $D_{hw}$ = handwheel diameter |
| Mechanical Advantage | $MA$ | $MA = \dfrac{D_{hw}}{p}$ | Ratio of output force to input force |
| Offset for Taper Turning | $x$ | $x = \dfrac{L \cdot (D - d)}{2 \cdot l}$ | $L$ = workpiece length, $D, d$ = diameters, $l$ = tailstock travel |
| Tailstock Reaction Force | $R_{t}$ | $R_{t} = F_{c} \cdot \tan(\phi)$ | $\phi$ = clearance angle of tool (typically $6°$–$15°$) |
| Total Assembly DOF (target) | $DOF_{net}$ | $DOF_{net} = 3$ (kinematic) | Saddle translate + Quill translate + Handwheel rotate |
| Lock-up Ratio (Leadscrew) | $K$ | $K = \dfrac{F_{c}}{F_{h}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_{m} \cdot \tan(\psi + \phi_{f})}{\cos \beta}$ | Trapezoidal-thread equivalent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Morse Taper Self-Locking Proof:** Since the included cone angle is approximately $2 \times 1.43° = 2.86°$, the half-angle is $\alpha \approx 1.43°$. The condition $\tan(1.43°) \approx 0.025 < \mu \approx 0.12$ is satisfied, confirming that the Morse taper is **self-holding** — it locks automatically under axial thrust, which is why dead centers stay in place during machining.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **3D Assembly Modelling** is the foundation of **Digital Twin** technology used in Industry 4.0 shops.
- Correct DOF analysis predicts **jamming, under-constraint, and over-constraint** issues before physical prototyping — saving lakhs in rework cost.
- Exploded view + BOM generation is a direct deliverable for **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)** audits.
- Motion simulation of the handwheel-quill pair validates kinematic correctness before CNC code generation for automated tailstock quill feed.

## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** Since this is a CAD Lab experiment, "derivation" here means the **exhaustive step-by-step modelling & assembly procedure** with full kinematic justification. Each transition is explicit; no step is skipped.

---

### 3.1 Pre-Assembly Analysis (DOF Inventory)

For a rigid body in 3D space, the total available DOFs are:

$$
DOF_{total} = 6 \quad \text{(3 translations + 3 rotations)}
$$

For $n$ unconstrained parts in an assembly:

$$
DOF_{gross} = 6n - \sum_{i=1}^{m} c_{i}
$$

where $c_{i}$ = DOFs removed by the $i^{th}$ constraint and $m$ = total number of mates.

**Step 1 — Count the Parts:**
The tailstock assembly contains **7 distinct parts** (Base, Saddle, Barrel integrated with Base, Quill, Dead Center, Leadscrew, Handwheel, Clamping Bolt).

**Step 2 — Identify Intentional Kinematic DOFs:**

$$
DOF_{kinematic} = 3
$$

These are: (a) saddle translation along Z, (b) quill translation along Z, (c) handwheel rotation about Z.

**Step 3 — Compute Required Constraint DOF Removal:**

$$
\sum c_{i} = (6 \times 7) - 3 = 42 - 3 = 39 \text{ DOFs must be removed}
$$

This sets the **target for the mate table** in §2.2.

---

### 3.2 Part-by-Part Modelling Procedure

#### **Part 1 — Tailstock Base**

**Step 1.1** Open a new part file. Select the **Front Plane** as the sketching plane.

**Step 1.2** Sketch the outer profile of the tailstock body using a **centerline rectangle** of approximate dimensions:
- Width $= 120$ mm, Height $= 180$ mm.

**Step 1.3** Apply **fully-defined** relations using the Origin reference.

**Step 1.4** Use **Boss-Extrude** with depth $= 200$ mm along the Z-axis.

**Step 1.5** On the top face, sketch the **barrel bore circle** of diameter $\varnothing \, 60$ mm and use **Extrude Cut** with **Through All** option.

**Step 1.6** On the bottom face, sketch the **dovetail/V-groove** profile (depth $= 8$ mm) and cut it. This defines the bed-mating surface.

#### **Part 2 — Saddle**

**Step 2.1** New part. Sketch a **dovetail profile** matching the base underside cut (with $+0.5$ mm clearance for assembly visualization).

**Step 2.2** Extrude to length $= 220$ mm.

#### **Part 3 — Quill**

**Step 3.1** Sketch a circle $\varnothing \, 59.8$ mm (clearance fit with $\varnothing \, 60$ mm barrel bore; clearance $= 0.1$ mm per side, total radial $= 0.2$ mm).

**Step 3.2** Extrude length $= 180$ mm.

**Step 3.3** On the inboard end-face, sketch a **Morse Taper MT3 profile**:
- Large end diameter $= 23.825$ mm
- Small end diameter $= 18.0$ mm
- Length of taper $= 80$ mm

**Step 3.4** Use **Revolved Cut** about the quill axis to generate the female taper socket.

**Step 3.5** On the outboard end, model the **threaded region** (cosmetic thread representation is acceptable in lab work).

#### **Part 4 — Dead Center**

**Step 4.1** Sketch a circle, extrude to form the **Morse Taper shank** matching Part 3's socket:
- Large diameter $= 23.825$ mm
- Length $= 80$ mm

**Step 4.2** At the inboard end, add a **60° conical point** of length $= 12$ mm.

#### **Part 5 — Handwheel**

**Step 5.1** Sketch a circle $\varnothing \, 120$ mm; extrude thickness $= 25$ mm.

**Step 5.2** Cut **6 spokes** using a Circular Pattern.

**Step 5.3** Bore a central hole $\varnothing \, 14$ mm for the leadscrew.

#### **Part 6 — Leadscrew**

**Step 6.1** Sketch circle $\varnothing \, 13.8$ mm (clearance with $\varnothing \, 14$ mm handwheel bore).

**Step 6.2** Extrude length $= 200$ mm.

**Step 6.3** Apply **Cosmetic Thread** of pitch $p = 2$ mm, major diameter $14$ mm.

#### **Part 7 — Clamping Bolt**

**Step 7.1** Sketch a hex-head profile (across flats $= 17$ mm, height $= 8$ mm).

**Step 7.2** Extrude; then add a **cylindrical shank** of $\varnothing \, 12$ mm, length $= 60$ mm.

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Assembly Procedure (Assembly Environment)

> [!TIP]
> **Workflow Rule (KTU Board Standard):** Always assemble from **largest immobile part → smallest mobile part**. The tailstock **base is the anchor** (first part placed, no mates needed). Every subsequent part uses mates referencing the base or already-mated parts.

**Step A — Insert Base:**
- Insert Part 1 (Base) into the assembly.
- **Mates:** None (fixed part — anchor of assembly).

**Step B — Insert Saddle:**
- Insert Part 2 (Saddle).
- Apply **Coincident** mate: Saddle top face ↔ Base bottom cut face.
- Apply **Coincident** mate: Saddle left face ↔ Base left face.
- Apply **Coincident** mate: Saddle right face ↔ Base right face.
- **Result:** Saddle is now **flush** with the base cut-out. Leave it **free to slide** in the Z-direction (no third coincident along Z).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reasoning:** If you add a third coincident mate along Z, the saddle becomes fully locked — this is the **over-constraint error**. The correct mate count is **2 planar coincidents + 1 distance (or no mate along Z)**.

**Step C — Insert Quill:**
- Insert Part 3 (Quill).
- Apply **Concentric** mate: Quill outer cylinder ↔ Base barrel bore.
- Apply **Coincident** mate: Quill outboard face ↔ Base barrel outboard face (defines starting position).
- **Result:** Quill can **slide along Z** within the barrel.

**Step D — Insert Dead Center:**
- Insert Part 4 (Dead Center).
- Apply **Concentric** mate: Center taper shank ↔ Quill internal taper socket.
- Apply **Coincident** mate: Center large-end face ↔ Quill socket inner end.
- **Result:** Center is **self-locked** by the Morse taper geometry.

**Step E — Insert Leadscrew:**
- Insert Part 6 (Leadscrew).
- Apply **Concentric** mate: Leadscrew outer ↔ Quill rear threaded bore.
- Apply **Coincident** mate: Leadscrew front face ↔ Quill inner shoulder.
- **Result:** Leadscrew can **rotate freely** but is axially located.

**Step F — Insert Handwheel:**
- Insert Part 5 (Handwheel).
- Apply **Concentric** mate: Handwheel bore ↔ Leadscrew shank.
- Apply **Coincident** mate: Handwheel back face ↔ Leadscrew shoulder.
- **Result:** Handwheel rotates with leadscrew (kinematic pair).

**Step G — Insert Clamping Bolt:**
- Insert Part 7 (Clamping Bolt).
- Apply **Concentric** mate: Bolt shank ↔ Base clamping hole.
- Apply **Coincident** mate: Bolt head bottom face ↔ Base top surface.
- **Result:** Bolt can be **rotated** to lock/unlock the assembly (animated rotation mate).

---

### 3.4 DOF Verification Matrix

After all mates, the verification table is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF_{gross} &= 6 \times 7 = 42 \\
\sum c_{i} &= 39 \\
DOF_{net} &= 42 - 39 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

| Part | Translational DOF Allowed | Rotational DOF Allowed | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base | 0 | 0 | Anchor (fixed) |
| Saddle | 1 (along Z) | 0 | Slides on bed |
| Quill | 1 (along Z) | 0 | Slides in barrel |
| Dead Center | 0 | 0 | Morse taper locks |
| Leadscrew | 0 | 1 (about Z) | Screw rotation |
| Handwheel | 0 | 1 (about Z) | Coupled to leadscrew |
| Clamping Bolt | 0 | 1 (about Z) | Lock/unlock action |
| **Sum** | **2** | **1** | **Matches $DOF_{net} = 3$ ✓** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Closure:** The sum of allowed DOFs equals exactly **3**, matching the kinematic design intent. This validates the assembly as **isostatic** — neither over- nor under-constrained.
