## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any six functional parts of a Lathe Tailstock and state the function of any two.*

**Model Answer (6 parts, 2 functions — within 3-mark limit):**

| S.No. | Part | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base / Foot | Mounts tailstock on lathe bed |
| 2 | Saddle | Slides along bed to set position |
| 3 | Barrel | Houses and guides the quill |
| 4 | Quill | Carries the dead center; advances axially |
| 5 | Dead / Live Center | Supports free end of workpiece |
| 6 | Handwheel | Operates the leadscrew to move quill |
| 7 | Leadscrew | Converts rotary to linear motion |
| 8 | Clamping Bolt / Lever | Locks tailstock to bed |

**Function of Quill:** The quill is a precision-ground cylindrical spindle that carries the dead center at its inboard end and is driven by the leadscrew at its outboard end. It advances linearly to clamp the workpiece between the chuck and the center.

**Function of Handwheel:** The handwheel is the manual input device that the operator rotates to drive the leadscrew. Each rotation advances the quill by one pitch length of the leadscrew, providing fine, controlled clamping force.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]**
*Why is the Morse taper self-locking? State the standard included angle for MT3.*

**Model Answer:**

The Morse taper is self-locking because its included cone angle is small enough that friction between the mating surfaces exceeds the axial thrust component trying to push the taper apart. Mathematically, the self-locking condition is:

$$
\tan \alpha < \mu
$$

For MT3, the half-angle is approximately $1° 25' 50''$ (i.e., $\alpha \approx 1.43°$). Therefore:

$$
\tan(1.43°) \approx 0.025
$$

Since the coefficient of friction $\mu$ for hardened steel-on-steel is approximately $0.12$, the inequality $0.025 < 0.12$ is satisfied. Hence the taper grips tighter under load and will not loosen.

**Standard Included Angle for MT3:** Approximately **2° 51' 40"** (full cone).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and provides internal choice. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. The model solution below is **examiner-key aligned**.

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** *With the aid of a labelled sketch, explain the construction and working of a Lathe Tailstock. Discuss the role of the Morse taper. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the step-by-step procedure to assemble the Lathe Tailstock in any standard 3D CAD software. List the mates used and justify the DOF analysis. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction:** The lathe tailstock consists of the following major parts (Fig. 4.1 below):

1. **Base / Body** — a cast iron block fitted on the inner bed ways of the lathe via a V-groove. It has a clamping bolt underneath to lock it to the bed.
2. **Saddle** — sliding block that allows axial positioning along the bed.
3. **Barrel** — a cylindrical housing bored into the upper portion of the body; provides a precision guide for the quill.
4. **Quill** — a sliding spindle with a Morse taper socket at the inboard end and a threaded portion at the outboard end for the leadscrew nut.
5. **Dead Center** — a 60° conical point with an MT3 Morse taper shank that seats in the quill socket.
6. **Handwheel** — mounted on the leadscrew at the outboard end of the quill; rotates to advance/retract the quill.
7. **Leadscrew** — a threaded shaft that converts rotary motion of the handwheel into linear motion of the quill.
8. **Clamping Lever / Bolt** — locks the tailstock body to the lathe bed at the chosen position.

**Working:** The operator positions the tailstock along the bed to support the free end of the workpiece. The handwheel is rotated, advancing the quill (along with the dead center) axially until the center tip contacts the centre hole drilled in the workpiece. Further rotation clamps the workpiece between the chuck and the center. Cutting forces generated during turning are reacted by the tailstock center into the bed, preventing deflection and chatter.

**Role of the Morse Taper:**
- Provides a **self-locking, high-precision, repeatable** tool-holding interface.
- Allows rapid tool change (dead center, live center, drill chuck, reamer holder) without fasteners.
- Aligns the tool axis **coaxially** with the spindle axis to within microns.
- Friction-locks under axial thrust due to its small cone angle ($\approx 1.43°$ half-angle).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step-by-Step Assembly Procedure:**

**Step 1:** Open the assembly file in the CAD environment. Insert the **Base** part as the first component. It becomes the **fixed anchor** — no mates required.

**Step 2:** Insert the **Saddle**. Apply the following mates referencing the base:
- **Coincident** (planar): Saddle top face ↔ Base bottom face.
- **Coincident** (planar): Saddle side faces ↔ Base side faces (2 mates, perpendicular to each other).
- The third axis (along the bed length) is intentionally **left free**, allowing the saddle to slide.

**Step 3:** Insert the **Quill**. Apply:
- **Concentric**: Quill outer cylinder ↔ Base barrel bore.
- **Coincident** (planar): Quill outboard face ↔ Base barrel outboard face (sets initial position).

**Step 4:** Insert the **Dead Center**. Apply:
- **Concentric**: Center taper shank ↔ Quill internal taper socket.
- **Coincident**: Center large-end face ↔ Quill socket shoulder.

**Step 5:** Insert the **Leadscrew**. Apply:
- **Concentric**: Leadscrew shank ↔ Quill rear bore.
- **Coincident**: Leadscrew shoulder ↔ Quill inner face.

**Step 6:** Insert the **Handwheel**. Apply:
- **Concentric**: Handwheel bore ↔ Leadscrew shank.
- **Coincident**: Handwheel rear face ↔ Leadscrew shoulder face.

**Step 7:** Insert the **Clamping Bolt**. Apply:
- **Concentric**: Bolt shank ↔ Base clamping hole.
- **Coincident**: Bolt head underside ↔ Base top surface.

**DOF Justification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF_{gross} &= 6 \times 7 = 42 \text{ DOFs (unconstrained)} \\
\text{Total mates applied} &= 13 \text{ mates} \\
\text{Average DOF removed per mate} &= 3 \text{ (mix of coincident + concentric)} \\
\sum c_i &= 13 \times 3 = 39 \\
DOF_{net} &= 42 - 39 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

The remaining 3 DOFs are:
- **Saddle translation** along bed (Z-axis) — set-up motion.
- **Quill translation** along Z-axis — clamping motion.
- **Handwheel + Leadscrew rotation** about Z-axis — drive input.

This matches the intended kinematic behaviour and confirms the assembly is **isostatically constrained**.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Explain the Degrees of Freedom (DOF) concept in 3D assembly modelling. Compute the net DOF for a Lathe Tailstock assembly with 7 parts. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *With neat sketches, describe the assembly procedure for the quill-dead center sub-assembly. Why is the Morse taper a better fit than a parallel cylindrical fit for the dead center? (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Degrees of Freedom (DOF):**
DOF is the number of independent ways a rigid body can move in 3D space without violating its constraints. In 3D, a single unconstrained rigid body has **6 DOFs**: 3 translations ($T_x, T_y, T_z$) and 3 rotations ($R_x, R_y, R_z$).

In an assembly of $n$ parts, the gross DOF is $6n$. Each geometric mate removes a certain number of DOFs:
- **Coincident (3 planes):** Removes 3 DOFs (2 translations + 1 rotation).
- **Concentric:** Removes 3 DOFs (2 translations + 1 rotation about the perpendicular axes).
- **Coincident (1 plane):** Removes 1 DOF (1 translation along the plane normal).
- **Distance / Angle:** Sets a controlled offset or angle.

**Net DOF for Lathe Tailstock:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of parts } n &= 7 \\
DOF_{gross} &= 6 \times 7 = 42 \\
\text{Total mates} &= 13 \\
\text{Assumed average removal} &= 3 \text{ per mate} \\
\sum c_i &= 39 \\
DOF_{net} &= 42 - 39 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The net DOF is **3**, which corresponds exactly to:
1. **Saddle** translating along Z.
2. **Quill** translating along Z.
3. **Handwheel–Leadscrew** rotating about Z.

This proves the assembly is **correctly constrained** (isostatic).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Quill-Dead Center Sub-Assembly Procedure:**

1. **Insert the Quill** into the assembly (already concentric with the barrel bore).
2. **Insert the Dead Center** as a separate part.
3. Apply **Concentric** mate between the dead center's taper shank and the quill's internal taper socket.
4. Apply **Coincident** mate between the dead center's large-end face and the quill's socket shoulder.
5. **Verify** by attempting to drag the dead center — it should not translate or rotate.

**Why Morse Taper is Superior to Parallel Cylindrical Fit:**

| Criterion | Parallel Cylindrical Fit | Morse Taper |
|---|---|---|
| Self-locking under axial load | No — requires external clamp | Yes — friction locks automatically |
| Concentricity | Depends on tolerance stack-up | Self-centring due to wedge geometry |
| Tool change time | Slow — requires bolt loosening | Fast — one-tap insertion/removal |
| Axial location | Requires shoulder + snap ring | Built into the taper geometry |
| Rigidity | Moderate (clearance or transition fit) | High (line contact + friction lock) |
| Standardization | Non-standard | Universal (MT1 to MT6) |

**Quantitative Justification:**

For a parallel cylindrical fit under axial load $F_a$, the holding force is purely frictional:

$$
F_{hold} = F_a \cdot \mu
$$

For a Morse taper of half-angle $\alpha$:

$$
F_{hold} = \dfrac{F_a \cdot \mu}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}
$$

where $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$. Since $\alpha \approx 1.43°$ and $\phi \approx 6.84°$ ($\mu = 0.12$):

$$
F_{hold} = \dfrac{F_a \times 0.12}{\tan(8.27°)} \approx \dfrac{F_a \times 0.12}{0.145} \approx 0.83 \cdot F_a
$$

Hence the taper holds **83% of the applied axial load** purely by friction — and the holding force **increases** as the load increases (since $\alpha < \phi$). This is impossible with a parallel fit.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks (KTU 2024 valuation patterns):**
>
> 1. **Over-Constraining the Saddle** — Adding a third coincident mate along the bed direction removes the kinematic DOF and makes the assembly non-functional. **Penalty: –2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Skipping the BOM and Exploded View** — These are **mandatory deliverables** under the KTU 2024 lab rubric. Submitting only the assembled model loses **3 marks** out of 14.
>
> 3. **Wrong Morse Taper Angle** — Using the full cone angle (2.86°) instead of the half-angle (1.43°) in the self-locking calculation. **Penalty: –2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Clamping Bolt Mate** — Most students assemble only the obvious motion parts (quill, handwheel) and forget the clamping lever. **Penalty: –1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Ignoring Clearance Fits** — Modelling the quill and barrel bore at the same diameter creates an **interference fit** (zero clearance). Real assemblies use H7/g6 (clearance $= 0.1$ to $0.3$ mm). The model will appear "stuck" during motion simulation. **Penalty: –2 marks.**
>
> 6. **Not Annotating the BOM Balloons** — Item numbers must be in ascending order along the explosion direction. Random numbering loses **1 mark**.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this section for last-minute revision before the lab exam or viva.**

- ✅ **Tailstock = movable auxiliary support** at the free end of a lathe workpiece.
- ✅ **8 core parts:** Base, Saddle, Barrel, Quill, Dead Center, Handwheel, Leadscrew, Clamping Bolt.
- ✅ **Two independent motions:** (1) whole-body slide along bed; (2) quill slide in barrel.
- ✅ **Morse Taper MT3** included angle $\approx 2° 51' 40''$ (half-angle $\approx 1.43°$); self-locking because $\tan(\alpha) < \mu$.
- ✅ **Dead Center point angle = 60°** (general turning); 75° for face work.
- ✅ **DOF budget:** $DOF_{net} = 6n - \sum c_i = 6(7) - 39 = 3$ for a fully constrained 7-part assembly.
- ✅ **Mate types to know:** Coincident (1 or 3 planes), Concentric, Distance, Angle, Tangent.
- ✅ **Assembly order:** Largest immobile part (base) first → progressively smaller mobile parts.
- ✅ **Clearance fit rule:** Quill OD should be $0.1$–$0.3$ mm smaller than barrel bore ID.
- ✅ **Exploded view** is a **mandatory deliverable**; sequence must be in line with assembly hierarchy.
- ✅ **BOM (Bill of Materials)** must list Item No., Part Name, Material, Quantity.
- ✅ **Never model as a single block** — multi-part assembly is the essence of this experiment.
- ✅ **Viva question frequency (high):** "Why Morse taper is self-locking?" → derive $\tan \alpha < \mu$.
- ✅ **Viva question frequency (medium):** "What is the difference between dead center and live center?" → dead = stationary, live = rotating (bearings inside).
- ✅ **Viva question frequency (medium):** "What is the mechanical advantage of the handwheel?" → $MA = D_{hw}/p$.
- ✅ **Viva question frequency (low):** "What is the offset method for taper turning?" → Shift tailstock by $x$ mm laterally to generate a small taper on long workpieces.
